metadata.full_name: Accounting, M.S.

details.description: The Master of Science in Accounting is a degree program designed to prepare students for professional careers in accounting. It is designed specifically to enable students whose undergraduate majors are business related (including Economics, Finance, Marketing and Management) to meet the New Jersey State Board of Accountancy eligibility requirements for taking the Uniform CPA examination in New Jersey. The current course work required to sit for the CPA exam is 150 credits. They may be at either the graduate or undergraduate level. The distribution of courses is as follows: 96 credits in any subject, 30 credits in accounting and 24 credits in business related subjects. Students whose undergraduate major is not business-related may need to take up to six additional courses in non-accounting business subjects.

ACCT 3210.description: This course involves the role of accounting as an information system and introduced the balance sheet accounts as economic resources.  The course analyzes various balance sheet and income statement accounts and includes preparation of financial statements for internal as well as external reporting.

ACCT 3220.description: This course is a continuation of ACCT 3210 Intermediate Accounting I and will involve an in-depth study of accounting for the financing activities of a business that includes long-term liabilities and stockholders' equity. The course also reviews the accounting for investments. A major focus of this course is to improve student's analytical and communication skills in an accounting environment through projects and case studies.

ACCT 5120.description: This course covers a broad spectrum of advanced accounting topics, including business combinations and consolidations, intercompany accounting, multinational enterprises, foreign currency transactions and translation, partnerships including formulation and liquidation, government and not-for-profit entities, and SEC reporting. Current FASB and GASB pronouncements are included with special emphasis to assist in preparations for the CPA examination. Ethical considerations in business are a primary theme.

ACCT 5140.description: Managerial and Cost Accounting introduces students to powerful tools and techniques used by accountants to generate reliable and relevant information for all levels of management to perform their functions of planning, controlling and decision making. In this course students will learn how an effective cost accounting system provides information that can impact operational and financial performances of an entity. Students will also learn how managerial and cost accounting information are developed and used for various purposes in different types of business entities. This course will place emphasis on managerial accounting systems that play a proactive role in planning, organizing, controlling and costs reduction.

ACCT 5160.description: This course is a continuation of the study of the federal income tax structure, use of tax services, and the concept of taxable income as it relates to corporations, partnerships and S corporations. The course will also focus on performing tax research both utilizing hard copy tax reference materials and interactive research media. The course will culminate with the preparation of two comprehensive tax returns: one for a corporation and one for a partnership. Corporate organizations, reorganizations, acquisition and liquidations are also covered.

ACCT 5161.description: This course will introduce the China Tax system to Master of Accounting students and provide an understanding of the major principles and technical areas of individual and enterprise taxation in mainland China. The course will cover the basic taxes, such as the individual and enterprise income tax, resident and non-resident income taxes, value added tax, consumption tax, customs duties and the land appreciation tax on the disposal of property.

ACCT 5190.description: The basic legal principles that apply to the areas of formation, governance and regulation of business entities. Consideration of property rights both real and personal. Development of an understanding of professional ethical regulations and legal liability affecting the accounting practitioner.

ACCT 5230.description: Auditing is fundamental business activity within the Accounting Profession and is tested in the AUD section of the CPA exam. Examination processes, evidence gathering, sampling, and reporting are connected to the tools used by Certified Public Accountants (CPAs) as guided by published auditing standards. CPAs utilize information systems to complete audit examination activities and this course evaluates the audit process, the judgment needed to evaluate results, and the reporting options available based on the audit findings.

ACCT 5260.description: This course focuses on tax planning for individuals. Emphasis will be on pension and retirement issues, trusts and estate plans. Students will identify relevant pension and retirement issues and the tax implications of various courses of action; prepare tax returns; prepare presentations on numerous topics throughout the semester, all while using traditional and computerized tax research methods to examine the various aspects of current tax practice.

ACCT 5251.description: A cross-topic course with an overarching global focus on current issues facing the accounting profession. Themes and topics selected by the instructor.

ACCT 5990.description: This course focuses on a comprehensive understanding of the competencies required of today's accounting professional.  A case-study approach is used to demonstrate how accounting and other business disciplines are integrated-enabling the accounting professional to fulfill the role of public accountant, consultant, strategic business partner, and financial advisor.  Critical thinking and communication skills (both oral and written) are an integral part of this course.

metadata.full_name: Advanced Certificate in Bilingual Speech Language Pathology
metadata.coordinator: Dr. Mahchid Namazi, Program Coordinator

details.description: The Post-Master's Certification option is designed to prepare speech-language pathologists to work with bilingual children and adolescents. This is a 12-credit program can be completed in two semesters.

CDD 5300.description: An overview of cultural diversity as it impacts speech-language pathology, both practical and theoretical, equipping students with the relevant skills to function as competent professionals with culturally diverse populations.

CDD 5304.description: An overview of current research on bilingualism focusing on language separation, bilingual lexical and grammatical development, code-switching, and the effects of early bilingualism on general cognition.

CDD 5305.description: Learn the characteristics of and apply the research in disorders of speech-language and communication in bilingual children including Autism, Down syndrome, phonological disorder and language disorder.

CDD 5303.description: Learn and implement best practice in the culturally and linguistically fair assessment and intervention of bilingual children with speech, language, or communication disorders.

metadata.full_name: Applied Behavior Analysis: Autism and Developmental Disabilities, Post-Master's Certification
metadata.coordinator:
Dr. Randi A. Sarokoff, BCBA-D
Office: Hennings Hall, Room 310
Phone: 908-737-3849
Email: rsarokof@kean.edu

details.description: The Post-Master’s Certificate Option: Applied Behavior Analysis: Autism and Developmental Disabilities is an option for those who already hold a Master’s Degree and would like to pursue becoming a Board Certified Behavior Analyst (BCBA®). This certificate program meets the 315-hour coursework requirement Applicants will need to meet additional requirements before they can be deemed eligible to take the examination. This Post-Master’s Certificate Program is rooted in the science of applied behavior analysis (ABA). Kean University, you will develop an evidenced-based approach to teaching students with Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) and other Developmental Disabilities.

SPED 5100.description: Course content includes, but is not limited to, the basic concepts and principles of operant and respondent conditioning. *As indicated in the course content section, this course is consistent with BACB Sixth Edition Task List and emphasizes Content Areas, B - Concepts and Principles

SPED 5101.description: Course content includes, but is not limited to, the selection and implementation of behavior assessment techniques and approaches from applied behavior analysis (ABA) and is in alignment with the BACB sixth edition.

SPED 5102.description: Course content includes, but is not limited to, behavior assessment, selection and implementation of behavior intervention techniques, and generalization and maintenance of behavior change, from the applied behavior analysis research in alignment with the BACB sixth edition course contents.

SPED 5103.description: Course content includes, but is not limited to, performance analysis and management, supervision, staff training, behavioral systems analysis, organizational culture, and leadership aligned with the BACB 6th ed. Task List  *As indicated in the course content section, this course is consistent with BACB Sixth Edition Task List and emphasizes Content Areas, G-Behavior-Change Procedures and I-Personnel Supervision and Management.

SPED 5200.description: Course content includes, but is not limited to, the underlying theoretical and philosophical foundations of behavior analysis. *This course is for students sitting for the BACB certification exam after January 2027.

SPED 7900.description: Course includes, but is not limited to, the study of single-subject research design in applied behavior analysis. Evaluation of behavior interventions using systematic manipulation of independent variables to analyze treatment outcomes. Interpreting graph data, and examining internal, social and external validities.  *Course content for those taking the BACB 5th Edition exam , 6th edition exam, and those taking the BCBA exam after 2027. *As indicated in the course content section, this course is consistent with BACB Fifth Edition and Sixth Edition emphasizes Content Area C-Measurement, Data Display, and Interpretation and D- Experimental Design.

SPED 7903.description: Students will gain an in-depth understanding of ethical and professional standards of conduct for behavior analysts based on the BACB Professional and Ethical Compliance Code for Behavior Analysts.*  *This course meets the BACB Professional and Ethical Compliance Code for Behavior Analysts (for those taking the BACB 5th Edition exam, 6th edition exam, and those taking the BCBA exam after 2027.) *As indicated in the course content section, this course is consistent with BACB Fifth Edition and emphasizes Content Area E-Ethics; and with BACB Sixth Edition and Emphasizes Content Area E-Ethical and Professional Issues.

metadata.full_name: Architecture 3 Year Option, M.Arch
metadata.coordinator: Students with an undergraduate degree other than Architecture will need a transfer credit evaluation by the Architecture Graduate Program Coordinator. Additional coursework may be required.

details.description: The Kean University professional program in architecture emphasizes knowledge of humanist values as they impact contemporary design.  It promotes the acquisition of comprehensive design skills that are made manifest through both cognition and representation.  The program addresses the increasing level of professional design services required in the 21st century, and the increasing globalization of architectural design.  It utilizes its two locations in major urban areas as resources for access to professional employment and training.

ARCH 2303.description: The integration of analog and digital representational skills is addressed. The appropriate selection of representational tools based on design problem components is paramount.

ARCH 3401.description: The fundamentals of structures – equilibrium, strengths of materials, statics and mechanics – are introduced looking at two-dimensional structural elements, with reference to local project case studies. These topics are examined through lectures, physical demonstrations, in-class worked examples, and assignment problem sets. Evaluation is predominantly based on a mid-term and final examination.

ARCH 4100.description: This is an introduction to the 2D & 3D architectural design concepts of solid/void, hierarchy, threshold, arrangement, patterns/geometries, etc. and how basic elements are defined, articulated and related to one another.

ARCH 4101.description: This is an introduction to the architectural concepts of site, space, place making, program and how building elements are formed and assembled on a site. Historical precedents and examples are studied for their application to contemporary building methods at differing scales.

ARCH 4102.description: The formation and assembly of building elements in the urban context are addressed in detail. The introduction of skills to develop the ability to analyze an urban site and organize programming, structure and materials on the site are covered in this Studio.

ARCH 4201.description: An Architectural History survey course covering the major developments of architectural significance, through the study of precedent and the understanding of the incremental development of the architectural thematic or idea. This course is an introduction to the basic ideas and language of architecture.

ARCH 4208.description: A research-based seminar into a defined period of architectural history, culminating in a major writing exercise.

ARCH 4402.description: The principles of structural systems - loading, load paths, stability systems and evaluation criteria - are explored considering different two- and three-dimensional structural typologies, with reference to local project case studies. These topics are examined through lectures, physical demonstrations, in-class worked examples, and assignment problem sets. Course evaluation is predominantly based on a semester-long project.

ARCH 4403.description: The application of the systems for building operation are addressed in detail. These include heat and air systems, water, lighting and electricity, communications, vertical movement, and security. The selection of appropriate systems based on building form, structure, and precedent are examined carefully.

ARCH 4601.description: Formal principles and analysis of formal relationships are investigated in depth. How building objects are composed, and their relationships to each other, are key elements in composing the built environment. Strategies for meaningful formal arrangements of the structures and places around us developed in detail and applied to studio projects.

ARCH 5109.description: The design of individual or multiple structures in a manner that addresses an increasingly complex program is addressed. Themes include a refined assessment of site conditions, functional program, structural and enclosure systems, ecological and environmental impact, and the historical and cultural precedents appropriate for the topic.

ARCH 5110.description: The design of individual or multiple structures in a manner that addresses an increasingly complex program is addressed. Particular emphasis is given to local conditions, including historical, cultural and geographical circumstances that affect buildings and urbanism.

ARCH 6111.description: Development of a complete building design at all stages of architectural development is the core of the comprehensive studio. Site assessment, zoning, programming, conceptual design, design development, and representation are all addressed.

ARCH 6112.description: Individual student projects based on a semester-long research seminar that precedes the studio are a capstone of the professional academic program.

ARCH 5502.description: The global practice of architecture is examined, with emphasis on both non-traditional as well as alternative methods of carrying out design and building.

ARCH 5209.description: A research-based seminar into a defined period of architectural history, culminating in a major writing exercise.

ARCH 5210.description: A culmination of the history/theory sequence and demonstration of research skills in composing a thesis program that is grounded in terms situating a design problem with respect to architectural history, and contemporary culture.

ARCH 5404.description: The application of the systems for building operation are addressed in detail. These include heat and air systems, water, lighting and electricity, communications, vertical movement, and security. The selection of appropriate systems based on building form, structure, and precedent are examined carefully.

ARCH 6405.description: The application of the systems for building operation and their thorough integration are addressed in detail. The selection of appropriate systems based on building form, structure, and precedent are examined carefully. Integration into larger urban systems is addressed as well.

ARCH 5501.description: The elements of establishing and maintaining a professional design practice are studied in detail. The relationships with building and code officials, property developers and managers, consultants, and contractors are considered. Case studies for local models are utilized for instruction, engaging the constituents of a broad professional world to present the elements by which buildings and spaces are conceived and executed in real time.

metadata.full_name: Architecture, M.Arch.
metadata.coordinator: Students with an undergraduate degree other than Architecture will need a transfer credit evaluation by the Architecture Graduate Program Coordinator. Additional coursework may be required.

details.description:
The Kean University professional program in architecture emphasizes knowledge of humanist values as they impact contemporary design.  It promotes the acquisition of comprehensive design skills that are made manifest through both cognition and representation.  The program addresses the increasing level of professional design services required in the 21st century, and the increasing globalization of architectural design.  It utilizes its two locations in major urban areas as resources for access to professional employment and training.

The program follows the 4+2 model for a professional degree in Architecture: a 4-year Bachelor of Arts degree in Architectural Studies, followed by a 2-year professional Master of Architecture degree.  It consists of 193-195 total credit hours and allows 18 credit hours for minors or concentrations within the discipline.  Fifty-four credit hours are at the graduate level. Kean’s requirement for 35 credit hours of general studies courses as a component of the baccalaureate degree is acceptable to NAAB provided the degree structure remains the 4+2 model.

metadata.full_name: Athletic Training, M.S.

details.description: The Master of Science Athletic Training program at Kean University will prepare students to become Athletic Trainers who collaborate with physicians and other healthcare providers to deliver quality healthcare in various employment settings, such as high schools, colleges, professional sports, industrial, military and performing arts.

AT 5000.description: Through lecture and laboratory exercises, the student will obtain both didactic information concerning the analytical and practical application of athletic training skills to recognize and manage musculoskeletal injuries of the physically active individuals.

AT 5001.description: This course will focus on the aspects of professional management and administrative issues in athletic training, including program management, budgeting, leadership development, human resources, insurance, risk management, ethics, development of policies and procedures, and facility design.

AT 5002.description: Examines clinical principles of human movement including the synergistic relationship of the muscular system and the myofascial meridians. Emphasis will be placed on normal and faulty pathological posture and movement.

AT 5003.description: This course focuses on the key concepts of evaluation, diagnosis, and management of the lower-extremity physically active-related injuries.

AT 5004.description: This course will help students understand principles used to develop, implement, and evaluate treatment care plans using therapeutic interventions, modalities, and rehabilitation with emphasis placed on developing patient care philosophy using evident-based principles.

AT 5005.description: This course promotes an understanding of the psychological and social aspects of sports, injury, and rehabilitation.

AT 5006.description: This course focuses on the key concepts of evaluation, diagnosis, and management of the upper-extremity physically active-related injuries. Students will be able to use an evidence-based approach to identify, explain, and assess pathologies of musculoskeletal injuries in the upper extremity and apply these concepts in real-life scenarios.

AT 5007.description: This course is a continuation of Therapeutic Intervention I that develop strategies for implementing treatment care plans using therapeutic interventions, modalities, and rehabilitation with emphasis placed on developing patient care philosophy using evidence-based principles.

AT 5008.description: This course promotes an understanding of sports nutrition with an emphasis on basic nutritional concepts, energy expenditure during resistance and endurance training, training diets, timing and consumption of pre-and-post competition meals, use of supplements and ergogenic aids, and special needs of various athletic groups.

AT 5009.description: This course will include injury prevention and risk management techniques, along with identification and management of general medical concerns of the physically active.

AT 5010.description: This course focuses on the key concepts of evaluation, diagnosis, and management of the spinal physically active-related injuries. Students will be able to use an evidence-based approach to identify, explain, and assess pathologies of musculoskeletal injuries in the upper extremity and apply these concepts in real-life scenarios.

AT 5011.description: This course will introduce the fundamentals of research design and interpretation, including basic statistical analysis.

AT 5012.description: The student will learn how to properly apply and remove orthopaedic fracture casts and splints using current research regarding joint immobilization, safety techniques, indications, contraindications, as well as patient education on how to care for the cast and splint.

AT 5013.description: This course will focus on the key concepts of evaluation, diagnosis, and management of general medical conditions commonly seen in the physically active.

AT 5014.description: This course focuses on the professional development of the athletic trainer through professional conduct, continuing education requirements, cultural competence, and interaction with other healthcare professions.

AT 5015.description: This course begins the athletic training student's education on the concepts of pharmacotherapeutics, establishing a knowledge base that applies to patient care education.

AT 5016.description: This course serves to prepare students for the Board of Certification examination and becoming licensed to practice athletic training.

AT 5017.description: This course accompanies the first of four clinical experiences in athletic training with a preceptor in order to further develop and enhance the practical skills and knowledge necessary for clinical practice.

AT 5018.description: This course accompanies the second of four clinical experiences in athletic training with a preceptor in order to further develop and enhance the practical skills and knowledge necessary for clinical practice.

AT 5019.description: This course accompanies the third of four clinical experiences in athletic training with a preceptor in order to further develop and enhance the practical skills and knowledge necessary for clinical practice. Students will engage in a clinical experience at a selected affiliated clinical experience site and demonstrate the ability to apply injury preventive, evaluation, and management skills.

AT 5020.description: This course accompanies the fourth of four clinical experiences providing a full immersive clinical experience with a preceptor in order to further develop and enhance the practical skills and knowledge necessary for clinical practice.

metadata.full_name: Biotechnology Science, M.S.

details.description: The Master of Science program in Biotechnology Science is a 34 credit interdisciplinary program designed to prepare students for research in the expanding field of biotechnology through course work and laboratory experience. Many students attend the program part-time while working in industry, as courses are scheduled in the evenings. Graduates become proficient in state-of-the-art technologies and are prepared to seek employment/advancement in biotech/pharma or to continue their study toward the Ph.D. degree in other graduate programs in related fields.

STME 5625.description: Medicinal chemistry focuses on how new medications are created and tested. The course will also provide an overview of the drug development process from research bench to patient ingestion. This will include the investigation of small molecule interactions with relevant biological targets, efficacy, absorption, metabolism, toxicity pharmacokinetics and patents.

STME 5320.description: Applied human immunology focuses on how the  human immune system works, what happens when the immune system goes awry, and how different components of the immune system can be harnessed for basic and clinical research. This course will explore many of the molecular and cellular players involved in  immunity and the importance of their interactions. This course will also discuss some of the fundamental experiments in immunology and practical applications of immunological research in current biotechnology industry.

STME 5140.description: Graduate level laboratory-intensive course. Biological approach to the study of RNA proteins, and cells with emphasis on current physiological principles relating structure to function, gene expression, cell communication and intracellular trafficking. Lab exercises include cellular biotechniques to purify and characterize mRNA, proteins and assess physiological activity in living cells. Students will be required to complete a guided research project.

CHEM 4190.description: Medicinal Chemistry focuses on the relationships between the structures of organic molecules and their functions in biological systems. The course will provide an overview of the drug development process from research bench to patient ingestion. This will include the investigation of small molecule interaction with relevant biological targets, efficacy, absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion and toxicity profiling of any drug candidate with complete pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics study. The course will emphasize major drug design techniques in the scope of medicinal chemistry so that students can have hands-on training of industrial standards. (3 hr. lecture)

STME 5120.description: An introduction to bioinformatics, including the general concepts of informatics, data, objectives of data analysis, public bioinformatics databases, methods of bioinformatics data analysis, computational tools, and future directions. Computer laboratory exercises are included.

STME 5170.description: The second semester in a sequence of graduate level laboratory courses presenting experimental design, methods and techniques.  Quantitative and qualitative laboratory approaches to the study of cell signaling pathways and using loss of function genetic alteration techniques to measure effects on aspects of cell physiology.  Advanced cell culture techniques including 3D spheroid and organoid culture and stem cell propagation will be explored.  Additional emphasis on multiplex approaches and cell population analyses.  Students will be required to complete a guided research project.

STME 5260.description: An advanced biotechnology course designed for second year graduate biotechnology students that will provide an understanding of the complex interactions within biological systems. Students will gain an appreciation for the methodology employed to investigate system-wide interrogation of molecular and cellular processes. Emphasis will be on genomic, transcriptomic, and proteomic approaches and description of the state of the art techniques used in these fields. Computational tools used to manage the volume of data generated using high-throughput techniques will also be subject of discussion.

STME 5310.description: An examination of the molecular mechanisms by which cancer cells arise, develop into tumors, and metastasize. Topic include: oncogenes, tumor suppressor genes, signal transduction, cell cycle control, DNA methylation, metastasis, diagnosis and therapy.

STME 5600.description: This course provides a comprehensive overview for graduate level students, of the theoretical principles of Pharmacology, including pharmacokinetics, pharmacodynamics, dosing and formulation, safety and toxicity. The course will delve into the pharmacologic mechanisms of action of biologically active molecules, such as drugs, chemical mediators, hormones and toxins, the effects of these molecules on organ systems and the therapeutic uses of such molecules, routes of drug administration and metabolism, adverse reactions, contraindications, drug-drug interactions and mechanisms of drug addiction.

STME 5615.description: The purpose of the course is to provide a thorough and detailed understanding of the principles of modern analytical techniques and their applications to solve analytical challenges in chemical, pharmaceutical, and biological sciences.

STME 5650.description: This course will introduce second year graduate students to the principles and methodologies of translational research through lectures, scientific readings, case study analyses and class discussions. Topics include mechanistic biology, linking clinical and basic research through evidence-based research, and bioethical considerations in personalized medicine.

STME 5810.description: Students will develop a basic understanding of modem liquid chromatography (LC)-mass spectrometry (MS) techniques and its applications to solve analytical challenges in chemical, pharmaceutical and biological sciences.

STME 5830.description: This course will introduce 4th year undergraduates and 1st or 2nd year graduate students to the principles and methodologies used in pharmaceutical drug development through lectures, scientific readings, case study analyses and class discussions. The course covers the range of drug development activities from target product profile, pharmacokinetics, pre-clinical and clinical safety, as well as the objectives of each phase of the drug development process encountered in the pharma, biotech and agriculture sectors as applied to New Chemical Entities (NCEs) and Novel Biologic Agents (NBAs). Regulatory requirements and marketing considerations will also be discussed with regard to clinical outcomes in Phases III and IV as well as post launch surveillance for toxicity. This course provides an introduction to the necessary training, knowledge, logic and steps for successful approval of a drug, including the ethical, conflict of interest and intellectual property issues involved in the process.

CPS 5920.description: Enterprise database management systems; database design; database implementation ; storage and indexing; transaction processing concepts; data warehousing, data mining and online analytical processing (OLAP); emerging database application.

CPS 5440.description: Advanced algorithm analysis techniques. Algorithms involving advanced data structures, sorting, trees, and graphs. NP-Completeness. Randomized Algorithms. Approximation Algorithms.

CPS 5965.description: Fundamental concepts of high performance computing; hardware architecture; software libraries; design,analysis, implementation and performance evaluation of parallel and distributed algorithms.

GMBA 5680.description: The course provides the students with a comprehensive understanding of the responsibilities of managers and leaders in private and public global organizations. In particular, competencies for leaders and managerial and leadership skills that are required to be an effective manager in the era of globalization will be explored.  Formerly: MGS 5680.

MKT 5400.description: This course provides an in-depth focus on strategic marketing management.  The fundamental principles of management (planning, organization, implementation, and evaluation) are applied to the total marketing effort of an organization.  Strategies are analyzed related to the market concept, social responsibility and ethics, product development, promotional strategy, physical distribution and pricing decisions.  Opportunities and challenges related to marketing management are studied including economic and competitive conditions, technology, social-cultural trends, political\legal issues, physical environmental issues, competition, and globalization.

MGS 5010.description: This course examines how firms use strategic management concepts for competitive advantage in local and global markets. Students adopt a top management team perspective and gain experience in strategy analysis and implementation relying primarily on case analyses. Topics include analysis of the environment (competitive, institutional, and technological), firms' resources and value chains, corporate diversification, strategic alliances, and organizational structure and governance.

STME 5010.description: Molecular cell biology is rapidly advancing the study of biotechnology, particularly in the fields of biomedical, pharmaceutical, and agricultural sciences. Understanding the chemistry of DNA, RNA, and proteins gives biotechnologists the ability to manipulate these macromolecules to more fully understand cellular functions, treat human diseases and engineer more viable crops and livestock. This course will provide students with a broad understanding of the foundations of molecular biology and its applications.

STME 5300.description: An advanced molecular cell biology course designed for first year/second semester graduate biotechnology students that will detail the study of cells in their social context. Topics include cellular signal transduction pathways and their control over cellular processes relevant to diseases including the apoptosis, cell growth and division, cell junctions, the extracellular matrix, stem cells, fundamentals of cellular development, cancer an immunity. Additional focus on strategies to manipulate pathway activity to alter disease states.

STME 5103.description: This writing intensive course teaches the skills and search tools required for effective scientific writing and oral presentations.  The class will include interactive lectures & classroom discussions, critiques of current scientific literature, guest lectures on special topics, written papers and class presentations.

STME 5510.description: Applied Biostatistics for Biotechnology researchers to include the use of standard deviation, standard error, normal distribution, significance tests and intervals, analysis of variance, linear and multiple regression, chi squared analysis, multiple comparison and data fitting procedures.

STME 5020.description: Examination of ethical issues arising from developments, technologies, and research practices in Biotechnology and related scientific and medical fields. Consideration given to the goals possibility of unforseen outcomes, and the implication of new developments and technologies.

STME 5411.description: Students will conduct original research in biotechnology laboratory facility outfitted for biological, chemical or bioinformatics investigations, under the advisement of a faculty member.  Each project will be approved by the Biotechnology Steering Committee, consisting of the Program Coordinator, the Dean or Associate Dean and relevant faculty, prior to the onset of the internship.

STME 5413.description: Students will conduct original research in biotechnology laboratory facility outfitted for biological, chemical or bioinformatic investigations, under the advisement of a faculty member.  Each project will be approved by the Biotechnology Steering Committee, consisting of the Program Coordinator, the Dean or Associate Dean and relevant faculty, prior to the onset of the internship.

STME 5555.description: Final course in the 3-course series allowing completion of work on the formal thesis for the MS Biotechnology Science Program. Data collection is ended and data analysis completed. Results and Discussion sections of the manuscript are written. Complete manuscript is reviewed, edited and accepted by the thesis committee. Completed manuscript is submitted to the thesis committee (consisiting of two faculty and the program coordinator). Student also gives an oral thesis defense in a formal seminar open to the public.

metadata.full_name: Business Administration, M.B.A.: Executive Global Option

details.description: Kean’s Executive MBA in Global is a specialized MBA degree program that prepares managers to succeed in a global business environment. It offers a wide and deep selection of global-focused courses, experiences, and training in global business issues. The program’s Executive Option is designed for executives and business professionals who already have substantial business experience and knowledge.

ACCT 5010.description: This course will focus on accounting as an information system that benefits users such as investors, creditors, managers, governmental regulators and other interested parties. This course includes financial accounting with an emphasis on the conceptual framework of accounting, the bookkeeping process of accounting, and financial statements presentation and analysis. The managerial segment focuses on managerial accounting tools used for decision making, the foundation of managerial accounting, ethical issues, operations planning and control and performance evaluation.

ECON 5020.description: Managerial Economics is an application of microeconomic theory to decision making in the firm. Topics include: demand, production and cost estimation, price and output determination in pure competition, monopolistic competition, oligopoly behavior, and government regulation. Formerly: MGS 5020.

FIN 5300.description: Business Finance is the entry-level graduate finance course.  The course offers introduction to modern financial theory and practice in the corporate finance decision-making process, In particular, the course will explore topics such as fundamental objectives of financial management, valuation of corporate securities, capital budgeting analysis, trade-off between risk and return, capital structure decisions, and corporate dividend policies, etc.

MGS 5010.description: This course examines how firms use strategic management concepts for competitive advantage in local and global markets. Students adopt a top management team perspective and gain experience in strategy analysis and implementation relying primarily on case analyses. Topics include analysis of the environment (competitive, institutional, and technological), firms' resources and value chains, corporate diversification, strategic alliances, and organizational structure and governance.

MGS 5110.description: Presentation of quantitative techniques and models in order to solve managerial problems. Topics include; probability distributions, decision theory, game theory, forecasting, linear programming, waiting lines and transportation models.

MKT 5400.description: This course provides an in-depth focus on strategic marketing management.  The fundamental principles of management (planning, organization, implementation, and evaluation) are applied to the total marketing effort of an organization.  Strategies are analyzed related to the market concept, social responsibility and ethics, product development, promotional strategy, physical distribution and pricing decisions.  Opportunities and challenges related to marketing management are studied including economic and competitive conditions, technology, social-cultural trends, political\legal issues, physical environmental issues, competition, and globalization.

GMBA 5065.description: The course provides the students with an advance understanding of the global environment of business and its potential effects on marketing strategy in cross-cultural markets. Specifically, students will be given an understanding of world cultures and their impact on product, pricing, distribution and promotion planning.  This course will help students to develop strategic thinking in a global marketing context to develop leadership skills for conducting marketing activities in global markets. Formerly: MKT 5465.

GMBA 5075.description: After reviewing basic concepts of strategic management, this course focuses on the strategic challenges confronting MNC's in the global economy.  We will explore the process, assumptions, and set of tools that international managers use to develop and implement a firm's global strategy. The main objective is to have an enhanced understanding of the most fundamental question in global strategic management: What determines the international success and failure of companies.

GMBA 5660.description: This course identifies and discusses differences and similarities in ethical values and principles found in the practice of organizations conducting business around the globe. Strategic opportunities and challenges emerging from cultural, religious and/or gender differences will be developed to guide ethical decision making in organizations conducting business in the global marketplace. Formerly:  MGS 5660.

GMBA 5680.description: The course provides the students with a comprehensive understanding of the responsibilities of managers and leaders in private and public global organizations. In particular, competencies for leaders and managerial and leadership skills that are required to be an effective manager in the era of globalization will be explored.  Formerly: MGS 5680.

GMBA 5785.description: Contemporary issues and current state of the art research and practice in multi-cultural management in the global environment will be explored.  The main objective is to improve the student CQ (Cultural Intelligence).  The students will be required to choose a specific topic and explore it in depth by leading a discussion in class well as final group project and presentation on cultural preparations in doing business in a specific culture (country).

GMBA 5885.description: This course will introduce the students to the main issues in global information system management, and how managers can use IT on a global basis to achieve strategic business advantage.  The focus will be on the management of information systems and technology in a global context in terms of planning, infrastructure, applications, business processes, and IT operations.

GMBA 5900.description: This course enables students through participation in a realistic global business simulation game to evaluate a firm's global competitive environment, develop strategic alternatives, and make effective decisions at both business and functional levels (i.e. marketing, operations, finance and accounting).

metadata.full_name: Business Administration, M.B.A.: Global Option

details.description: Kean’s MBA in Global Management program is a specialized MBA degree program that prepares managers to succeed in a global business environment. It offers a wide and deep selection of global-focused courses, experiences, and training in global business issues. In addition, it has an extensive global experience requirement that sets it apart from other programs.

ACCT 5010.description: This course will focus on accounting as an information system that benefits users such as investors, creditors, managers, governmental regulators and other interested parties. This course includes financial accounting with an emphasis on the conceptual framework of accounting, the bookkeeping process of accounting, and financial statements presentation and analysis. The managerial segment focuses on managerial accounting tools used for decision making, the foundation of managerial accounting, ethical issues, operations planning and control and performance evaluation.

ECON 5020.description: Managerial Economics is an application of microeconomic theory to decision making in the firm. Topics include: demand, production and cost estimation, price and output determination in pure competition, monopolistic competition, oligopoly behavior, and government regulation. Formerly: MGS 5020.

FIN 5300.description: Business Finance is the entry-level graduate finance course.  The course offers introduction to modern financial theory and practice in the corporate finance decision-making process, In particular, the course will explore topics such as fundamental objectives of financial management, valuation of corporate securities, capital budgeting analysis, trade-off between risk and return, capital structure decisions, and corporate dividend policies, etc.

MGS 5010.description: This course examines how firms use strategic management concepts for competitive advantage in local and global markets. Students adopt a top management team perspective and gain experience in strategy analysis and implementation relying primarily on case analyses. Topics include analysis of the environment (competitive, institutional, and technological), firms' resources and value chains, corporate diversification, strategic alliances, and organizational structure and governance.

MGS 5110.description: Presentation of quantitative techniques and models in order to solve managerial problems. Topics include; probability distributions, decision theory, game theory, forecasting, linear programming, waiting lines and transportation models.

MKT 5400.description: This course provides an in-depth focus on strategic marketing management.  The fundamental principles of management (planning, organization, implementation, and evaluation) are applied to the total marketing effort of an organization.  Strategies are analyzed related to the market concept, social responsibility and ethics, product development, promotional strategy, physical distribution and pricing decisions.  Opportunities and challenges related to marketing management are studied including economic and competitive conditions, technology, social-cultural trends, political\legal issues, physical environmental issues, competition, and globalization.

GMBA 5065.description: The course provides the students with an advance understanding of the global environment of business and its potential effects on marketing strategy in cross-cultural markets. Specifically, students will be given an understanding of world cultures and their impact on product, pricing, distribution and promotion planning.  This course will help students to develop strategic thinking in a global marketing context to develop leadership skills for conducting marketing activities in global markets. Formerly: MKT 5465.

GMBA 5075.description: After reviewing basic concepts of strategic management, this course focuses on the strategic challenges confronting MNC's in the global economy.  We will explore the process, assumptions, and set of tools that international managers use to develop and implement a firm's global strategy. The main objective is to have an enhanced understanding of the most fundamental question in global strategic management: What determines the international success and failure of companies.

GMBA 5660.description: This course identifies and discusses differences and similarities in ethical values and principles found in the practice of organizations conducting business around the globe. Strategic opportunities and challenges emerging from cultural, religious and/or gender differences will be developed to guide ethical decision making in organizations conducting business in the global marketplace. Formerly:  MGS 5660.

GMBA 5680.description: The course provides the students with a comprehensive understanding of the responsibilities of managers and leaders in private and public global organizations. In particular, competencies for leaders and managerial and leadership skills that are required to be an effective manager in the era of globalization will be explored.  Formerly: MGS 5680.

GMBA 5785.description: Contemporary issues and current state of the art research and practice in multi-cultural management in the global environment will be explored.  The main objective is to improve the student CQ (Cultural Intelligence).  The students will be required to choose a specific topic and explore it in depth by leading a discussion in class well as final group project and presentation on cultural preparations in doing business in a specific culture (country).

GMBA 5885.description: This course will introduce the students to the main issues in global information system management, and how managers can use IT on a global basis to achieve strategic business advantage.  The focus will be on the management of information systems and technology in a global context in terms of planning, infrastructure, applications, business processes, and IT operations.

GMBA 5350.description: Multinational financial management is a graduate finance course which has the objective of studying problems and complexities that may arise when trade and investments are made overseas. The course will enable students to understand core areas of international finance such as financing international trade, exchange rate risk, risk exposure management, international capital structure, cost of capital and multinational capital budgeting.

GMBA 5710.description: The course provides students with a real-life business experience and training on global business issues, problems, and opportunities. This is achieved through, first, studying the business opportunities and risk of a specific international region/country. Second, a two week, organized travel learning trip to that region/country. The trip includes relevant lecture, visits in local business and appropriate cultural activities. Completion of 21 credits in the Global MBA program and or permission from the program director and the trip leader.

GMBA 5900.description: This course enables students through participation in a realistic global business simulation game to evaluate a firm's global competitive environment, develop strategic alternatives, and make effective decisions at both business and functional levels (i.e. marketing, operations, finance and accounting).

metadata.full_name: Combined School and Clinical Psychology, Psy.D.

details.description: Kean University’s Psy.D. Program in Combined School and Clinical Psychology prepares general practice, primary care, and health service psychologists who are competent to function in a variety of professional and academic settings and roles. This goal is achieved by intentionally combining and integrating education and training across the two practice areas of school psychology and clinical psychology, providing students with a wide breadth and depth of training in both psychological practice areas. By deeply integrating these two practice areas throughout the curriculum, the combined training program model prepares students to function as clinical or school psychologists in a wide variety of professional and academic positions, thus increasing career flexibility and marketability.

PSYD 7100.description: Critical examination of the multifaceted influences responsible for the diverse outcomes in human development. Emphasis on contemporary views regarding genetics, contextualism, and multi-levels of development. Review of major research issues and practical application of developmental theory.

PSYD 7200.description: This course provides students with basic competencies in the selection, administration, scoring and interpretation of individually administered tests of intelligence and achievement in regards to adults. The Wechsler scales will be highlighted. Issues of theory, research, clinical utility and ethics are addressed. Special attention will be given to issues relating to culturally sensitive assessment methodology.

PSYD 7321.description: This is the first course in the statistics/research methodology progression. The focus will be upon understanding psychological measurement, data analyses, and using a variety of statistical procedures. In learning these procedures, students will also gain proficiency in SPSS. Methods of reporting results in APA style are depicted.

PSYD 7110.description: This first course of a three semester sequence is designed to continue to familiarize students with the role(s) and function(s), similarities and differences of the school and clinical psychologist is a variety of settings, including: schools, clinics, hospitals, private practice settings, post-secondary education settings, and other alternative service delivery systems. The philosophical and empirical base of combined training in school and clinical psychology will be emphasized. Issues that impact the role and function of school and clinical psychologists will also be addressed in this course such as history and foundation of school and clinical psychology, ethics and standards, public policy (including legislation and regulations), ethics and legal issues, professional trends, quality of practice and the organization and operation of schools and mental health settings.

PSYD 7005.description: This course is designed to provide students with an understanding and appreciation of the main research trends and findings with regards to contemporary cognitive psychology and emotion science, with particular relevance to the relationship between these concepts and the practice of school and clinical psychology.

PSYD 7220.description: Personality, Individual Differences, and Learning is a course focused on exploration of historical and contemporary models for understanding human personality. Categorical and dimensional models, sociocultural differences in behavior, and constructs in clinical and social psychology literature (e.g., emotion regulation and psychological flexibility) will be discussed. There will also be a detailed exploration of learning theory and its applicability for the practice of clinical psychology and school psychology with attention to individual differences.

PSYD 7202.description: This course provides students with basic competencies in the selection, administration, scoring and interpretation of individually administered tests of intelligence and achievement in regards to toddlers, infants, and children. The Wechsler scales will be highlighted. Issues of theory, research, clinical utility and ethics are addressed. Special attention will be given to issues relating to culturally sensitive assessment methodology.

PSYD 7421.description: This course offers an exploration of the major diagnostic categories and specific psychological disorders affecting adults, with an emphasis on epidemiological factors, etiology, symptomatology, maintaining factors, pathological processes, and treatment. Additional attention will be given to differential diagnosis and comorbidities among disorders. Students will become proficient in the use of the latest version of the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual (DSM).

PSYD 7111.description: This second course of a three semester sequence is designed to continue to familiarize students with the role(s) and functions (s), similarities and differences of the school and clinical psychologist in a variety of settings, including: schools, clinics, hospitals, private practice settings, post-secondary education settings, and other alternative service delivery systems. The philosophical and empirical base of combined and integrative training in school and clinical psychology will be emphasized. Issues that impact the role and function of school and clinical psychologists will also be addressed in this course such as history and foundation of school and clinical psychology, ethics and standards, public policy (including legislation and regulations), ethics and legal issues, professional trends, quality of practice and the organization and operation of schools and mental health settings.

PSYD 7331.description: The purpose of this course is for students to develop a keen understanding of research design and the skills necessary for the implementation of a research project. In particular, students will become familiar with issues, concepts and procedures in clinical efficacy and effectiveness research, including single-case experimental design. An expanded consideration of the ethics of research, IRB requirements and APA style of data reporting and manuscript writing will be included. Students will also gain experience with manuscript reviewing.

PSYD 7401.description: A laboratory, based-skills course designed to develop foundational interviewing and intervention skills essential to obtaining relevant clinical information, determining appropriate diagnoses, establishing an effective therapeutic relationship, and promoting behavior change.

PSYD 7204.description: This course is designed to help students develop knowledge and skills related to the selection, administration, and interpretation of some representative objective personality and behavioral assessment instruments used in school, clinical, and forensic settings. This course will take a life-span approach to personality and behavior assessment, and address issues of theory, research, clinical utility, cultural competence, and ethics. Students will also learn to integrate data obtained from an assessment battery to write comprehensive psychological assessment reports.

PSYD 7431.description: A first course in psychological treatment designed to provide the student with an understanding of foundational knowledge and skills required for provision of effective evidence-based psychological treatment. Students will be encouraged to think critically about psychological treatment selection and will be exposed to the range of approaches to individual psychological treatment utilized in contemporary clinical practice, with particular attention and emphasis given to behavioral, cognitive-behavioral, and other empirically supported models. Issues of diversity in clinical practice will be considered and infused throughout the course.

PSYD 7112.description: This third course of a three semester sequence is designed to continue to familiarize students with the role(s) and function(s), similarities and differences of the school and clinical psychologist in a variety of settings, including: schools, clinics, hospitals, private practice settings, post-secondary education settings, and other alternative service delivery systems. The philosophical and empirical base of combined training in school and clinical psychology will be emphasized. Issues that impact the role and function of school and clinical psychologists will also be addressed in this course such as history and foundation of school and clinical psychology, ethics and standards, public policy (including legislation and regulations), ethics and legal issues, professional trends, quality of practice, and the organization and operation of schools and mental health settings.

PSYD 7419.description: This course is an introduction to the interdisciplinary field of Developmental psychopathology. Developmental psychopathology is a paradigm that articulates the role of time and change in childhood disturbances. We will examine this fundamental aspect of childhood disorders. Students will become familiar with relevant literature concerning the disorders of childhood and adolescents. Furthermore, students will gain mastery in conceptualizing and synthesizing clinical information to formulate an initial clinical diagnosis. In addition to a broad overview of child clinical disorders, we will examine a number of key themes including 1) a developmental perspective which is important for understanding childhood disorders; 2) the role of risk and resilience; and 3) how social, cultural, and economic diversity impacts

PSYD 7206.description: This course is designed to provide a survey of the most often used projective instruments (Rorschach, TAT, Sentence Completion, Projective Drawings, etc.), which contribute to the assessment of personality. Students will be introduced to the projective hypothesis and the psychometrics underlying these instruments. The primary focus, however, will be on the development of clinical skills regarding the administration, scoring, interpretation, and communication of data obtained from these instruments. Ethical and cultural factors related to projective data will also be discussed.

EDL 7602.description: An advanced course that examines the philosophical and social forces that shape public school curricula.  Attention is given to curriculum development, planning and integration, principles and procedures, preparation and survey of current practices and trends.

PSYD 7113.description: This first course of a three-semester sequence focuses on issues important to professionals about to enter the field including an orientation to issues in day-to-day practice and professional development in modern school and clinical psychology. Ethical and legal issues will be covered in detail, with particular reference to the everyday issues of practice, and special emphasis will be made to integrate these legal, ethical and professional issues into a thorough discussion and monitoring of students 'current externship experiences.

PSYD 7411.description: A comprehensive examination of current knowledge and research on individuals with disabilities within the context of human growth and development across the lifespan. Content includes historical factors, legislation, etiology, characteristics, needs, multidisciplinary approaches, best practices in social, psychological and educational strategies, including existing and emerging technologies, assessment, response to intervention (RTI), and support services for individuals with disabilities ranging from mild, moderate, to severe levels of varying disabilities. The course will study the impact of disabilities on academic, social and emotional functioning, and contain a unit on writing, developing, and updating Individualized Education Plans (IEP). Coverage of issues relating to linguistic and cultural diversity.

PSYD 7701.description: This first course of a three semester course sequence is intended to be the first full-year supervision-based educational experience that allows students to develop a professional identity and translate the knowledge and theory of clinical science into practical clinical skills while they provide direct assessment, consultation, and clinical services to children, adolescents, families and individual adults at the doctoral program outpatient community mental health center.

PSYD 7109.description: Study of physiological processes involved in human learning and behavior. Functioning of the nervous system, endocrine system and sensory systems as well as an overview of learning disorders and psychological disorders and their treatment will be discussed.

PSYD 7451.description: In this course students will become exposed to available child and adolescent psychological treatments, understand how to critically select and evaluate treatments, begin to understand and practice how psychological treatments for children and adolescents are implemented, consider relevant issues pertaining to diversity in clinical practice, and gain an appreciation for relevant ethical issues in the delivery of psychological treatments with this population. While this course places emphasis on empirically supported therapies, the majority of which are behavioral or cognitive-behavioral in nature, several treatments will be discussed that have not been validated as extensively.

EDL 7502.description: Public school administration in historical perspective.  Analysis of organizational roles, including leadership, and curriculum and instruction oversight.  Examination and application of strategic planning techniques, communication strategies, current practices and research.

PSYD 7114.description: This is the second part of a three-part, year-long, professional seminar designed to support the school externship and "positive impact" project, as well to study the various ethical codes, specialty guidelines and recommendations that apply to the practice of school psychology, clinical psychology, and the intersection between the two. The literature on ethics is critically examined and readings include a variety of original sources. Students will examine case material that features ethical conflicts and controversies, and students practice processes to resolve ethical dilemmas.

PSYD 7702.description: This second course of a three semester course sequence is intended to be the first full year supervision-based educational experience that allows students to develop a professional identity and translate the knowledge and theory of clinical science into practical clinical skills while they provide direct assessment, consultation and clinical services to children, adolescents, families and individual adults at the doctoral program outpatient community mental health center.

PSYD 7461.description: This course is intended to provide the historical development, theoretical rationale and the empirical base for rationalist, constructivist, and contextual approaches to cognitive behavioral therapy as well as other treatment modalities that have garnered empirical support, such as interpersonal psychotherapy and emotion-focused psychotherapy. These psychological treatment modalities will be discussed in terms of efficacy in the treatment of a wide range of psychological disorders and with diverse populations. Issues relating to psychotherapy integration, ethical considerations in treatment selection, and treatment of diverse populations will also be covered in this class.

PSYD 7115.description: This third part of a three-part, yearlong, professional seminar designed to support the completion of the school externship/beginning of the clinical externship (as applicable), as well as to study the various ethical codes, specialty guidelines and recommendations that apply to the practice of school psychology, clinical psychology, and the intersection between the two. Risk management will also be discussed. The literature on ethics is critically examined and readings include a variety of original sources. Students will examine case material that features ethical conflicts and controversies, and students practice processes to resolve ethical dilemmas.

PSYD 7208.description: This integrative battery course teaches students how to incorporate cognitive, projective, and objective personality test data into comprehensive psychological evaluations and provide experience integrating assessment data for educational, clinical, and industry referrals. Students will be able to write evaluations using professional style and technical language that: 1) Describe and predict complex behaviors within the context of the whole person, 2) accurately provide a diagnostic formulation and 3) lead to pragmatic recommendations for interventions.

PSYD 7703.description: This third course of a three semester course sequence is intended to be the first full-year supervision-based educational experience that allows students to develop a professional identity and translate the knowledge and theory of clinical science into practical clinical skills while they provide direct assessment, consultation and clinical services to children, adolescents, families and individual adults at the doctoral program outpatient community mental health center.

PSYD 7209.description: This course is designed to introduce students to the foundations of neuropsychological assessment. An overview of the concept of cerebral localization in addition to a general introduction to the major brain structures and functions will be provided. The foremost realms of cognitive functioning and dysfunction will be presented and the student will be expected to develop a basic knowledge and understanding of brain-behavior relationships. The course will include an introduction to neuropsychological batteries in addition to exposure to specific, relevant neuropsychological tests. Application of neuropsychological theories and principles to the appropriate assessment, remedial programming, and school related issues and/or concerns will also be discussed.

PSYD 7470.description: A clinical skills course that will examine psychological interventions as a change agent in clinical-community and school settings. The empirical foundations of psychological interventions will be addressed with an emphasis on practice implications of current therapy research on readiness for change, efficacy and effectiveness, client-therapist variables and the therapeutic relationship. In addition, consideration will be given to issues relating to matching clients to intervention type as well as the theoretical, empirical and practical consideration of essential mechanisms of change in psychological treatment.

PSYD 7116.description: This first course of a three-semester course sequence will provide students with the knowledge needed to be clinically-sensitive psychologists able to work with a range of individuals from diverse backgrounds in educational and clinical settings. Cultural differences and how they relate to the diagnosis of psychopathology will be explored as will how cultures differ on important clinical issues such as violence, suicide, expression of emotions, child-rearing, etc. The APA guidelines for multi-cultural competency will also be presented. Other ways individuals are diverse and therefore can be treated unfairly in our society such as gender, religious practices, sexual orientation, disability, socioeconomic status, or medical conditions will be also explored as will be the role of psychologists in assisting those in need and the importance of social activism. Finally, special emphasis will be made to integrate these issues within a thorough discussion and monitoring of students' current externship experiences.

PSYD 7704.description: This first course of a three semester course sequence is intended to be the second full year supervision-based educational experience that allows students to further develop a professional identity and translate the knowledge and theory of clinical science into a higher level of practical clinical skills while they provide direct assessment, consultation and clinical services to children, adolescents, families and individual adults at the doctoral program outpatient community mental health center.

PSYD 8000.description: This course will focus on developing a dissertation idea; conducting a preliminary literature search and developing advanced strategies for statistical analyses. The dissertation is intended to help students develop the skills necessary to become informed consumers of the psychological literature and apply these skills in the production of an original and independent scholarly product. The dissertation also provides students the opportunity to further enhance their critical thinking and provides an opportunity to deepen knowledge and thought about a particular clinical area of interest.

PSYD 7900.description: This first course of a three semester course sequence is intended to be an opportunity for students to develop an emphasis area within school and clinical psychology by working directly with a core program faculty member in that faculty members' area of expertise/specialty. This sequence allows further development and refinement of a students' skill sets as a practitioner-scientist by engaging in advanced readings and research work under close faculty supervision.

PSYD 7010.description: An in-depth analysis of best practices and current research in consultation and collaboration between multidisciplinary school and clinical personnel, family members, and community agencies in fostering learning, and emotional/social development in children and adolescents. Understanding achievement and performance difficulties across the child and adolescent lifespan in terms of parent-school-community communication and problem solving. Explore the major models of consultation and collaboration and develop expertise to consult with teachers, administrators, other helping professionals, and family members. Presentation of a generic model that includes the main components of effective school/family community consultation and collaboration. Consideration of factors relating to linguistic and cultural diversity.

PSYD 7180.description: This course provides the student with knowledge about and understanding of the development of psychology as a science and profession. Three general topic areas are examined: psychology's historical roots in philosophy, natural science, and national cultures; the work of the early psychologists; and the development of various "systems" or " schools" of psychology in the 20th century. The course is also concerned with the nature of science, and aims at providing the student with an understanding of how scientific ideas develop, are applied, and change.

PSYD 7117.description: Proseminar IIIb Gender, Ethnicity, and Culture in the Professional Practice of School and Clinical Psychology is the second course in a three-course series that addresses issues of cultural competence in the practice of psychology.  A seminar course designed to assist doctoral psychology trainees in the ongoing process of becoming culturally competent professionals, it offers an opportunity to explore further social constructs related to cultural identity, including race, ethnicity, gender, and sexual identity that were introduced in Proseminar IIIa. In addition, this section of the course will address spirituality/religiosity and how they pertain to psychotherapy practice, and will also address aspects of living with disability, including physical disability and cognitive disabilities. The course uses a variety of active learning methods to encourage participants to explore their own cultural positions, including biases and assumptions that could influence psychological assessment and treatment.

PSYD 7705.description: This second course of a three semester course sequence is intended to be the second full year supervision-based educational experience that allows students to further develop a professional identity and translate the knowledge and theory of clinical science into a higher level of practical clinical skills while they provide direct assessment, consultation and clinical services to children, adolescents, families and individual adults at the doctoral program outpatient community mental health center.

PSYD 8010.description: This course is a continuation of PSYD 8000 and focuses on the continued development, under supervision, of an individual doctoral dissertation. Graded on a credit granted (CG) or credit not granted (NC) basis.

PSYD 8001.description: This course consists of work between student and dissertation chair developing a dissertation idea; conducting a thorough literature search; and writing up and orally defending the dissertation proposal. Graded on a credit granted (CG) or credit not granted (NC)basis. The doctoral dissertation is intended to help students develop the skills necessary to become informed consumers of the psychological literature and apply these skills in the production of an original and independent scholarly product. The dissertation project also provides students the opportunity to further enhance their critical thinking and provides an opportunity to deepen knowledge and thought about a particular area of interest relevant to the practice of school and/or clinical psychology.

PSYD 7901.description: This second course of a three semester course sequence is intended to be an opportunity for students to develop an emphasis area within school and clinical psychology by working directly with a core program faculty member in that faculty members' area of expertise/specialty. This sequence allows further development and refinement of a students' skill sets as a practitioner-scientist by engaging in advanced readings and research work under close faculty supervision.

PSYD 7207.description: Introduction and comprehensive overview of theoretical concepts and intervention strategies. Examines basic premises of varying family therapy models and ways of thinking essential to working with relational units, including couples, families and organizational systems. Influence of cultural/ethnic differences and their effect on family relationships and functioning. Marital interaction, family development, parenting, societal and community resources including presentation and analysis of contemporary methods in systemic family therapy.

PSYD 7118.description: This third course of a three-semester course sequence will provide students with the knowledge needed to be clinically sensitive psychologists able to work with a range of individuals from diverse backgrounds in educational and clinical settings. Cultural differences and how they relate to the diagnosis of psychopathology will be explored as will how cultures differ on important clinical issues such as violence, suicide, expression of emotions, child-rearing, etc. The APA guidelines for multicultural competency will also be presented. Other ways individuals are diverse and therefore can be treated unfairly in our society such as gender, religious practices, sexual orientation, disability, socioeconomic status, or medical conditions will be also explored as will the role of psychologists in assisting those in need and the importance of social activism. Finally, special emphasis will be made to integrate these issues within a thorough discussion and monitoring of students? Current externship experiences.

PSYD 7706.description: This third course of a three semester course sequence is intended to be the second full year supervision-based educational experience that allows students to develop a professional identity and translate the knowledge and theory of clinical science into practical clinical skills while they provide direct assessment, consultation and clinical services to children, adolescents, families and individual adults and the doctoral program outpatient community mental health center.

PSYD 7902.description: This third course of a three semester course sequence is intended to be an opportunity for students to develop an emphasis area within school and clinical psychology by working directly with a core program faculty member in the faculty members' area of expertise/specialty. This sequence allows further development and refinement of a students' skill sets as a practitioner-scholar by engaging in advanced readings and research work under close faculty supervision.
