PSYD 8011.description: This course consists of work between student and dissertation chair, based on a successfully defended dissertation proposal, to complete an Institutional Review Board application and prepare to collect data. Graded on a credit granted (CG) or credit not granted (NC) basis. The doctoral dissertation is intended to help students develop the skills necessary to become informed consumers of the psychological literature and apply these skills in the production of an original and independent scholarly product. The dissertation project also provides students the opportunity to further enhance their critical thinking and provides an opportunity to deepen knowledge and thought about a particular area of interest relevant to the practice of school and/or clinical psychology.

PSYD 7311.description: This course is designed to prepare students for professional roles as an applied behavioral consultant and/or program evaluator. This course will focus on the dynamics of working with groups in a variety of settings and on developing effective interventions at a systemic level. It will also provide the knowledge, skills, perspectives, and reflective practice students will need to effectively conduct a program evaluation in human service agencies. A range of exercises will be utilized to give each student an opportunity to become familiar with issues involved in consultation and program evaluation.

PSYD 7600.description: In-depth consideration of selected major theories and findings of social behavior as they relate to the school setting. Topics include aggression, social perception and cognition, attitudes, non-verbal communication, prejudice, social influence and conformity, pro-social behavior, group dynamics, interpersonal attraction, and intimate relationships.

PSYD 7119.description: This first course of a two-semester sequence focuses on issues important to professionals about to enter the field including an orientation to issues in day-today practice and professional development in modern school and clinical psychology with a special emphasis on new technologies available for assessment, treatment and practice management, as well as consideration of emerging issues in the field such as health care financing, professional accountability, psychology as a primary care profession, professional activism, etc.. Special emphasis will be made to integrate these issues into a thorough discussion and monitoring of students' current externship experiences.

PSYD 7707.description: This first part of a two-semester course sequence is intended to be the third full year supervision-based educational experience that allows students to develop a professional identity and translate the knowledge and theory of clinical science into practical clinical skills while they provide direct assessment, consultation and clinical services to children, adolescents, families and individual adults at the doctoral program outpatient community mental health center.

PSYD 8020.description: This course is a continuation of PSYD 8000 and PSYD 8010 and focuses providing support and information for students actively engaged in the final stages of completing and writing their dissertation, preparing for the dissertation defense, and submitting the final document. This course must be repeated until the dissertation is fully complete.

PSYD 8021.description: This course consists of work between student and dissertation chair, based on an approved Institutional Review Board application, to begin data collection and develop a database. The doctoral dissertation is intended to help students develop the skills necessary to become informed consumers of the psychological literature and apply these skills in the production of an original and independent scholarly product. The dissertation project also provides students the opportunity to further enhance their critical thinking and provides an opportunity to deepen knowledge and thought about a particular area of interest relevant to the practice of school and/or clinical psychology.

PSYD 7140.description: This course is designed to focus upon the supervisory and management roles of the school and clinical psychologist, acknowledging both theoretical and conceptual understanding for the supervision process. The goal is to empower the student to become a leader in their professional area of interest.

PSYD 7480.description: This course will focus on how psychoactive drugs impact human behavior. Emphasis will be on how selected drugs interact with neurotransmitter systems and how neurotransmitter systems modulate behavior. Particular reference will be made to reactions in the central nervous system to chemically effective drugs such as the anti-psychotic, anti-depressant, and anti-anxiety drug classes. Indications for and contraindications to the use of these prescribed drugs will be stressed, as well as other management strategies.

PSYD 7120.description: This second course of a two-semester sequence focuses on issues important to professionals about to enter the field including an orientation to issues in day-to-day practice and professional development in modern school and clinical psychology with a special emphasis on new technologies available for assessment, treatment and practice management, as well as consideration of emerging issues in the field such as health care financing, professional accountability, psychology as a primary care profession, professional activism. Special emphasis will be made to integrate these issues into a thorough discussion and monitoring of students' current externship experiences.

PSYD 7708.description: This second part of a two-semester course sequence is intended to be the third full year supervision-based educational experience that allows students to further develop a professional identity and translate the knowledge and theory of clinical science into a higher level of practical clinical skills while providing direct assessment, consultation and clinical services to children, adolescents, families and individual adults at the doctoral program outpatient community mental health center.

PSYD 8500.description: This is the First course in a yearlong sequence, which includes supervised experience of 1,500 hours (program requirement) or 1,750 hours (pre-doctoral licensing requirement) in a setting determined by the program director and the student. Graded on a credit granted (CG) or credit not granted (CNG) basis.

PSYD 8510.description: This is a Second course in a yearlong sequence of 1,500 hours (program requirement) or 1,750 hours (pre-doctoral licensing requirement) in a setting determined by the program director and the student. Graded on a credit granted (CG) or credit not granted (CNG) basis.

PSYD 8520.description: This is a third course in a year-long sequence: which includes Supervised experience of 1,500 hours (program requirement) or 1,750 hours (pre-doctoral licensing requirement) in a setting determined by the program director and the student. Graded on a credit granted (CG) or credit not granted (CNG) basis.

metadata.full_name: Communication Studies, M.A.

details.description: The Master of Arts in Communication Studies links the process of human communication with organizational behavior, interpersonal relationships, leadership, culture, and management practices within a diverse and changing society. Students are provided the opportunity to engage in a multifaceted course of study that integrates both theoretical frameworks and practical approaches. Our program not only prepares students for advanced doctoral education, but also equips them with competent communication skills needed in a workplace.

COMM 5002.description: Intensive study of quantitative research designs with an emphasis on assessing strengths and limitations of the various approaches. Individual research projects are planned, conducted, and reported.

COMM 5005.description: An intensive study of interpretive and critical research methods in communication.  Emphasis will be on specific interpretive methods such as discourse analysis, textual analysis, inter- views and focus groups.

COMM 5004.description: Analysis of traditional and contemporary theories of communication in the context of modern complex organizations. The theoretical relationship between communication and organizations through the study of discourse, meanings, symbols, and information flow. TYPE OF COURSE: Required

COMM 5006.description: An investigation of the intersection between organizational culture and communication. This course will cover various facets of culture that influence communication in an assortment of organizational settings.  TITLE CHANGED AS OF 10/25/07 PREV TITLE: CULTURAL COMMUNICATION WITHIN ORGANIZATIONS

COMM 5001.description: Intensive evaluation and application of theories and research within interpersonal communication. Selected problems and contemporary research will be emphasized. Prerequisite: Permission of the instructor.

COMM 5003.description: This course focuses on advanced skills and knowledge needed for strategic and organizational communication consulting.  In-depth analysis of consulting methods, meeting design and facilitation, strategic messaging, and presenta- tion coaching. CAT.TITLE FORMERLY: CONSULTING AND ASSESSMENT IN ORGANIZATIONAL COMMUNICATION. revised 11/21/05.

COMM 5007.description: Intensive study of a selection topic from the theoretical and research literature of organizational communication; critique of research findings and methodologies.

COMM 5009.description: Examination of public relations theory and strate- gies for shaping organizational goals.  Emphasis on managing corporate communication campaigns.

COMM 5010.description: Intensive study of a selected topic from the theoretical and research literature of communication and culture; critique of research findings and methodologies.

COMM 5012.description: In-depth analysis of small group communication theory, decision making, and group dynamics. Analysis of how small groups function in multiple contexts with emphasis on improving communication. CAT.TITLE FORMERLY: SMALL GROUP DECISION MAKING AND LEADERSHIP. revised 11/21/05

COMM 5013.description: A focus on a broad study of formal and informal speaking and listening skills within business, corporations, and public and governmental agencies, including creating a professional image and corporate strategies and tactics. CAT.TITLE FORMERLY: ADVANCED PRESENTATION SKILLS AND MOTIVATIONAL PRACTICES. revised 11/21/05.

COMM 5014.description: Theory and practice of planning, preparing, presenting and coaching presentation of speeches for a range of situations and client needs. PREREQUISITE:GRADUATE LEVEL STATUS OR PERMISSION OF INSTRUCTOR

COMM 5016.description: Intensive study of a selected topic from the theoretical and research literature of applied communication; critique of research findings and methodologies. With the permission of the program director, students can take this course a second time as long as it is a different topic.

COMM 5020.description: Advanced concepts, theories, and skills related to communication and leadership.  In-depth analysis focusing on various communication strategies and approaches for leading organiza- tions and managing individuals and teams.  Exam- ination of leadership related to decision-making, organizational problem solving and change management.

COMM 5021.description: EXAMINATION OF THE THEORIES, PRINCIPLES, MODELS, AND INTERVENTIONS IN COMMUNICATION TRAINING  AND DEVELOPMENT IN AN ORGANIZATION.  FOCUS IS ON TRADITIONAL COMPONENTS OF EFFECTIVE TRAINING  AND DEVELOPMENT WITHIN ORGANIZATIONS.  EMPHASIS ON PRACTICAL APPLICATION INCLUDING RESEARCH, NEEDS, PROGRAMS, TRAINING, DEVELOPMENT SESSIONS, AND PROGRAM EVALUATION. LIMITATION OF ENROLLMENT: 15 TYPE OF COURSE OF COURSE:  ELECTIVE

COMM 5225.description: ADVANCED EXAMINATION OF THE BASIC THEORIES AND PRINCIPLES USED IN DEVELOPING CORPORATE ADVERTISING. EXAMINATION OF THE RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN COMPANIES, PUBLIC RELATIONS, AND AD AGENCIES IN THE DEVELOPMENT OF COMMUNICATIONS CAMPAIGNS.  PREREQ: NONE

COMM 5280.description: Analysis of the role of the American news media and the forces that influence news coverage, with an emphasis on developing skills to judge the credibility of reports and sources in an age of rapidly changing technology.

COMM 5425.description: Advanced examination of the concepts of nonverbal communication including the study of space, touch, body movements, facial and vocal expression.  Emphasis on integrating theory, research, and practical knowledge of nonverbal communication.

COMM 5535.description: Analysis of the stages of a crisis, development of crisis plans and dealing with the media in public and interpersonal settings.  Students will prepare and present crisis messages and press packets.  Students will learn to conduct commun- ity meetings. Prerequisite: Bachelor's degree or permission of instructor.

COMM 5540.description: In-depth study and analysis of communication messages that create, build, maintain and re- solve conflict on an interpersonal or societal level.  Examination of current research.

COMM 5550.description: Examination various aspects of management principles and theories relevant to electronic media management of personnel, programming, sales, and promotion. Discuss the operation of radio, television, cable and Internet Service Provider organizations at the local, national and international levels.  COURSE TYPE: Elective

COMM 5590.description: An introduction to the principles, practice and importance of oral presentations within business and professional organizational organizational settings.  Presenting informative and persuasive reports and research.  Revised as of 5/26/09  Previous Title:  Business and Technical Presentations

COMM 5620.description: COURSE #: COMM 5620 SEMESTER HOURS: THREE(3) ENROLLMENT LIMIT: 15 COURSE TYPE: ELECTIVE PREREQ: NONE  Advanced application of television and media journalism techniques, and the handling of news and information in society. Analysis of various advanced reporting and editing strategies.

COMM 5615.description: Advanced practical training and theoretical understanding of mediators and mediation.  Under- standing the tradition, history, and theory be- hind mediation, as well as implications of/for alternative dispute resolution, and practical hands on training in the communication processes of mediation.  Gradute level course. Prerequisite: Admission to the graduate program or instructor permission.

COMM 5625.description: Communication skills to negotiate personal, community and high stake disputes.  Constructive problem solving approaches to find solutions to meet the needs of all disputants. TYPE OF COURSE: Elective (cannot receive credit if previously taken as an undergraduate course) PREREQUISITES: Admission to graduate program or permission of instructor

COMM 5626.description: Explores international communication to address conflict and resolutions in mediated, inter- personal, organizational, and multicultural interactions.   Prerequisite: Undergraduate degree or permission of Instructor

COMM 5630.description: Advanced analysis of communication processes and behaviors that create and recreate gender roles for both women and men.  An in-depth examination of gender issues in media and technology, edu- cation, workplace, and interpersonal relation- ships.  Analysis of current research related to the topic.

COMM 5635.description: Catalog Description:  Advanced examination of the nature of persuasion in human interaction and decision making. Explores various definitions, models, theories, and research in persuasion.  Examines ethical perspectives of persuasion and how to create effective persuasive messages.  Prerequisite:  Course cannot be taken if taken at the undergraduate level.  Type of course:  Elective

COMM 5640.description: Advanced critique and analysis of effective public relations principles, practices, and strategies in a legal and ethical context. Examiniation of current research related to the topic.

COMM 5665.description: Advanced preparation of public relations documents and portfolio.  Analysis of current research re- lated to the topic.

COMM 5675.description: An in-depth study of the concepts and processes of media advertising focusing on the economic, regulatory, global and social forces that impact the management of advertising campaigns in the radio, television, cable, and telecommunication industries.  Students will complete a three-part advertising campaign.  Examination of current research as it relates to media and advertising.

COMM 5900.description: COURSE NO:  COMM 5900 SEMESTER HOURS:  THREE(3) PREREQ:  Graduate standing, or permission of the instructor ENROLLMENT LIMIT:  15 COURSE TYPE:  Elective for Communication graduate students   A production-oriented course designed to intergrate principles and practices of digital media communication. Students will prepare and present digital media productions.

ID 5800.description: Designed to provide timely investigation of a particular contemporary issue. As subject and content change each semester the course number also changes.

metadata.full_name: Computer Science, M.S. (Formerly M.S. Computer Information Systems - No longer accepting new students)
metadata.coordinator: Students who do not meet the University’s admission requirements or prerequisite coursework may be granted conditional admission, as determined by the Graduate Program Coordinator. In such cases, they must complete assigned bridge courses, which do not count toward the 30-credit degree requirement.

details.description: The Master of Science in Computer Science provides a strong foundation in advanced computing principles, algorithms, and system design, preparing students with the analytical and technical skills to solve complex computing challenges. Students gain expertise in key areas such as software engineering, artificial intelligence, data science, cybersecurity, and computer systems. The program provides hands-on experience in designing, developing, and optimizing computing technologies while strengthening analytical thinking skills. Graduates are prepared for leadership roles in academia, industry, government, and the technology sector, with opportunities to engage in research and independent projects across various areas of computer science.

CPS 5520.description: This course provides a comprehensive overview of foundational and advanced topics in computer systems, including architecture, operating systems, data processing, and emerging trends. Emphasis is placed on understanding core concepts, practical applications, and ethical considerations in modern computing environments.

CPS 5301.description: Application of a systemic, disciplined, quantifiable approach to the development, operation, and maintenance of software. Methods and techniques used in the design and development of large-scale information systems. Project management, cost models, user requirements, analysis, design, implementation strategies, testing techniques, quality, and maintenance issues are covered.

CPS 5440.description: Advanced algorithm analysis techniques. Algorithms involving advanced data structures, sorting, trees, and graphs. NP-Completeness. Randomized Algorithms. Approximation Algorithms.

CPS 5250.description: This course explores the intersection of computing, ethics, and society. It examines the ethical implications of technology in various societal contexts and provides students with a foundation in ethical reasoning and decision-making within the field of computing.

CPS 5498.description: Computer forensic investigation principles, digital evidence, Window and Command Line Interface (CLI) operating systems investigation, online and virtual machine forensics, document and photograph forensics, forensic tools, countermeasure of anti-forensics, processing digital crime and incident scenes, and presenting digital evidence in legal cases.

CPS 5500.description: Introduction to programming on the Internet and the technical aspects of the World Wide Web. Topics include HTML, CSS, Web server administration, JavaScript, Java, data access, and Internet security. Students are expected to complete a programming project.

CPS 5601.description: Principles and methods in modern human-computer interaction (HCI) design and development; User requirements; Usability tests; Interaction devices; Interface evaluation; Tools and platforms.

CPS 5721.description: This course covers fundamentals of knowledge discovery and data mining concepts, techniques, algorithms and languages; architectures, designs and technology; and includes applications in business and the sciences.

CPS 5740.description: Enterprise database management systems; database design; database implementation; storage and indexing; transaction processing concepts; data warehousing, data mining and online analytical processing (OLAP); emerging database applications.

CPS 5745.description: This course focuses on the methods to automatically transform data into a visual context in different formats-charts, graphs, dashboards and dynamic web pages. Students will study how to design, develop the effective and interactive visualization tools to analyze the data from files, database, and various data sources as well as learn how to use popular visualization software.

CPS 5801.description: This course introduces the five fundamental topics of AI, 1) problem-solving, 2) reasoning and planning, 3) dealing with uncertainty, 4) learning, and 5) communication with perceiving and acting. The focus will be on the first three with an example of autonomous systems for the 5th one.

CPS 5802.description: This course will explore machine learning (ML) algorithms as an integrated part of Artificial Intelligence (AI). The emphasis will be on the algorithms and their implementations including supervised learning, statistical learning, neural network and support vector machine. We will also look into more advanced topics such as domain reduction and clustering and deep reinforcement learning. Besides existing algorithms, we will also study how to create new algorithms based on advanced analytical results.

CPS 5881.description: Independent Graduate Study in Computer Science focuses on an advanced topic not covered in the department's regular course offerings. Students develop an independent course of study under the guidance of a faculty member using an independent study form. This course may be taken only once; can be applied as a graduate elective.

CPS 5882.description: Independent Graduate Study in Computer Science focuses on an advanced topic not covered in the department's regular course offerings. Students develop an independent course of study under the guidance of a faculty member using an independent study form. This course may be taken only once; can be applied as a graduate elective.

CPS 5883.description: Independent Graduate Study in Computer Science focuses on an advanced topic not covered in the department's regular course offerings. Students develop an independent course of study under the guidance of a faculty member using an independent study form. This course may be taken only once; can be applied as a graduate elective.

CPS 5930.description: Analysis of concepts and functions of an operating system.  Interrelationships of the operating system and hardware, file organization and access, storage and process management in a multi-processor environment.

CPS 5931.description: Principles, concepts, design and development of network systems. Enterprise, virtual, and cloud based systems will be reviewed for performance, security, and access.

CPS 5965.description: Fundamental concepts of high performance computing; hardware architecture; software libraries; design,analysis, implementation and performance evaluation of parallel and distributed algorithms.

CPS 5980.description: Introduction to contemporary computer and network security issues, concepts and applications.  Topics include security threats, secret key and public key cryptographic algorithms, authentication and signature, IP security architecture, applications and standards.

CPS 5981.description: Concepts, practices and methodologies for ensuring integrity, security, and reliability in software code development, including processes and procedures that diminish the possibility of erroneous code, malicious code, or trap doors that could be introduced during development.

CPS 5990.description: The study of topics in computer science that are extensions of existing courses or topics that result from new developments  and research in computer science.

CPS 5961.description: This course is exclusively designated for M.S. thesis research. Throughout this semester-long course, M.S. students will engage in the composition and public defense of a master’s under the mentorship of a faculty member. Students must identify a significant research question within the discipline, select an appropriate methodology for investigation, and apply relevant concepts, theories, and methods to conduct and analyze their research. Each student will work with a supervisor who will guide the research process and M.S. thesis writing. The course is repeatable for up to 6 credits.

CPS 5995.description: A software implementation project course that integrates theory and practice in the design and development of software systems. Students will select a project to solve a practical problem, applying research, design, development, testing, documentation, and presentation skills. All projects require instructor approval.

metadata.full_name: Computer Science, Ph.D.

details.description: This program aims to prepare students for advanced careers and research opportunities in the rapidly evolving tech industry. To further address national needs, the program will specifically focus on critical areas such as Artificial Intelligence (AI), Cybersecurity, and Data Science. The PhD program in Computer Science is designed to develop leading researchers and educators who will contribute across various sectors including academia, industry, education, non-profits, and government.

CPS 5250.description: This course explores the intersection of computing, ethics, and society. It examines the ethical implications of technology in various societal contexts and provides students with a foundation in ethical reasoning and decision-making within the field of computing.

CPS 5440.description: Advanced algorithm analysis techniques. Algorithms involving advanced data structures, sorting, trees, and graphs. NP-Completeness. Randomized Algorithms. Approximation Algorithms.

CPS 5010.description: Computing concepts, components and processes; hardware and software components; communications and information systems; systems and applications software; introduction to programming concepts and techniques; computational problem-solving and designing algorithms using a high-level programming language. Required to take as a bridge course for Computer Information Systems (CIS) degree with admission condition. Credit hours are not counted for graduate degree requirement.

CPS 5011.description: Fundamental computing and programming concepts; use of systems software; problem solving; design of algorithms using a high-level, object-oriented programming language. Required to take as a bridge course for Computer Information Systems (CIS) degree with admission condition. Credit hours are not counted for graduate degree requirement.

CPS 5012.description: The course covers the theory of Abstract Data Types (ADT's), applications and implementations of the classical ADT's including lists, sets, stacks, queues, trees, and graphs, recursion and dynamic programming, and elementary algorithm analysis. Required to take as a bridge course for Computer Information Systems (CIS) degree with admission condition. Credit hours are not counted for graduate degree requirement.

CPS 5013.description: An introductory course covers the operational characteristics and design principles of modern computer systems and their applications. Computer systems and architecture are examined, particularly operating systems and networks. Required to take as a bridge course for Computer Information Systems (CIS) degree with admission condition. Credit hours are not counted for graduate degree requirement.

CPS 5014.description: Analysis techniques. Algorithms involving advanced data structures, sorting, trees, and graphs. NP-Completeness. Required to take as a bridge course for Computer Information Systems (CIS) degree with admission condition. Credit hours are not counted for graduate degree requirement.

CPS 5015.description: Concepts, scope, trends, and strategies for computer security, including cryptographic algorithms and tools, computer security technologies, security management, operating system and network/Internet security, security model, and software vulnerability assessment. Required to take as a bridge course for Computer Information Systems (CIS) degree with admission condition. Credit hours are not counted for graduate degree requirement.

CPS 5100.description: Concepts, principles, and state-of-the-art trends in information systems including enterprise system architectures. Information systems and project management. Enterprise system architectural and analysis. The role of system architecture in software engineering, specifically during requirements analysis, design and implementation. Emerging topics in the development of information systems applications.

CPS 5301.description: Application of a systemic, disciplined, quantifiable approach to the development, operation, and maintenance of software. Methods and techniques used in the design and development of large-scale information systems. Project management, cost models, user requirements, analysis, design, implementation strategies, testing techniques, quality, and maintenance issues are covered.

Students develop an independent course of study under the guidance of a faculty member using an independent study form. This course may be taken only once; can be applied as a graduate elective.

CPS 5900.description: The development and analysis of numerical algorithmic solutions.  Applications of algorithmic modes that can employ measuring devices, desk calculators and/or computers.

CPS 5910.description: Design, analysis and implementation of discrete event and continuous simulation models; statistical modeling techniques used in simulation; random number generation methods.

CPS 5930.description: Analysis of concepts and functions of an operating system.  Interrelationships of the operating system and hardware, file organization and access, storage and process management in a multi-processor environment.

CPS 5931.description: Principles, concepts, design and development of network systems. Enterprise, virtual, and cloud based systems will be reviewed for performance, security, and access.

CPS 5960.description: Deterministic models of operations research including linear programming, network flows, gradients, and minimization methods. Emphasis is on programming solutions and their interpretation.

CPS 5965.description: Fundamental concepts of high performance computing; hardware architecture; software libraries; design,analysis, implementation and performance evaluation of parallel and distributed algorithms.

CPS 5981.description: Concepts, practices and methodologies for ensuring integrity, security, and reliability in software code development, including processes and procedures that diminish the possibility of erroneous code, malicious code, or trap doors that could be introduced during development.

CPS 5990.description: The study of topics in computer science that are extensions of existing courses or topics that result from new developments  and research in computer science.

CPS 6991.description: This course is designed for doctoral students to build on their research competencies from their methods courses to prepare their doctoral dissertation proposal and dissertation. It provides a structured framework within which students conduct original research under the guidance of a faculty advisor, leading to the completion of their doctoral dissertation. The course encompasses formulating research questions, literature review, research design, data collection and analysis, and dissertation writing and defending. Students will engage in independent study, with regular advisory meetings to assess progress and provide feedback.

metadata.full_name: Conflict Resolution and Communication, Post- Baccalaureate Certificate

details.description: Individuals with professional expertise in resolving conflicts are in high demand. Whether you are looking to add conflict resolution to your professional skills or are interested in becoming a conflict resolution professional, Kean Universityâs Certificate Program in Conflict Resolution and Communication provides both the highest level of skill development in conflict-resolution methods and the communication skills required to successfully apply these methods.

COMM 5012.description: In-depth analysis of small group communication theory, decision making, and group dynamics. Analysis of how small groups function in multiple contexts with emphasis on improving communication. CAT.TITLE FORMERLY: SMALL GROUP DECISION MAKING AND LEADERSHIP. revised 11/21/05

metadata.full_name: Counseling, M.A.: Alcohol and Drug Abuse Counseling Option

details.description: The Master of Arts in Counseling, Alcohol and Drug Abuse Counseling Option provides a common core of professional counseling competencies based on national standards, specialized studies and supervised experiences in the field of Alcohol and Drug Abuse Counseling.  The 48 credit degree leads to eligibility for the Licensed Alcohol and Drug Abuse Counselor (LCADC) as courses have been approved by the Certification Board (NJ) for the LCADC.  Depending on the student's background an additional 12 credits may be needed, so this should be discussed with an advisor.

CED 5910.description: Introduction to Philosophy, history and development of counseling and human services, theories and methods of counseling, and current research. Focus on self-exploration and self-awareness, and a foundation for all future counseling courses.  Previous title: Introduction to Counseling and Human Service.

CED 5962.description: Major emphasis on achieving better understanding of the self, the small group, its characteristics and dynamics, and the relationship of group process to effective counseling.

metadata.full_name: Counseling, M.A.: Clinical Mental Health Counseling Option

details.description: The Master of Arts in Counseling, Clinical Mental Health Counseling Option is CACREP accredited (Union and Ocean campuses). It provides education and training in professional counseling for work in a variety of settings including agencies, community organizations, mental health centers, hospitals, and private centers. The program provides comprehensive training involving the knowledge and skills required to work effectively as a professional counselor. Graduates will be immediately eligible to apply for the Licensed Associate Counselor (LAC) credential and take the National Counselor Examination (NCE), if they have not already done so. Students will be able to work in community organizations, counseling and mental health centers, hospitals, private for profit centers and counseling practices while working under supervision to complete the remaining portion of the 4,500 supervised clinical hours.

metadata.full_name: Counseling, Ph.D.: Counseling and Supervision

details.description: The Ph.D. degree doctoral program offered at the Union New Jersey campus isâ¯CACREP accredited with an expiration date of March 31. 2030.

CED 7910.description: An intensive orientation to the doctoral program with an emphasis on professional involvement, leadership roles, teaching, and advocacy. Students will select a Program of Study Committee and develop a proposed Program of Student Plan.

CED 7915.description: An intensive experiential course in social and cultural awareness and issues related to gender, sexual orientation, ethnicity, values, and cultural development of individuals and groups. Includes cultural immersion experiences and examination of emergent research practices and processes in social and cultural counseling.

CED 7952.description: This course is an intensive study of integrative and postmodern counseling theories and evidence-based practices in counseling, such as motivational interviewing, dialectical behavioral therapy, narrative therapy and social construtivism. Research on the efficacy of postmodern therapy, common factors, client-counselor relationships, and continuous client feedback is reviewed.

CED 7963.description: This course addresses advanced group counseling, consultation, and leadership skills in clinical and educational settings, including a comprehensive examination of group theories, approaches, strategies, and therapeutic applications. Students will increase their group skills by leading a master's level process group and participating in group supervision. Group dynamics, group counseling stages, roles, and interventions, as well as ethical, legal and cultural issues are addressed.

CED 7957.description: This course provides an overview of trauma-informed counseling and the triphasic model for trauma recovery with an emphasis on the etiology of trauma, posttraumatic stress disorder, and the neurobiological impact on the brain and body. Trauma sequelae are examined in a range of individual and mass trauma events including sexual abuse, intimate partner violence, mass violence, natural disasters, and combat stress.

CED 7959.description: This course is an intensive study of counseling issues in sexuality and intimacy with diverse populations. Assessment and treatment techniques for sexual dysfunction and disorders are examined.

CED 7975.description: This course provides a comprehensive study of career counseling and development across the life span with an emphasis on life roles, family, gender, and cultural issues. Decision-making, leadership, and advocacy skills are integrated into a case study approach to address lifestyle needs for work, education, and leisure. Students will increase their career assessment, interpretation, and counseling skills by working with university students in a clinic setting. Occupational trends and technology tools will be addressed.

CED 7989.description: This course will review the empirical and conceptual literature on clinical supervision theories, models, methods, and evaluation with an emphasis on supervisee development.  Legal, ethical, and cultural issues will be explored using critical incidents in counseling settings. Students will provide clinical supervision to practicum students and participate in individual and group supervision.

EDL 6330.description: The focus of this first course in Statistics will be to introduce statistical concepts and methods that would help the doctoral students to learn the language of statistics, to use appropriate statistical procedures and gain domain knowledge and analysis skills to interpret data and draw valid inferences.

CED 7930.description: An examination of qualitative research design and methodology with an emphasis on selecting and applying appropriate methodology for research questions including narrative, phenomenological, grounded theory, ethnography, and case study. Procedures in developing the dissertation proposal, as well as conference proposals and journal articles are examined.

EDL 6340.description: The focus of this second course in Statistical Analyses is to build upon fundamental quantitative/qualitative statistical concepts and introduce new constructs, concepts and methods through the use and application of SPSS (Software Package for the Social Sciences) in order to facilitate advanced data analyses and model building.

CED 7936.description: Advanced study of qualitative research design and methodology with an emphasis on selecting and applying appropriate methodology for data collection, analysis, and reporting using narrative, phenomenological, grounded theories, ethnography, and case study. Researcher roles and experiences, selection of site and participants, techniques of data analysis, types of coding and interpretation are addressed. Procedures in developing the dissertation proposal and defense, as well as conference proposals and journal articles are examined.

CED 7980.description: A 120-hour supervised counseling experience, including 50 hours in direct counseling services. The course is designed to expand counseling skills. Recorded counseling sessions are reviewed during weekly individual and group supervision. Successful completion of advanced practicum is a prerequisite for Internship II.

CED 7985.description: A 300-hour supervised field experience in counseling, supervision, research and scholarship, or leadership and advocacy designed in consultation with program faculty. Live and/or recorded sessions are reviewed during weekly individual and group supervision.

CED 7986.description: A 300-hour supervised internship in teaching designed in consultation with clinical and doctoral coordinators. All aspects of course planning, instruction, and assessment are addressed. Live and/or recorded teaching sessions are reviewed during weekly individual and group supervision. Ethical, legal and cultural issues, and technology are discussed. Course may be taken more than once.

CED 8001.description: The doctoral candidate will complete the dissertation process as outlined in the Doctoral Handbook. Candidates must enroll in the 1-credit course CED*8002 Dissertation Continuation if the dissertation process is not complete after completing 12 credits of CED*8001 Dissertation.

CED 8002.description: The doctoral candidate will complete the dissertation process as outlined in the Doctoral Handbook. Candidates must enroll in the 1-credit course CED*8002 Dissertation Continuation if the dissertation process is not complete after completing 12 credits of CED*8001 Dissertation.

CED 7958.description: An overview of psychotropic medications using a case study approach to assessment and treatment planning. Attention is given to counseling and behavioral interventions, drug interaction and side effects, drug dependence, dual diagnosis, and medication adherence.

CED 7961.description: A comprehensive study of trauma assessment and treatment, focusing on developing the therapeutic relationship, regulating emotions, and constructing and sharing the trauma story. Trauma therapy models addressed include somatic, cognitive behavioral, narrative, integrative, EMDR, Brainspotting, and emerging models.

CED 7631.description: An advanced examination of the development, practice, and evaluation of college services for students with diverse backgrounds. Professional roles and programs focus on developmental and systemic student needs. Leadership advocacy, and collaboration models are examined between academic and student services across multiple areas such as admission, orientation, retention, health and wellness, residential life, athletics, activities, and career planning.

CED 7632.description: A comprehensive examination of college student development theory and practice with a focus on the changing profile of college students. Development of advanced counseling skills with students in diverse higher education settings, including crisis intervention, adjustment, suicide prevention, substance use, and peer leadership.

metadata.full_name: Criminal Justice, M.A.
metadata.coordinator: Dr. Thomas Lateano, Program Coordinator

details.description: The Kean University master’s degree program in Criminal Justice studies the various systems within the criminal justice system, the current theoretical models explaining crime and delinquency, their practical use in addressing crime and enforcement, and the use of scientific inquiry to research and address the theoretical and practical problems facing the criminal justice system currently and in the future. The Master of Arts in Criminal Justice program is designed to ensure that students keep pace with the changing environment and are prepared to be productive, engaged contributors to the debate on policy and practice through instruction in the three major components of the system: police, courts, and corrections.

CJ 5670.description: This course presents an in depth overview and analysis of the concepts and complexities of criminological theory.  The ideological and historical context of justice and the application of established theory will be explored.

MATH 5500.description: A course designed to give graduate students with some mathematical maturity basic skills in the use of statistics in research. The course includes the use of the computer to do statistical analysis.  Some topics are: review of descriptive statistics; correlation and regression; hypothesis testing for different types of data; distributions of data; estimation. An undergraduate course in statistics, or the equivalent.

CJ 5610.description: The importance of the scientific method is critical to the evolving study of the criminal justice system and the various agencies and constituencies that comprise and are dependent on the system.  Students will be led through the various stages of research designs, techniques for information gathering, and their application in both policy and inquiry issues.

CJ 5630.description: This course is designed to provide students with a foundation for understanding and analyzing ethical dilemmas in the discipline of criminal justice.  Application of ethical theory to ethical issues and behavior will be examined within the context of its philosophical origins.

CJ 5620.description: This course presents an overview of the criminal justice system and its complexities.  It is designed to explore historical and contemporary criminal justice issues as well as the structure, processes and functions of law enforcement, corrections, and the criminal courts.

CJ 5740.description: This course examines modern American policing and its many challenges. Topics explored include historical trends, recruitment and training, managerial approaches, patrol and investigatory strategies, discretion, misconduct, and responsiveness to the public.

CJ 5690.description: The evolving changes in the criminal justice system are manifested in the country's prison system. As society demands retribution for criminal offenses, prisons become the repository of this social demand.  The need to examine the effects of sentencing enhancements on the prison population and the changing philosophies of punishment are placing an undue burden on correctional facilities across America.  The operational issues exacerbated by prison crowding, reduced state budgets, and longer sentences are the focus of this course.  An examination of the death penalty in the face of mounting public pressure to ensure the innocent are not executed is also addressed in this course.

CJ 5600.description: This course consists of a general survey of the legal system in the United States.  It will examine and analyze the development, structure, function, and nature of the state and federal system through common and statutory law. Additionally, this course will explore the impact of social, economics and political factors on the legal system.

CJ 5650.description: This course is a study of the dimensions of stratification in society and the criminal justice system, particularly stratification on the basis of race, class and gender.  The examination will provide a framework in which contemporary social issues may be interpreted and analyzed. Through an in-depth study of the many different experiences of diverse groups, the student will gain an appreciation for how race, class and gender effect these experiences.

CJ 5710.description: This course presents an in depth overview and analysis of today's four legal traditions: civil law, common law, socialist law and Islamic law. Students will learn to take a global perspective in analyzing the four legal traditions.  As nations' legal systems reflect their historical, cultural, and economic development, these contexts will be explored.

CJ 5680.description: This course exams the scope, causes, and effects of victimization including the impact of victim vulnerability and victim culpability.  The in- creasing role of the victim in the criminal justice process will be thoroughly discussed. Topics will include a focus on victimization as it relates to sexual, child, elderly and spousal abuse; victims rights; the complex interrelation- ship between offender and victim; and restitution alternatives.

CJ 5720.description: The course is a supervised placement within an approved criminal justice agency a bi-weekly seminar to integrate the field experience with academic knowledge. The emphasis for this course is the acquisition of career relevant knowledge of the agency placement.

CJ 5730.description: This course consists of a general survey of criminal law in the United States including elements of crimes, applicable defenses, and sanctions.  It will examine the development, structure, function, and nature of criminal law in the state and federal system.  The theoretical framework and application of common and statutory law will be analyzed.

CJ 5640.description: This course examines the complex relationship between public interest and individual liberty. The nature and effectiveness of theoretical constitutional interpretation and the use of precedent as a means to adjudication will be discussed and analyzed.

ID 5801.description: Designed to provide timely investigation of a particular contemporary issue. As subject and content change each semester the course number also changes.

metadata.full_name: Director of School Counseling, Post-Masterâs Certification
metadata.coordinator: This endorsement is required for any person who is assigned as a director, administrator or supervisor of school counseling (formerly Student Personnel Services) in a school system, including the supervision of educational activities in areas related to and within the counseling program and the supervision of various services in a school district. To acquire New Jersey State certification as Director of School Counseling Services, a student must hold New Jersey Certification in School Counseling and have completed three years experience in school counseling.

EDL 5502.description: Public school administration in historical perspective. Analysis of organizational roles, including leadership, and curriculum and instruction oversight. Examination and application of strategic planning techniques, communication strategies, current practices and research.

EDL 5613.description: Examination of philosophical and social forces that shape public school curricula.  Attention given to curriculum design, development, implementation and evaluation as well as to the master planning and integration.

CED 5989.description: This course prepares counselors to use a variety of individual and group supervision models that are appropriate for counseling settings. Class time is devoted to instruction, role-play, theoretical and supervision issues, and ethical dilemmas. Students also discuss specific situations and issues that arise in their supervisory relationships.

metadata.full_name: Early Childhood Education, M.A.: Administration in Early Childhood and Family Studies Option

details.description: This program is designed for individuals who wish to assume teaching or administrative positions in early childhood settings. Students are offered two options for programs leading to the Master of Arts: Administration in Early Childhood & Family Studies, and Advanced Curriculum and Teaching.

EC 5000.description: Examination and evaluation of philosophical, historical, psychological, and social foundations of early childhood and family studies and their implications for policy and practice.  Formerly: EC 5000 Modern Trends in EC

EC 5260.description: A research course designed to initiate the graduate student into the processes of educational research and its specialized designs and methodologies.

EC 5300.description: Course explores and evaluated theory and practices in organizing and administering early childhood centers.

EC 5310.description: Designed for educators responsible for the professional development of early childhood staff in different early childhood settings, emphasizing staff development, supervision, and program planning. Observations in an early childhood program are required.

EC 5320.description: Using an ecological system perspective, this course prepares professionals in early childhood settings, birth through age 8, to design and implement partnership program with diverse family groups.

EC 5581.description: Issues in curriculum and teaching in early childhood, based on exploration of theories of developmental learning.

EDL 5606.description: Application of organizational and leadership theories to school supervision.  Emphasis on management of human resources in a professional setting, identification of personal leadership style, and major models and techniques of teacher evaluation.

EC 5590.description: Students complete graduate portfolio that demonstrates their ability to organize and interpret their learning in the graduate program. Emphasis on integration of theory and reflective practice, and application of academic writing conventions.

EC 5100.description: In-depth study of language development and reading as a cognitive and effective process. Current research and strategies for developing language arts/reading programs for young children.

EC 5230.description: Students will acquire an understanding of major theories related to child development in the contexts of the child and the family, and their applications to program planning and activities for young children and their families.

EC 5270.description: To assist teachers in pre-primary and primary grades in the development of knowledge, skills, and values of the various American cultures which impact on our schools.

EC 5275.description: Students will develop skills and strategies for planning and teaching integrated curriculum for young children.

EC 5500.description: Explore developmentally appropriate use of technology by young children in early childhood settings as well as examine the application of digital tools for use in research and action research. Emphasize computer, Internet and other digital technologies. Elective all other graduate students. Formerly: Innovative Computer Applications for Primary Grade Teachers

EC 5598.description: The development of a quality and feasible research proposal on a topic in the area of early childhood and family studies.

EC 5599.description: The development of an action research project in early childhood and family studies, including design of study, collection and analysis of data and formation of conclusions and summary. This research project is a continuation of the project started in EC 5598.

details.description: This degree program is designed for individuals who wish to assume teaching or administrative positions in early childhood settings. Students gain essential theoretical understandings in the field of early childhood education and practical classroom applications of current, research-based teaching strategies.

EC 5100.description: In-depth study of language development and reading as a cognitive and effective process. Current research and strategies for developing language arts/reading programs for young children.

EC 5111.description: Students explore the policies and procedures for identifying children with special needs and understanding the laws relevant to teaching this group of children (Disabilities Act Amendments). Students learn how to appropriately assess student learning and implement differentiated instruction to address all learning needs in the classroom.

EC 5222.description: Students learn about various developmentally, linguistically, and culturally appropriate forms of assessment to promote learning and development in young children. Further examination focuses on what these forms of assessment are, why they are important, and how to apply them effectively.

metadata.full_name: Educational Administration, M.A.: School Business Administrator Option

details.description: The Master of Arts in Educational Administration, option in School Business Administrator is designed to prepare individuals for the position of School Business Administrator. Completion of this program satisfies the requirements for New Jersey certification as a school business administrator, providing all other State certification requirements are met. Under the requirements, in addition to possession of a Bachelor or Master's degree in a recognized field of administration, a Certificate of Eligibility (CE) may be granted by the State when the candidate obtains and completes a State-approved mentorship in a local district.

EDL 5541.description: Investigates use of computer hardware, software, communications and network services in educational administration.

EDL 5606.description: Application of organizational and leadership theories to school supervision.  Emphasis on management of human resources in a professional setting, identification of personal leadership style, and major models and techniques of teacher evaluation.

EDL 5502.description: Public school administration in historical perspective. Analysis of organizational roles, including leadership, and curriculum and instruction oversight. Examination and application of strategic planning techniques, communication strategies, current practices and research.

EDL 5503.description: Advanced analysis of organizational theory applications in public school setting. Emphasis on personnel administration, leadership theory, systems analysis and planning to bring about controlled change in the public school setting. Focus on developing strategies for positive cultural change through effective leadership.

EDL 5520.description: Considers the structure, operation and management of public education finance, including federal and state aid, taxation, budget planning, financing capital projects, debt management, legal factors affecting school functions, income and cost forecasting, and capital management.

EDL 5521.description: Conceptual and operational aspects of school business management and accounting; application of modern technology and systems procedures.

EDL 5530.description: Overview of the planning, evaluation and improvement of school building (s).  In addition, taking into consideration ongoing repairs and maintenance of the facilities, which includes the inter-relationship of pupil needs, community needs,and personnel.   Required: For all School Business Administrator Program Candidates; Elective for Principals, and Supervisors Program Candidates.

EDL 5551.description: Covers legal principles generally applicable to education in the United States and to New Jersey public schools in particular, including Constitutional, statutory, and decisional law affecting policies and practices, with emphasis on analysis of major cases. Required for all School Business Administrator, Principal, and Supervisor Program Candidates. Completion of Modules I & II of the Principal/Supervisor Program sequence, where applicable or instructor's permission. Students not meeting this requirement must withdraw from the class.

PA 5040.description: Introduction to the budgetary cycle and budgetary systems used in the public sector. Emphasis on the development and implementation of operating budgets at the state and local levels of government; consideration of related aspects of financial management; e.g. purchasing, risk management, cash management.

EDL 5813.description: Activities planned in cooperation with sponsoring school districts.  Emphasis on involvement with functional responsibility for planning, developing and/or managing administrative-supervisory tasks.  Prerequisite(s): Graduate students who have completed 24 semester hours (12-15 in certification only programs) of required course work and have a minimum grade point average of 3.0 (B) and school law must have been taken. In addition, if the student is a master's level student, they must have taken research  or are taking research concurrently. Admission is subject to submission of a written plan, signed by the cooperating school district.

EDL 5814.description: Activities planned in cooperation with sponsoring school districts.  Emphasis on involvement with functional responsibility for planning, developing and/or managing administrative-supervisory tasks.   Prerequisite(s): Graduate students who have completed 24 semester hours (12-15 in certification only programs) of required course work and have a minimum grade point average of 3.0(B). Students must have participated in EL*5813 Field I. Admission is subject to submission of a written plan, signed by the cooperating school district.

metadata.full_name: Educational Administration, M.A.: Supervisor and Principal Option

details.description:
Program Description
The Master of Arts in Educational Administration, Principal and Supervisor option is designed to train individuals for the positions of school principal and general supervisor in the K-12 setting. Completion of the program qualifies the individual who holds a New Jersey instructional certificate or educational services certificate, and has had three years’ experience, for certification as a general supervisor, K-12, and five years’ experience for principal certification, K-12. Program completion satisfies the degree and subject matter preparation requirements for a Certificate of Eligibility as a principal. Principal certification candidates also must pass a written State required examination; obtain an offer of employment in a position requiring the principal’s endorsement; and complete a two-year mentored residency following graduation as mandated by the New Jersey Department of Education.

Focus on developing strategies for positive cultural change through effective leadership.

EDL 5607.description:
Theory, research and practice of supervision, approaches to staff development, evaluation, and in-service training.  

Required for all Principal/Supervisor, Supervisor only, and Principal/Supervisor/School Business Administrator combined program candidates.

EDL 5613.description: Examination of philosophical and social forces that shape public school curricula.  Attention given to curriculum design, development, implementation and evaluation as well as to the master planning and integration.

EDL 5614.description: Study of the teaching and learning process. Instructional theories and models, evaluation and assessment techniques applied to the curriculum.

details.description:
The Master of Arts in Educational Administration, Supervisor, Principal and School Business Administrator option is designed to prepare candidates for certification as principals, supervisors, and school business administrators.  Students who complete this program will be eligible to receive certificates of eligibility.  Full certification for principals is granted by the State after successful completion of a mentoring program and the State-approved examination.  Full certification for SBA's is granted by the State after successful completion of a mentoring program.  No exam or mentoring programs are required for supervisors.

This program is in compliance with the requirements of the New Jersey State Department of Education Code.

metadata.full_name: Educational Administration, Post-Master's Certification in Digital Leadership

details.description: The Graduate Certificate Program for Digital Leadership is designed for students who possess a master’s degree, are interested in the field of educational leadership, and wish to receive a certificate that assures they can use digital technology to supplement their roles as leaders.  Please note that this certificate leads to a University issued certificate only.

EDL 5513.description: Students will gain the fundamental foundations of utilizing technology within specific content areas. Specifically, students will acquire the skill set to execute technology within instruction, assessment, and content specific 21st methods.

EDL 5512.description: Students will gain the fundamental foundations of utilizing technology within specific content areas. Specifically, students will acquire the skill set to execute technology within instruction, assessment, and content specific 21st methods.

EDL 5511.description: Students will gain the fundamental foundations of how to incorporate cutting edge technology into their learning environment to increase student engagement and motivation. Students will be exposed to the latest innovative tools as they pertain to 21st century instruction, assessment, and professional development.

EDL 5514.description: Students will be able to explore a variety of 21st century instructional models that pertain to blended and personalized learning. Students will explore how the State of New Jersey catalyzed innovation with the creation of the Department of Innovation. Students will explore a series of classrooms that use 21st century instructional models in the State of New Jersey, in the United States, and at the international level.

EDL 5510.description: Students will learn how to leverage technology to communicate with a variety of stakeholders in their roles as educational administrators. Students will understand complexity of family issues pertaining to the use technology pertaining to different demographics. Student will learn how communication through technology may differ in rural, suburban, and urban settings. Students will understand the importance of developing a digital brand for the school districts that they serve.
