EDL 5521.description: Conceptual and operational aspects of school business management and accounting; application of modern technology and systems procedures.

EDL 5530.description: Overview of the planning, evaluation and improvement of school building (s).  In addition, taking into consideration ongoing repairs and maintenance of the facilities, which includes the inter-relationship of pupil needs, community needs,and personnel.   Required: For all School Business Administrator Program Candidates; Elective for Principals, and Supervisors Program Candidates.

EDL 5813.description: Activities planned in cooperation with sponsoring school districts.  Emphasis on involvement with functional responsibility for planning, developing and/or managing administrative-supervisory tasks.  Prerequisite(s): Graduate students who have completed 24 semester hours (12-15 in certification only programs) of required course work and have a minimum grade point average of 3.0 (B) and school law must have been taken. In addition, if the student is a master's level student, they must have taken research  or are taking research concurrently. Admission is subject to submission of a written plan, signed by the cooperating school district.

EDL 5814.description: Activities planned in cooperation with sponsoring school districts.  Emphasis on involvement with functional responsibility for planning, developing and/or managing administrative-supervisory tasks.   Prerequisite(s): Graduate students who have completed 24 semester hours (12-15 in certification only programs) of required course work and have a minimum grade point average of 3.0(B). Students must have participated in EL*5813 Field I. Admission is subject to submission of a written plan, signed by the cooperating school district.

metadata.full_name: Educational Leadership, Ed.D.

details.description:
The Ed.D. in Educational Leadership program is dedicated to developing the next generation of educational leaders. The program offers dedicated coursework and practicums in K-12 education, higher education, and research methods, allowing students to hone their expertise as both researchers and practitioners. For their dissertation studies, students can choose between a focus on K-12 or higher and postsecondary education.

The Ed.D. program is designed to provide a rich learning environment which supports students' growth. The doctoral program prepares educational leaders with the knowledge and skills needed to effectively lead across all sectors of education.

EDL 6001.description: Students study and analyze the dynamics of public policy as it impacts schools, school districts and communities at large.

EDL 6002.description: Addresses legal and policy issues in public education, with special emphasis on New Jersey schools.

EDL 6005.description: The history and application of organizational theory is studied particularly in the context of an educational environment. Students also study the strategies for successful organizational change.

EDL 6101.description: Addresses issues of personnel administration and collective bargaining in the context of public schools and community organizations.

EDL 6106.description: Study leadership and organizational cultures in higher education by focusing on the types of institutions, structures of administrations, forms of leadership, and processes of organizational change.

EDL 6102.description: The aspects of obtaining, managing and expending funds are studied. Particular attention is given to the concepts of adequacy, equality and equity.

EDL 6007.description: Explore the current research literature in the field of education towards developing students’ understanding of contemporary issues in educational leadership and skills in academic, doctoral-level writing.

EDL 6103.description: Develop an understanding of the principles of assessment with an emphasis on the context and format of standardized tests and standards-based alternative qualitative strategies. Systematically analyze assessment data to facilitate evidence based educational decision making.

EDL 6004.description: Apply research on educational leadership and decision making to the operation of an education community.

EDL 6104.description: Explores, analyzes and identifies strategies and skills that administrators can adopt to promote effective instructional practices in educational settings. Focuses on performance based practice and assessment for documentation of learning and achievement.

EDL 6105.description: Explores, analyzes and identifies strategies and skills that admininstrators can adopt to promote equitable learning opportunities for students with diverse educational needs. Diverse learning abilities are explored including gifted and special need students.

EDL 6003.description: Explores the relationship between school districts and community organizations and how this relationship impacts teaching and student learning.

EDL 6006.description: Study how college students can develop within, and progress through, higher and postsecondary education through an exploration of the theories of college student development and the research on academic pathways.

EDL 6302.description: Develop a deeper understanding of the principles of research methodology through the study and analysis of the latest and most current research in all areas of urban education and urban community organizations.

EDL 6330.description: The focus of this first course in Statistics will be to introduce statistical concepts and methods that would help the doctoral students to learn the language of statistics, to use appropriate statistical procedures and gain domain knowledge and analysis skills to interpret data and draw valid inferences.

EDL 6331.description: This course will introduce doctoral students to the philosophy and methods of qualitative research. Through an examination of the evolution of qualitative methods to the practice of different strategies of inquiry, students will develop the skills necessary to design and conduct qualitative research.

EDL 6340.description: The focus of this second course in Statistical Analyses is to build upon fundamental quantitative/qualitative statistical concepts and introduce new constructs, concepts and methods through the use and application of SPSS (Software Package for the Social Sciences) in order to facilitate advanced data analyses and model building.

EDL 6201.description: Students work at least 10 hours per week under the direction of a mentor in a public school district central office for a minimum of 15 weeks. Projects and activities that lead toward proficiency with the ELCC standards are completed.

EDL 6202.description: Students spend at least 10 hours per week working under the direction of a mentor in a public school district central office. Projects and activities that lead toward proficiency with the ELCC standards are completed. Required: Ed.D Candidates

EDL 6107.description: Study teaching and learning in higher education by examining and critiquing studies and theories of student learning and pedagogical practice, the structures of college curriculum, and the nature of faculty work.

EDL 7101.description: Develop an understanding and apply the principles of advanced research design in order to facilitate the development and submittal of a doctoral dissertation proposal. Action research methodology that focuses on issues in the environment will be emphasized.

EDL 7102.description: Continued development and application of the skills and knowledge learned in Dissertation Seminar (EDD 7101) in order to facilitate the completion and defense of a doctoral dissertation. Action research methodology that focuses on educational issues will be emphasized.

EDL 7103.description: Development and completion, under individual supervision, of a research dissertation. Grading credit given no credit. Required for Doctor of Education in Leadership to complete the Doctoral Dissertation.

metadata.full_name: English Writing Studies, M.A.

details.description: The Master of Arts in English Writing Studies offers both a rigorous overview of writing scholarship and flexible, programmatic choices that support students in following their own professional, practical, and creative aspirations. The program was designed for individuals who want to pursue both an in-depth exploration of writing studies as a discipline, and an individualized course of study tailored to their particular interests and employment needs.

ENG 5002.description: Provide a representative overview of composition studies approaches to research and methods. Writing Emphasis.

ENG 5020.description: Introduction to the major principles and issues related to the teaching of writing, emphasizing practical application across the disciplines. This course cannot count as graduate credit if taken for an undergraduate degree. Formerly: College Writing: Theory/Practice.

ENG 5000.description: Intensive writing of poetry. Special attention to voice, prosody, models, crafting and theory of writing poetry. Multi-media mixes. Community outreach and publishing. Writing Emphasis.

ENG 5010.description: Professional writing for the pre-professional with emphasis on expository, investigatory, or fiction writing. Writing Emphasis.

ENG 5017.description: A course on writing creative nonfiction devoted to in-depth, practical and theoretical experimentation with one or more sub-genres of creative nonfiction, such as memoir, humor and satire, self-help, travel, science, politics, spirituality and religion, or nature.

ENG 5030.description: Focused study of specific aspects of composition practice. Topics, such as the writing process, assessment, or revision, will vary each time the course is offered.  This course may be taken for credit more than once if the topics studied differ.

ENG 5031.description: Intensive examination, analysis, and application of theories explaining how people produce texts. Topics, such as social constructivism, post-process models, and current traditional approaches will vary each time the course is offered. This course may be taken for credit more than once if the topics differ.

ENG 5040.description: Study of ethnographic theory and practice within new literacies studies. Students survey ethnographic research methods and design and conduct original research within the field of new literacies.

ENG 5050.description: Theory and practice of creative writing for children and young adults with an emphasis on fiction. Students will examine and discuss representative works and contemporary issues, experiment with craft and technique, and create a series of original works. Writing Emphasis. Cannot take both ENG 4050 and ENG 5050 for credit.

ENG 5070.description: Introduction to writing center theory and practice. This course will strengthen students' strategies for writing and communication as it prepares them to work as peer tutors at a writing center.  Cannot take ENG 5070 if credit has been given for ENG 4070.  Writing Emphasis.

ENG 5071.description: Examine and critique current models for developing and administering writing centers. Students will develop administrative protocols, budgets, vision statements tutor training programs and other documents necessary for the operation and ongoing development of a writing center at institution(s) where they (expect to) work. This course may be taken for credit more than once if the topics studies differ.

ENG 5075.description: Critically engage current models for developing and administering writing programs.  Students will develop administrative protocols, curricula, vision statements, budgets, and other documents appropriate for the operation and ongoing development of writing program(s) appropriate to the grade level, students, and context of the institutions(s) where they (expect to) work.

ENG 5081.description: An overview of established and emerging forms of electronic literature including hypertext fiction, network fiction, interactive fiction, and various forms of digital poetry. Students will critically examine how composing, perceiving and interpreting electronic genres both connect to and transforms traditional conceptions of literature. Writing Emphasis. Cannot receive credit for both ENG*4081 and ENG*5081.

ENG 5085.description: Examines composition theory and practice in light of emerging technologies for writing. This course may be taken for credit more than once if topics differ. Writing Emphasis.

ENG 5090.description: Readings, discussion and writing projects focused on theory, research methods and practice in professional writing. Topics include authorship and ethical responsibilities; professional, cultural, and political dynamics; technological opportunities and challenges; and print and electronic publication.

ENG 5091.description: Examination of various theoretical and practical issues involved in collaborative writing: working in a group to create a single document. Additional focus on sharpening the professional writing skills learned in earlier courses or during time in the workforce. Includes primary research of a collaborative writing context.

ENG 5092.description: Intensive examination of and practice in the process, structure and skills for professional proposal writing that address the basics of gaining foundation, corporate and government funding. Students will complete and submit a grant proposal.

ENG 5093.description: Introduction to and practice in principles for editing technical documents including both copyediting and comprehensive editing.

ENG 5125.description: Analysis of language as a reflection of society and language use in specific social contexts. Introduction and application of research tools for language studies.

ENG 5130.description: Seminar in the structure of American English.Emphasis on the relationship between the phonological and systems as well as on the differences between spoken and written English.

ENG 5140.description: The development of English from its Indo-European roots to roots to modern times. Processes of change and attitudes toward change.

ENG 5150.description: An examination of the historical evolution of black English and its linguistic features during different periods.  An analysis of relationships between cultural context and language use in black English and those of standard English. Formerly:  Origins, Structures and Uses of Black English.

ENG 5155.description: Examination of relationships between language and power and their influence on the rhetorical construction of racial and ethnic identity in reading and writing. Writing Emphasis. Research Intensive.

ENG 5171.description: The study of meaning in context:  The application of pragmatics in a variety of contexts such as literary analysis, second and foreign language learning, professional-client interaction, dispute resolution, interpreting and gender interaction.

ENG 5698.description: Designed to bring graduate students together for the purpose of exploring significant concepts or issues in their area of graduate specialization.  These courses consist of reading, research writing and practice, culminating in the completion of a thesis evidencing competence in the field.  Enrollment open to matriculated students nearing completion of their degree only.

ENG 5699.description: Designed to bring graduate students together for the purpose of exploring significant concepts or issues in their area of graduate specialization.  This course culminates in the completion of a thesis evidencing competence in the field. Enrollment opens to matriculated students nearing completion of their degree only. Writing Emphasis.

ID 5800.description: Designed to provide timely investigation of a particular contemporary issue. As subject and content change each semester the course number also changes.

ID 5801.description: Designed to provide timely investigation of a particular contemporary issue. As subject and content change each semester the course number also changes.

metadata.full_name: Exercise Science, M.S.
metadata.coordinator:
D'Angola, Room 211
Phone: 908-737-0659
Email: pghimire@kean.edu

details.description: The Master of Science program in Exercise Science is designed to prepare students to work as professionals in corporations, wellness centers, hospitals, sports medicines centers, amateur and professional athletics, health clubs, resorts and spas, community associations such as the YMCA and YWCA, colleges, universities, school districts, and rehabilitation centers. More specifically, the program will provide students with the knowledge and skills to enable them to function as exercise specialists, technicians, strength coaches, and personal and athletic trainers. Finally, the program will provide the opportunity for individuals who are currently employed in these professional areas to enhance their ability to advance to such positions as coordinators and/or directors and head athletic trainers.

EXSC 5101.description: The students will study the application of physiological and metabolic principles to research for exercise training and rehabilitation.

EXSC 5102.description: Students will study the effects on dynamic health, as reflected in measures of physical function.

EXSC 5103.description: This course is designed to interconnect medicine, physiology, exercise, and nutrition.

EXSC 5104.description: Students will examine the principles and practices necessary for the organization and administration of effective exercise science programs.

EXSC 5105.description: The students will study the principles and practices for the prevention and rehabilitation of cardiovascular disease, pulmonary diseases, metabolic diseases and disorders, neurological diseases and disorders, and muscular skeletal diseases, disorder and injury.

EXSC 5106.description: Students will examine the advanced theories and training applications in Exercise Physiology.

EXSC 5107.description: The students will study the fundamentals of laboratory and research methods with special emphasis on its application to exercise testing.

EXSC 5108.description: Students will examine the use of exercise to prescribe, evaluate, and improve fitness and performance.

EXSC 5112.description: Students will examine the fundamental research design method techniques in the various disciplines of Exercise Science.

EXSC 5113.description: Students will examine the application of descriptive and inferential statistical methods used in Exercise Science Research.

EXSC 5114.description: Students will focus on investigating, designing implementing and evaluating a research problem in Exercise Science.

EXSC 5115.description: Students will focus on investigating, designing, implementing and evaluating a research problem in Exercise Science.

EXSC 5116.description: Engage in a minimum of 400 hours of practical field work experience under the direct supervision of a qualified fitness/wellness professional in an approved Exercise Science setting.

metadata.full_name: Fine Arts, M.A.: Studio Option

details.description: The Master of Arts in Fine Arts: Studio Option is 33-credit program designed for artists and art education professionals interested in pursuing advanced work in the studio art. The program provides a means of practicing artists to prepare for personal entrepreneurship and for art educators qualify for increased earnings. Exciting areas of Specialization include drawing, painting, printmaking, jewelry/metals, textiles, photography, ceramics, sculpture, computer art, and furniture, among others. Many school districts provide financial support for teachers who wish to pursue this advanced degree.

FA 5998.description: First part of a two course sequence that addresses issues in the studio arts and engages in critical assessment which helps prepare students for their final thesis exhibition. Enrollment is limited and open only to fully matriculated students nearing completion of their program.

FA 5999.description: Second part of a two course sequence that addresses issues in the studio arts and engages in critical assessment which helps prepare students for their final thesis exhibition. Enrollment is limited and open only to fully matriculated students nearing completion of their program.

FA 5090.description: Begin to develop, one-on-one with a faculty mentor, a thesis of creative work, which demonstrates competence in a selected studio specialization and a cohesive concept/theme.  First course in the required three semester Mentor Sequence.  Students required to purchase materials/supplies.

FA 5091.description: Continue to develop, one-on-one with a faculty mentor, a thesis of creative work, which demonstrates competence in a selected studio specialization and a cohesive concept/theme. Second course in the required three semester Mentor Sequence.  Students required to purchase materials/supplies.

FA 5092.description: Organize and install, one-on-one with a faculty mentor, a thesis of creative work, which demonstrates competence in a selected studio specialization and a cohesive concept/theme. Final course in the required three semester Mentor Sequence.  Students required to purchase materials/supplies.

metadata.full_name: Fine Arts, M.A.: Supervision Certification Option

details.description: The M.A. in Fine Arts: Supervision Certification Option is a 30-credit program designed for certified art teachers who desire additional educational background for career advancement, and certification for supervisory and administrative positions. In some cases, school districts will provide financial support for teachers wishing to pursue this graduate degree.

EDL 5606.description: Application of organizational and leadership theories to school supervision.  Emphasis on management of human resources in a professional setting, identification of personal leadership style, and major models and techniques of teacher evaluation.

EDL 5607.description:
Theory, research and practice of supervision, approaches to staff development, evaluation, and in-service training.  

Required for all Principal/Supervisor, Supervisor only, and Principal/Supervisor/School Business Administrator combined program candidates.

EDL 5613.description: Examination of philosophical and social forces that shape public school curricula.  Attention given to curriculum design, development, implementation and evaluation as well as to the master planning and integration.

EDL 5614.description: Study of the teaching and learning process. Instructional theories and models, evaluation and assessment techniques applied to the curriculum.

FA 5997.description: A leadership course for the role of the Supervisor in a school district. Emphasis is on student's engagement, via a written contract, that formalizes the intern's responsibilities for planning, developing, and/or managing art education supervisory tasks in guided field assignments in the graduate's school district.

FA 5995.description: This course explores the proposal and design of an academic document that will serve as the outline for the written graduate thesis leading toward the MA degree.

FA 5996.description: This course explores the concluding stages of conducting a research project, including the construction and writing of final sections of the graduate thesis leading toward the MA degree.

metadata.full_name: Fine Arts, M.A.: Teaching Certification Option

details.description: The M.A. in Fine Arts: Teaching Certification Option is a 41-credit academically intensive program for individuals who wish to become professional certified art educators. The Program's philosophical approach is rooted in developmental psychology and supported by contemporary research in the field. Admission to the program is competitive and by application and interview. Certain courses and fieldwork components are chronological in sequence. Therefore, all prospective applicants are urged to contact the program coordinator as soon as possible to discuss the course of studies and ensure a timely placement in required fieldwork. Our graduates do exceptionally well at obtaining teaching position in New Jersey and beyond.

FA 5904.description: Course for graduate art and art education students. Students visit multicultural settings in which art educates, informs, and contributes to individual and societal growth. Artists investigate museums, galleries, studios, art schools, art resources, and community initiatives. Art educators spend fifty hours observing urban and suburban school settings inclusive of special needs learners. Contextual factors are examined as relevant to the edTPA or currently mandated assessment tool. Identification of research topics for future thesis. Course requires journals and written papers.

FA 5015.description: Elementary art education including analytical study of the artistic process, artistic development of children, identification of national and state standards, formulation of lesson plans, teaching procedures for normative and special needs populations, applications to the edTPA or currently mandated assessment tool, implications to topical areas of thesis research. Fall semester only. Writing emphasis course.

FA 5016.description: Middle and secondary school art education including lesson planning, assessment, the edPTA, K-12 curricular studies, and classroom-based research methods. Applications to normative and special needs populations. Spring semester. Five days per week.

FA 5810.description: Psychological aspects of artistic process, aesthetics, philosophy and the social, economic and cultural relationships between art, artist, and society are explored through reading, research, discussion, and studio production. Written papers and field studies examining the implications of course concepts to differentiated aesthetic development are also required.

PSY 5110.description: Examines theories and research in physical, psychological, social development from conception to adolescence. It addresses maturational and enviromental factors as well as the multi-layered dynamic interplay of processes in the physical, cognitive, emotional, and social domains.

EDL 5613.description: Examination of philosophical and social forces that shape public school curricula.  Attention given to curriculum design, development, implementation and evaluation as well as to the master planning and integration.

FA 5018.description: This course is designed for professionals such as pre-service and in-service teachers in are education, special education, and general education who want to gain skills and strategies to work effectively with students with autism or another Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) in the classroom.

SPED 5005.description: This course examines the etiologies and characteristics of learning and behavioral disabilities as defined by IDEIA (2004). Implications for classroom practice, including the referral process for special education, and effective inclusion, are discussed in relation to historical and legal foundations. Current issues and trends are discussed.

SPED 5020.description: Theories and techniques that pertain to special education are applied to specific instructional, social, and emotional domains. Evidence-based practices for students with Autism Spectrum Disorder are covered. Current legislation, special education processes, and trends are reviewed.

FA 5902.description: Supervised field experience for graduate students. Emphasis is on lesson planning, curriculum content, and basic teaching strategies in K-5 classrooms. Students spend one full day per week for one semester in an elementary school setting, observing, teaching, engaging in school activities, and exploring the potential of the elementary classroom as a setting of research. Setting is inclusive of formative and special needs learners, and requires 100 hours of fieldwork. Taken in spring semester prior to final year of study.

FA 5903.description: Supervised field experience for graduate students.  Emphasis is on lesson planning, curriculum content, and basic teaching strategies and construction of edTPA.  Students spend one full day per week for one semester in a middle or secondary school setting to observe, teach, engage in school activities, and explore the potential of the classroom as a setting of research. Setting is inclusive of normative and special needs learners, and requires 100 hours of fieldwork are required.  Taken in fall semester prior to final semester of study.

FA 5991.description: Graduate student will teach in an art education setting under the direction of a university supervisor and cooperating teacher.  Five full days per week for entire semester.  Includes work with normative, gifted, and talented, mainstreamed, special needs, and ethnically diverse students, and classroom-based research methods.  Completion of edTPA assessment document and Professional Education Portfolio. Final spring semester of study.  Includes three on-campus seminars.

FA 5995.description: This course explores the proposal and design of an academic document that will serve as the outline for the written graduate thesis leading toward the MA degree.

FA 5996.description: This course explores the concluding stages of conducting a research project, including the construction and writing of final sections of the graduate thesis leading toward the MA degree.

metadata.full_name: Forensic Psychology, M.A.

details.description: Forensic psychology is designed for students interested in pursing doctoral training in psychology or law. Successful graduates pursue careers in areas such as juvenile/adult court clinics, correctional settings, law enforcement, or forensic psychiatric settings.

PSY 5130.description: A life-span approach to understanding the degree to which personality, cognitive abilities, and socio-emotional competencies show stability/change over time. Examination of the role of cohort, historical context, and culture in human development.

PSY 5555.description: The application of psychological knowledge and concepts to legal issues. Areas discussed are: (a) clinical-forensic applications of psychology to legal issues; and (b) legal-forensic (non-clinical) application of psychology to legal issues. Landmark cases will also be discussed.

PSY 5560.description: Experimental and non-experimental designs described as are basic statistics. Students will develop research projects.

PSY 5565.description: Continuation of PSY 5560. Advanced quantitative and qualitative research methods and techniques discussed. Emphasis on non-experimental and correlational methods including regression, partial correlation and path analysis.

PSY 5530.description: Advanced exploration of the major biopsychosocial theoretical and applied approaches to the diagnosis, treatment, and prevention of psychological disorders. Implications of current relevant research and clinical work.

PSY 5570.description: This course focuses on the major theories and classifications of criminal and aggressive behavior.

PSY 5575.description: Introduction to the foundation of cognitive and neuropsychological assessment.

PSY 5580.description: Administration, selection, scoring, and interpreting of psychological assessments and their utilization in forensic settings, with particular emphasis on objective and projective assessment of personality, behavior disorders. ADHD, and psychiatric symptoms for children, adolescents and adults.

PSY 5585.description: Examines violence, aggression, and threat assessment from multiple perspectives.

PSY 5610.description: In-depth consideration of selected major theories and findings of social behavior. Topics chosen from among: aggression, social perception and cognition, attitudes, non-verbal communication, prejudice, social influence and conformity, prosocial behavior, group dynamics, interpersonal attraction and intimate relationships.

PSY 5000.description: Perspectives on human behavior and contemporary traits, as originating in the ancestral environment.

PSY 5110.description: Examines theories and research in physical, psychological, social development from conception to adolescence. It addresses maturational and enviromental factors as well as the multi-layered dynamic interplay of processes in the physical, cognitive, emotional, and social domains.

PSY 5120.description: Current issues and research in the social psychology of adolescence. Personality and cognitive development, the peer group, and parent-youth relationships, socio-political action, the disadvantaged youth, and the role of the educational institution in adolescent development.

PSY 5130.description: A life-span approach to understanding the degree to which personality, cognitive abilities, and socio-emotional competencies show stability/change over time. Examination of the role of cohort, historical context, and culture in human development.

PSY 5170.description: Major theoretical, conceptual and methodological issues in psychological studies on the later years of the lifespan. Emphasis on the biopsychosocial model, and the normative changes in cognition, emotion, social relationships, sense of self and personality.

PSY 5420.description: Presents Theoretical and experimental evidence on which behavior modification programs, maintenance of changed behavior, and evaluations are based.

PSY 5510.description: Major theoretical investigations of personality (psychodynamic, behavioral, phenomenological, existential, psychophysical). Not open to students who have completed a basic course in personality theory.

PSY 5515.description: Current personality theory and experimental research emphasizing topics such as traits, anxiety, stress, mechanisms of defense, creativity, intelligence, and biological and cognitive influences. Emphasis on reading and evaluation of empirical work and research and clinical application.

PSY 5550.description: The etiology, syndromes, and treatment of psychopathology and behavior disorders of childhood.

PSY 5610.description: In-depth consideration of selected major theories and findings of social behavior. Topics chosen from among: aggression, social perception and cognition, attitudes, non-verbal communication, prejudice, social influence and conformity, prosocial behavior, group dynamics, interpersonal attraction and intimate relationships.

PSY 5660.description: Survey of organizational settings and psychology of work behavior related to personnel psychology; interviewing; human resource training and development; design of work; socio-technical approaches; and, motivation-productivity.

PSY 5920.description: Development and completion, under individual supervision, of a research thesis. Grading on credit granted/no credit basis.

PSY 5960.description: Study of a particular area of psychology not covered comprehensively in one of the other advanced psychology courses. Topics to be announced by department. A student may receive credit more than once for PSY 5960 if a different topic is covered each time.

COMM 5425.description: Advanced examination of the concepts of nonverbal communication including the study of space, touch, body movements, facial and vocal expression.  Emphasis on integrating theory, research, and practical knowledge of nonverbal communication.

COMM 5540.description: In-depth study and analysis of communication messages that create, build, maintain and re- solve conflict on an interpersonal or societal level.  Examination of current research.

COMM 5625.description: Communication skills to negotiate personal, community and high stake disputes.  Constructive problem solving approaches to find solutions to meet the needs of all disputants. TYPE OF COURSE: Elective (cannot receive credit if previously taken as an undergraduate course) PREREQUISITES: Admission to graduate program or permission of instructor

COMM 5635.description: Catalog Description:  Advanced examination of the nature of persuasion in human interaction and decision making. Explores various definitions, models, theories, and research in persuasion.  Examines ethical perspectives of persuasion and how to create effective persuasive messages.  Prerequisite:  Course cannot be taken if taken at the undergraduate level.  Type of course:  Elective

CJ 5600.description: This course consists of a general survey of the legal system in the United States.  It will examine and analyze the development, structure, function, and nature of the state and federal system through common and statutory law. Additionally, this course will explore the impact of social, economics and political factors on the legal system.

CJ 5650.description: This course is a study of the dimensions of stratification in society and the criminal justice system, particularly stratification on the basis of race, class and gender.  The examination will provide a framework in which contemporary social issues may be interpreted and analyzed. Through an in-depth study of the many different experiences of diverse groups, the student will gain an appreciation for how race, class and gender effect these experiences.

CJ 5690.description: The evolving changes in the criminal justice system are manifested in the country's prison system. As society demands retribution for criminal offenses, prisons become the repository of this social demand.  The need to examine the effects of sentencing enhancements on the prison population and the changing philosophies of punishment are placing an undue burden on correctional facilities across America.  The operational issues exacerbated by prison crowding, reduced state budgets, and longer sentences are the focus of this course.  An examination of the death penalty in the face of mounting public pressure to ensure the innocent are not executed is also addressed in this course.

CJ 5730.description: This course consists of a general survey of criminal law in the United States including elements of crimes, applicable defenses, and sanctions.  It will examine the development, structure, function, and nature of criminal law in the state and federal system.  The theoretical framework and application of common and statutory law will be analyzed.

metadata.full_name: Genetic Counseling, M.S.

details.description: The Master of Science in Genetic Counseling is a 65 credit terminal degree program designed to train students to become genetic counselors.

GC 5900.description: This course is first in the series of foundational classes regarding the profession of genetic counseling.  Focus is education about the profession and skills development to include basic counseling techniques, language, contracting and pedigree construction.

CED 5910.description: Introduction to Philosophy, history and development of counseling and human services, theories and methods of counseling, and current research. Focus on self-exploration and self-awareness, and a foundation for all future counseling courses.  Previous title: Introduction to Counseling and Human Service.

GC 5912.description: Advanced molecular genetics course to serve as basis for other coursework.  Course will start with study of structure and function of genes to include heredity, genetic variation, mutations and gene expression.  Population genetics to be discussed.  The later lectures of the course will focus on specific clinical laboratory techniques to provide the genetic counseling student with an understanding of current tests available to the patient.  Test discussion to include complete blood count/chemistry, hemoglobin electrophoresis, biochemical tests and molecular tests to include first generation sequencing and next-generation sequencing inclusive of whole genome sequencing and whole exome sequencing.  Newborn screening to be reviewed.  Genetic modification techniques to be discussed.

GC 5915.description: This course will introduce genetic counseling graduate students to the principles of clinical human cytogenetics including structure and function of chromosomes, chromosomal abnormalities and their clinical presentations, and cytogenetic laboratory techniques.  Topics include aneuploidy, reciprocal and Robertsonian translocations, inversions, rings, markers, complex chromosome rearrangements, deletions and duplications, uniparental disomy and imprinting.

GC 5919.description: Course to review human development from gamete to delivery.  Normal and abnormal development to be discussed leading to a review of teratology.  Genetic counseling for congenital defects and pregnancy exposures to be discussed.

GC 5961.description: This is the first course in a two-course sequence in which the concepts and methodologies of scientific research as related to genetic counseling are discussed. Topics of this course include sourcing and critical review of existing research, development of evidence-based practice, actions essential for conducting research legally and ethically including completion of the training modules on research involving human subjects sponsored by the National Institutes of Health (NIH), quantitative and qualitative research methods, applied statistics, and analysis and interpretation of data. Students will gain proficiency in statistical software. Emphasis will be placed on becoming an educated consumer of research.

GC 5901.description: This course is second in the series of foundational classes regarding the profession of genetic counseling.  Focus is on clinical knowledge and skill development in the areas of reproductive and pediatric care.

CED 5950.description: Understanding of use of self by the counselor in a helping relationship. Development of basic counseling skills. Synthesis of theories and skills in the counseling process. Practice in counseling through audio and video taping, critique and feedback.

GC 5940.description: The course starts with an examination of the molecular mechanisms by which cancer cells arise; develop into tumors; and metastasize. This knowledge will then be applied to clinical care as cancer genes; cancer genetic disorders/syndromes; single gene and panel testing; and genetic counseling for cancer will be discussed.

GC 5962.description: This course is the second in the research methods sequence.  Focus is placed on development of the actual Master of Science thesis.  American Psychological Association style of data reporting and manuscript writing is detailed.  Additional topics include research question development, study population and methodology choice, and survey and consent form construction.  Students will complete an initial literature review for the proposed investigation and submit the Institutional Review Board (IRB) application.  Communication of results via conference oral and poster presentations and written publications discussed.  Grant writing to be reviewed.

GC 5931.description: This rotation will introduce the student to various types of professional roles of the genetic counselor and related laboratory and clinical settings. Students will begin to apply skills learned via coursework to clinical settings.

GC 5971.description: Following the 2-course sequence of Research Methods in Genetic Counseling; students undertake a 3-course series to conduct original research culminating in the manuscript for and oral presentation of the Master of Science thesis. Following submission of the Institutional Review Board (IRB) application; work on the manuscript continues. Manuscript sections drafted to include the literature review; introduction; and materials and methods. Work is discussed with and reviewed by the thesis committee regularly. Upon IRB approval; actual study is initiated.

GC 5941.description: This rotation will allow students to apply the knowledge and skills attained in coursework to clinical genetic counseling settings. Students will conduct clinical work with clients under the supervision of a qualified genetic counselor.

GC 5942.description: This rotation will allow students to continue to apply the knowledge and skills attained in coursework to clinical genetic counseling settings. Students will conduct clinical work with clients under the supervision of a qualified genetic counselor.

GC 5902.description: This course is third in the series of foundational classes regarding the profession of genetic counseling.  Focus is on clinical knowledge and skill development in the areas of adult genetics, expanded roles, the various service delivery models in genetic counseling, and working with interpreters.  Advanced genetic counseling techniques discussed.

CED 5980.description: Experience doing professional counseling in a school, college, post-secondary program, community and agency setting, or closely related setting under the supervision of a cooperating, experienced, professional counselor and college supervisor.

GC 5918.description: This course is an introduction to the objectives, data and data analysis methods of bioinformatics.  Application to the clinical setting inclusive of variant classification, variant database identification, universal carrier screening, and role of the genetic counselor to be discussed.  Not a laboratory-based course.

GC 5972.description: Second in the 3-course series allowing continuation of work on the formal thesis. Administration of the study continues and data collection is conducted. Writing of the manuscript sections of the literature review; introduction; background; and materials and methods continues. Work is discussed with and reviewed by the thesis committee regularly.

GC 5943.description: This rotation will allow students to continue to apply the knowledge and skills attained in coursework to clinical genetic counseling settings. Students will conduct clinical work with clients under the supervision of a qualified genetic counselor.

GC 5944.description: This rotation will allow students to apply the knowledge and skills attained in coursework to clinical genetic counseling settings. Students will conduct clinical work with clients under the supervision of a qualified genetic counselor.

GC 5903.description: This course is the final course in the series of foundational classes regarding the profession of genetic counseling.  Focus is on professional development to include review of résumé writing; interviewing skills; negotiating; public speaking; educational material creation; student supervision; billing and coding; professional genetics organizations; board exam preparation, certification and licensure; and self-care.

GC 5930.description: This course is an examination of ethical issues in genetics with specific application to the profession of genetic counseling. The Code of Ethics of the National Society of Genetic Counselors (NSGC); Ethical, Legal and Social Implications program of the Human Genome Project; privacy laws; confidentiality; informed consent; and legal perspectives in human subject research will be reviewed. Further topics of discussion include eugenics; reproductive rights; assisted reproductive technologies; multi-parent family building and preimplantation genetic diagnosis; genetic testing through the lifespan; and legal cases in genetics including gene patenting.

GC 5970.description: This course will provide an introduction to the “bench to bedside” approach of medicine known as translational medicine.  Students will gain an understanding of how genomic, molecular, developmental, physiological, environmental and social information will allow for fuller comprehension of mechanisms of disease and allow for more precise interventions for such.  Foundational aspects of clinical pharmacology and pharmacogenomics will be studied.  Application of translational medicine to oncology will be reviewed.  Emphasis will be placed on the clinical application of research.  Multidisciplinary collaboration and the role of the genetic counselor will be discussed.

GC 5973.description: Final course in the 3-course series allowing completion of work on the formal thesis. Data collection is ended, and data analysis completed. Results and Discussion sections of the manuscript are written. Complete manuscript is reviewed, edited and accepted by the thesis committee. Completed manuscript is submitted to the Program Director. Study poster is created and presented at Kean Research Day. Study is presented at program colloquium. Study abstract is written and submitted to the National Society for Genetic Counselors for acceptance as oral or poster presentation at the fall conference.

GC 5945.description: This rotation will allow students to continue to apply the knowledge and skills attained in coursework to clinical genetic counseling settings. Students will conduct clinical work with clients under the supervision of a qualified genetic counselor.

metadata.full_name: Holocaust and Genocide Studies, M.A.

details.description: The Master of Arts in Holocaust and Genocide Studies is an interdisciplinary program exploring genocide in comparative perspective and its aftermaths. The core curriculum comprises courses on the Holocaust, the event that produced the grammar of genocide studies.

MAHG 5000.description: A historical and historiographical investigation of totalitarian Germany under Hitler, its political and cultural precursors and its violent expressions within Germany before the outbreak of World War II.

MAHG 5001.description: Examines the persecution and murder of the Jews and other victims, beginning with Germany's invasion of Poland, Western Europe, and the Soviet Union. Analyzes the incarceration of Jews in ghettos, deportations to death camps, the death marches near the end of the war, and liberation. Additional examination of victims' life and death under Nazism, resistance and its effectiveness and the interrelationships among perpetrators, bystanders and rescuers.

MAHG 5002.description: Examination of the modern development and growth of a distinctive Jewish culture, as well as significant threats to that culture, from European Jews' 18th century emancipation from ghetto isolation to the rise and virulence of racial Anti-Semitism.  Formerly: History of the Jewish People.

MAHG 5003.description: An historical analysis of articulated hatred toward Jews. The course will study examples from antiquity and classicaly Christianity through modern times. Expressions of anti-Semitism in the contemporary world, as well as Jewish reactions will be considered.

MAHG 5004.description: Representations of the Holocaust in literature and film. Students will examine works of fiction and drama, diaries, survivor testimonies, autobiographies, and films, both documentary and fictional.  Formerly:  MALS 5001.

MAHG 5041.description: A graduate-level seminar where students will explore the ways in which historians research, process, and present the ideas of holocaust and genocide studies. Students will be exposed to various methodological approaches and will be called upon to apply these concepts to a written historiographic research project.

MAHG 5011.description: Examines selected episodes of attempted or consummated genocide in colonial and post-colonial African history. Attention is given to the course of events, structures of intellectual interpre- tation, and the expressive problem of communica- ting truthfully about ultimate horror.

MAHG 5012.description: In-depth study of the genocide of native peoples of the Americas, from the invasions by the Spanish, English and other Europeans until the present day.  Uses documentary evidence including journalistic and historical accounts plus accounts by the native peoples themselves.

MAHG 5013.description: Explores the contested definitions of genocide and their applicability to Latin America. Examines genocide from the conquest to the modern age. Emphasis on National case studies, including examples such as the Tainos of the Caribbean, the Mayan of Guatemala, the Araucanians of Argentina and various indigenous communities of the Amazon such as the Yanomami. Phenomena, often wrongly identified as genocide, are addressed.  Concepts as ecocide, politicide and mass political killings will also be explored.

MAHG 5014.description: Study of the conditions of the Armenian population in the Ottoman Empire; struggle between the Empire and the Great Powers of Europe; dissolution of the Empire and the rise of the Young Turks and Pan-Turkism; genocide of the Armenian population; world reaction to the genocide, particularly that of the United States. Will explore documentary evidence, literature, journalistic accounts, and diaries.

MAHG 5015.description: Exploration of the contested notion of genocide through selected examples of mass death in Asian history. Examination of the events and their impact on perpetrators, victims and bystanders. Discussion of history and memory, commemoration, reparations and restitution, especially in context of international law and notions of universal rights.

MAHG 5016.description: Study of the condition of the Ukrainian population under Stalin, 1932-1933; historical background leading to the conflicts; the struggle between the peasants and Soviet collectivization; the struggle between Ukrainian nationalism and Stalin's regime; the resulting famine-genocide of at least 7 million Ukrainians; Western response to the genocide, effects of the genocide; recovery.  Exploration of documentary evidence, literature, journalistic accounts, and meomoirs.

MAHG 5018.description: The course surveys the system devised by international society as a reaction to the holocaust, which gave rise to the term "genocide". Five modern case studies will be considered and assessed. Formerly: Integrative Seminar: Contemporary Genocides.

ID 5800.description: Designed to provide timely investigation of a particular contemporary issue. As subject and content change each semester the course number also changes.

MAHG 5025.description: Educational tour of designated countries' sites of genocidal assault and contemporary commemoration. Emphasizes on-site visits with local officials and jurists, witnesses and survivors, journalists, curators, scholars, and other local observers who establish or revise the historical record as well as shape and contest the public's memory.

MAHG 5026.description: Hands-on experience in a human rights and social action setting. Field work under site supervision in museums, settlement agencies, research centers, non-governmental organizations, and genocide watch and human rights campaigns.

MAHG 5030.description: Explores key issues in the theory and practice of transitional justice in post-conflict and post-genocide circumstances that seek to address the wrongs of the past in the process of achieving political reconciliation and establishing the foundations of a just and stable social order. Considers criminal trial, truth commissions, political apologies, reparations, and commemorations.

MAHG 5031.description: A graduate level seminar that provides an analysis of modern day anti-Semitism Considers how contemporary anti-Semitism is similar and/or different than classical anti-Semitism. Addresses presence and persistence of anti-Semitism after the SHOAH.

SOC 5060.description: A sociological examination of proposed and implemented policies and the strategies for influencing and changing social policy as it pertains to areas related to social justice. Case studies of social policies that have perpetuated injustice, those that have been developed to achieve social justice, and efforts to affect the policy process.

SOC 5100.description: Sociological analyses of contemporary global oppression and power understood in social, cultural, economic, and political contexts. Examination of strategies for resistance and of actions for empowerment and social change.

EMSE 5342.description: This course is designed to prepare K-12 teachers in New Jersey to teach the Holocaust in accordance with the mandate of the State Legislature in 1994.  Prerequisite(s): New Jersey teacher certification.

EMSE 5343.description: Students will develop strategies for teaching prejudice reduction by examining topics such as race, ethnicity, discrimination, bullying, and preconception.  University instructors will emphasize approaches that will reduce the role of prejudice in their student's lives.

EMSE 5348.description: This seminar is designed to bring together graduate students who have previously taken EMSE 5342 and wish to pursue an in-depth examination of critical issues in Holocaust education. In addition to this examination, the course is primarily a research seminar, and participants will be engaged in independent study for the purpose of designing and implementing courses of study for their students and their districts. Students will also have the opportunity to examine scholarly works on related Holocaust issues. The seminar meetings will provide an opportunity for educators from diverse districts to network and share strategies and curricular materials that have been successfully implemented in their districts.

EMSE 5349.description: This seminar is designed to bring together graduate students who have background in teaching prejudice reduction for the purpose of designing and implementing courses of study for their students and school districts.

MAHG 5019.description: Critical consideration of the philosophical, religious, moral, and legal significance of the intentional of European Jewry by Nazi Germany and its allies. This course will examine various theological and philosophical responses to intentional murder particularly from Jewish and Christian sources, especially the impact on Judaic-Christian values.  Formerly: MAHG 5005, Philosophical, Theological, and Legal Aspects of Genocide.

MAHG 5020.description: Critical consideration of the silent Christian community, its role in facilitating the Holocaust (Shoah), and its legacy of passivity and complicity in the face of evil. The course will probe the contradiction between the ethical, religious, and theological teachings of Christianity and the hermeneutics contributing to a climate of anti-Semitism culminating in the Shoah.

MAHG 5900.description: Studies a particular area not covered comprehensively in one of the other electives. Topics to be announced by department. A student may receive credit more than once for MAHG 5900 if a different topic is covered each time.

metadata.full_name: Instruction and Curriculum, Certification: Bilingual/Bicultural Education

details.description: The Graduate Certification Program in Bilingual/Bicultural Education is designed to prepare certified teachers (P3, K-6, K -6/5-8, K-12) for work in bilingual settings. Our goal is for bilingual/ multilingual professionals to acquire a foundation in language and literacy and teaching methodologies that assist them in accommodating the needs of diverse learners from various educational, linguistic and cultural backgrounds.

TESL 5404.description: Examination of historical and cultural backgrounds, cognition, & communicative processes that impact second language learning. Emphasis is placed on emergent bilingual students in various contexts and differentiated instruction aligned with TESOL & WIDA standards.

EMSE 5410.description: A comparison of the process of acquiring language and reading skills for bilingual/bicultural students in one's native language and in a second language.

EMSE 5411.description: Analysis and modeling of innovative approaches for teaching the content areas in bilingual/multicultural programs, including integrating content areas and language learning .

EMSE 5420.description: Provides and introduction to theories, methods and materials designed to teach English language learners in a variety of settings.  This course is aligned with the College of Education Domains, NJPST, and ACEI. Formerly: IEL 5420.

metadata.full_name: Instruction and Curriculum, Certification: Teaching English as a Second or Other Language for Professional Educators

details.description: The Graduate Certification Program in Teaching English as a Second or Other Language Option is designed to prepare practicing teachers to develop an understanding of how first and second languages are learned, and to acquire strategies to assist English Language Learners in achieving proficiency in a variety of ESL/EEL settings.

TESL 5105.description: Examines the nature, origins and uses of American English through the study of phonetics, phonology, morphology, lexicon, semantics, pragmatics, the difference between spoken and written language, language variety, and language change. Meets TESOL and WIDA standards.

TESL 5404.description: Examination of historical and cultural backgrounds, cognition, & communicative processes that impact second language learning. Emphasis is placed on emergent bilingual students in various contexts and differentiated instruction aligned with TESOL & WIDA standards.

TESL 5435.description: This course reviews current theories & research in language acquisition within the context of ESL/EFL classrooms, learning language through content, TESOL & WIDA standards and L2 acquisition and assessment.

TESL 5420.description: This course examines the methodologies required to effectively teach all students, with a focus on ELLs in ESL and EFL settings. TESOL & WIDA standards-based instruction, curriculum development, and assessment will be emphasized.

TESL 5500.description: An overview of L2 learners' literacy development and their transition from basic English proficiency to success in the use of Academic English across the major content areas, e.g., LA, Math, Science, Social Studies, & Instructional Technology.

metadata.full_name: Instruction and Curriculum, M.A.: Bilingual/Bicultural Education Option

details.description: The Master of Arts in Instruction and Curriculum - Bilingual/Bicultural Education Option is designed to prepare certified teachers (P3, K-6, K-6/5-8, K-12) for work in bilingual settings. Our goal is for bilingual/multilingual professionals to acquire a foundation in language and literacy and teaching methodologies that assist them in accommodating the needs of diverse learners from various educational, linguistic and cultural backgrounds. This option requires two-semesters of thesis work that begins in the fall of a candidate’s final year.

EMSE 5401.description: Study and analysis of the objectives of bilingual-multicultural education, and the role of the teacher in a bilingual-multicultural program, as well as an examination of alternative methods, materials, and existing programs in bilingual-multicultural education.

EMSE 5403.description: Examination and analysis of the adjustments that the bilingual-bicultural child makes in the process of adapting to American schools and society.

EMSE 5404.description: This course is an in-depth study of the histories, languages, and cultural heritages of English language learners and an examination of ethnographic research methods related to the study of culture and communication.  Formerly: IEL 5404.

EMSE 5410.description: A comparison of the process of acquiring language and reading skills for bilingual/bicultural students in one's native language and in a second language.

EMSE 5411.description: Analysis and modeling of innovative approaches for teaching the content areas in bilingual/multicultural programs, including integrating content areas and language learning .

EMSE 5420.description: Provides and introduction to theories, methods and materials designed to teach English language learners in a variety of settings.  This course is aligned with the College of Education Domains, NJPST, and ACEI. Formerly: IEL 5420.

EMSE 5421.description: This course focuses on the advanced study of principles underlying language teaching methodologies, as well as testing and curriculum design utilized for academic support with Multilingual learners in various settings.

EMSE 5810.description: Individualized study of teaching based on systematic observation and analysis of actual bilingual/multicultural classroom teacher performance using established category and observation systems. Formerly: IEL 5810.

EDL 5030.description: Familiarization with the steps necessary to carry out action research and evaluation in producer and consumer aspects of research in educational settings.  Stresses issues of design, analysis, interpretation, and development of research.

EMSE 5310.description: A survey of literature for children and adolescents and its impact on learning. a cross-section of genre, thematic unit development and literature circles across cultures and curricula.  Formerly:  IEL 5310.

EMSE 5320.description: A study of (1) Implications of computer technology for education, (2) the nature and use of computer language in education and (3) the application of computers in subject matter areas, and review of research regarding educational use of computers.

PSY 5610.description: In-depth consideration of selected major theories and findings of social behavior. Topics chosen from among: aggression, social perception and cognition, attitudes, non-verbal communication, prejudice, social influence and conformity, prosocial behavior, group dynamics, interpersonal attraction and intimate relationships.

SPAN 5110.description: Evolution of the Spanish language, Middle Ages to the present day. Topics: Latin and early Romance, Arabic influence in Al-Andalus, Judeo-Spanish, spread of Castilian, modern Peninsular Spanish, Spanish in America and in the United States. Use of Web.

ENG 5105.description: An introduction to the systematic study of language and its components, phonology, morphology and syntax.

EMSE 5098.description: This advanced seminar course is the first semester of a one year course that leads to a master's degree. In advanced seminar I students develop and write a master's thesis proposal.

EMSE 5099.description: This advanced seminar course is the second semester of a one year course that leads to a master's degree. In advanced seminar II students write a master's thesis which is required to earn a master's degree.  Formerly:  Advanced Seminar II: Res in Education Practicum.

ID 5800.description: Designed to provide timely investigation of a particular contemporary issue. As subject and content change each semester the course number also changes.

metadata.full_name: Instruction and Curriculum, M.A.: Teaching English as a Second or Other Language Option

details.description: The Master of Arts in Instruction and Curriculum, Teaching English as a Second or Other Language is designed to prepare teachers by developing an understanding of how first and second languages are learned, by acquiring strategies to assist English language learners in achieving proficiency in a variety of settings, and by conducting action research related to ESL/EFL teaching and learning.

ENG 5105.description: An introduction to the systematic study of language and its components, phonology, morphology and syntax.

ENG 5122.description: Introduction to historical, social, economic, and political factors underlying the globalization of English and the rise of new "World Englishes." Examines structural and sociolinguistic differences among World Englishes, as well as explores issues such as language dominance, standardization, identity, ownership, and pedagogy. Cannot receive credit for both ENG*4122 & ENG*5122.

ENG 5130.description: Seminar in the structure of American English.Emphasis on the relationship between the phonological and systems as well as on the differences between spoken and written English.

TESL 5404.description: Examination of historical and cultural backgrounds, cognition, & communicative processes that impact second language learning. Emphasis is placed on emergent bilingual students in various contexts and differentiated instruction aligned with TESOL & WIDA standards.

TESL 5420.description: This course examines the methodologies required to effectively teach all students, with a focus on ELLs in ESL and EFL settings. TESOL & WIDA standards-based instruction, curriculum development, and assessment will be emphasized.

TESL 5435.description: This course reviews current theories & research in language acquisition within the context of ESL/EFL classrooms, learning language through content, TESOL & WIDA standards and L2 acquisition and assessment.

EDUC 5300.description: This course includes theories of learning, child/adolescent development, curriculum, assessment procedures & instruments, classroom management, diverse cultures and learners, state standards and the creation of positive classroom cultures; edTPA lesson planning and commentaries. Required for Graduate Students.

TESL 5930.description: This course focuses on the role of assessment in planning and instruction, setting realistic expectations for student performance, and meaningful assessment of ESL/EFL language performance in context.

TESL 5500.description: An overview of L2 learners' literacy development and their transition from basic English proficiency to success in the use of Academic English across the major content areas, e.g., LA, Math, Science, Social Studies, & Instructional Technology.

TESL 5810.description: This course requires candidates to complete systematic observation, instruction and assessment of learners in ESL/EFL settings under the guidance of a highly qualified professional. Candidates will complete learning segments that incorporate TESOL & WIDA standards for the edTPA assessment portfolio. For students in Graduate TESL programs.

ENG 5125.description: Analysis of language as a reflection of society and language use in specific social contexts. Introduction and application of research tools for language studies.

EMSE 5421.description: This course focuses on the advanced study of principles underlying language teaching methodologies, as well as testing and curriculum design utilized for academic support with Multilingual learners in various settings.

TESL 5098.description: This capstone course requires students to apply theoretical understandings of research and the knowledge/skills gained through their emergence as practitioner researchers in educational settings to conduct an action research project related to the teaching and learning of languages.

TESL 5099.description: This capstone course requires students to apply theoretical understanding of research and the knowledge/skills gained through their emergence as practitioner researchers in educational settings to conduct an action research project related to the teaching and learning of languages.

metadata.full_name: Instruction and Curriculum: Science and Technology-Biology Option, M.A.

details.description: Praxis Exam (Core, Biology Content Knowledge & Science Content Knowledge)

STME 5032.description: Exploration of different research methodologies in science education: quantitative, qualitative, and mixed-method design. Investigations of current research trends in the field of science education, based on current literature and rigorous research designs.

EMSE 5561.description: An introduction to the historical, philosophical, social, psychological, political, economic, legal, and cultural foundations of education. Particular emphasis on the application of these foundations for the practice of teaching.  Formerly:  IEL 5561

STME 5330.description: An investigation into science pedagogy design and science teaching methods with regards to Next Generation Science Standards, assessment strategies, laboratory investigations and technology integration. An integration with the edTPA portfolio assessment will also be utilized.

EMSE 5031.description: An independent research seminar that develops an understanding and skills in conducting action research in the classroom by planning a research proposal that incorporates the four basic phases or the action research cycle.

SPED 5024.description: This course examines the essential elements of inclusive education. Strategies to differentiate instruction in inclusive settings are explored, along with methods to foster acceptance and to address Harassment, Intimidation, and Bullying (HIB).

STME 5610.description: Development of frameworks and models to facilitate collaboration between science and technology educators and industry professionals.  Application of results to various societal scenarios.

STME 5711.description: Continuation/Expansion of Clinical Internship I; Interns complete 175 hours (approximately 2 full days per week for 15 weeks) of supervised content-specific teaching experience in a public middle or high school placement; clinical interns plan and deliver instruction, engage in authentic modes of assessment, reflect on their reaching practices, and begin work on an edTPA portfolio.

STME 5564.description: This clinical practice is a full-semester (5 days per week for 15 weeks), full-time supervised (8 visits) content-specific teaching experience in a public middle or high school, in which students plan and deliver instruction, engage in authentic modes of assessment, reflect on their teaching experiences, complete and submit an edTPA portfolio, and enlist the theories of best practices they have gained through their prior studies.

metadata.full_name: Instruction and Curriculum: Science and Technology-Mathematics Option, M.A.

details.description: Degree: B.S. Science & Technology (Mathematics Option)

STME 5700.description: Critical study of the K-12 mathematics curriculum the development and implementation of the Common Core State Standards for Mathematics, student learning in mathematics, instructional methodologies in the math classroom, international comparisons in mathematics achievement, educational technology in mathematics, issues of equality for underserved and underprivileged populations, math teacher preparation and development.

EMSE 5561.description: An introduction to the historical, philosophical, social, psychological, political, economic, legal, and cultural foundations of education. Particular emphasis on the application of these foundations for the practice of teaching.  Formerly:  IEL 5561

EMSE 5323.description: Study of the structure of number systems, patterns, Algebra, Intuitive Geometry, data analysis, and their application to creative teaching strategies in the school setting.  Emphasis on K-12 schools.

EMSE 5031.description: An independent research seminar that develops an understanding and skills in conducting action research in the classroom by planning a research proposal that incorporates the four basic phases or the action research cycle.

SPED 5024.description: This course examines the essential elements of inclusive education. Strategies to differentiate instruction in inclusive settings are explored, along with methods to foster acceptance and to address Harassment, Intimidation, and Bullying (HIB).

EMSE 5031.description: An independent research seminar that develops an understanding and skills in conducting action research in the classroom by planning a research proposal that incorporates the four basic phases or the action research cycle.

SPED 5024.description: This course examines the essential elements of inclusive education. Strategies to differentiate instruction in inclusive settings are explored, along with methods to foster acceptance and to address Harassment, Intimidation, and Bullying (HIB).

STME 5610.description: Development of frameworks and models to facilitate collaboration between science and technology educators and industry professionals.  Application of results to various societal scenarios.

STME 5711.description: Continuation/Expansion of Clinical Internship I; Interns complete 175 hours (approximately 2 full days per week for 15 weeks) of supervised content-specific teaching experience in a public middle or high school placement; clinical interns plan and deliver instruction, engage in authentic modes of assessment, reflect on their reaching practices, and begin work on an edTPA portfolio.

STME 5564.description: This clinical practice is a full-semester (5 days per week for 15 weeks), full-time supervised (8 visits) content-specific teaching experience in a public middle or high school, in which students plan and deliver instruction, engage in authentic modes of assessment, reflect on their teaching experiences, complete and submit an edTPA portfolio, and enlist the theories of best practices they have gained through their prior studies.

metadata.full_name: Leadership and Communication, Post- Baccalaureate Certificate

details.description: Designed with the working professionals in mind, this certificate program provides basic and advanced strategies and techniques to become an effective leader. It helps students develop the highest level of skills in communication and leadership.

COMM 5020.description: Advanced concepts, theories, and skills related to communication and leadership.  In-depth analysis focusing on various communication strategies and approaches for leading organiza- tions and managing individuals and teams.  Exam- ination of leadership related to decision-making, organizational problem solving and change management.

COMM 5006.description: An investigation of the intersection between organizational culture and communication. This course will cover various facets of culture that influence communication in an assortment of organizational settings.  TITLE CHANGED AS OF 10/25/07 PREV TITLE: CULTURAL COMMUNICATION WITHIN ORGANIZATIONS

COMM 5009.description: Examination of public relations theory and strate- gies for shaping organizational goals.  Emphasis on managing corporate communication campaigns.

COMM 5012.description: In-depth analysis of small group communication theory, decision making, and group dynamics. Analysis of how small groups function in multiple contexts with emphasis on improving communication. CAT.TITLE FORMERLY: SMALL GROUP DECISION MAKING AND LEADERSHIP. revised 11/21/05

COMM 5013.description: A focus on a broad study of formal and informal speaking and listening skills within business, corporations, and public and governmental agencies, including creating a professional image and corporate strategies and tactics. CAT.TITLE FORMERLY: ADVANCED PRESENTATION SKILLS AND MOTIVATIONAL PRACTICES. revised 11/21/05.

COMM 5535.description: Analysis of the stages of a crisis, development of crisis plans and dealing with the media in public and interpersonal settings.  Students will prepare and present crisis messages and press packets.  Students will learn to conduct commun- ity meetings. Prerequisite: Bachelor's degree or permission of instructor.

COMM 5540.description: In-depth study and analysis of communication messages that create, build, maintain and re- solve conflict on an interpersonal or societal level.  Examination of current research.

COMM 5590.description: An introduction to the principles, practice and importance of oral presentations within business and professional organizational organizational settings.  Presenting informative and persuasive reports and research.  Revised as of 5/26/09  Previous Title:  Business and Technical Presentations

COMM 5625.description: Communication skills to negotiate personal, community and high stake disputes.  Constructive problem solving approaches to find solutions to meet the needs of all disputants. TYPE OF COURSE: Elective (cannot receive credit if previously taken as an undergraduate course) PREREQUISITES: Admission to graduate program or permission of instructor

metadata.full_name: Learning Disabilities Teacher Consultant, Post Masterâs Certification
metadata.coordinator: Dr. Joseph A. Hogan, Ed.D., Program Coordinator

details.description: This program leads to certification as a Learning Disabilities Teacher/Consultant (LDT/C) in the state of New Jersey.
