The program is designed for the experienced teacher who wants to advance to a position on a Child Study Team. Its purpose is to prepare competent, capable, fully prepared professional Learning Disabilities Teacher/Consultants to effectively function in a variety of settings assessing, diagnosing, and remediating individuals who require special education and/or related services. The program’s design follows a cohort model in which specific competencies are presented sequentially integrating the background and experience of the professional entering the program. Students are admitted in the Fall and will follow a sequence of courses as a cohort beginning in the Fall of Year One leading to the culminating Internship experience in a PK-12 school-based setting. This program is offered in a fully remote modality with synchronous and asynchronous courses. SPED 5422 provides an optional evening residency session to provide hands-on training for clinical evaluation tools used by LDT/Cs.

SPED 5000.description: This course provides a basic knowledge of the neurological basis of educational disabilities. Students explore basic brain functioning, neurological development, and abnormal conditions that might impact learning and typical development. Students will explore current research related to brain functioning and neurological development with classroom implications.

SPED 5402.description: This course will provide students with the knowledge and skills that specialists need to understand, assess, and plan strategic literacy instruction targeted for P-12 students struggling with dyslexia and other reading disabilities. Specialized programs will be examined.

SPED 5050.description: Formal and informal assessment tools will be examined in cognition, achievement, interests, and social/emotional functioning. Statistical bases of educational assessment will be analyzed, including limitations and misuses of educational assessment for students who are racially, culturally, and linguistically diverse.

SPED 5422.description: This course will concentrate on specific standardized assessments essential for identification, evaluation, and remediation of learning disabilities. Administration, scoring, and interpretation of results will be emphasized. In addition, this course will highlight issues and problems in assessment of student learning, specifically analyzing the reliability and validity of frequently used assessments, training in non-biased evaluation techniques, and examining the over identification of students who are culturally or linguistically diverse for special education programs.

SPED 5005.description: This course examines the etiologies and characteristics of learning and behavioral disabilities as defined by IDEIA (2004). Implications for classroom practice, including the referral process for special education, and effective inclusion, are discussed in relation to historical and legal foundations. Current issues and trends are discussed.

SPED 5423.description: This course will enable students to select and utilize standardized evaluation instruments. Emphasis will be placed upon the interpretation of formal and informal testing results leading to IEP development, special education programming, and remedial instructional models. This course will also address the importance of collaboration between special education professionals.

SPED 5420.description: This course will provide students with the opportunity to critically examine learning theories and theories of motivation, as well as their applications in inclusive settings, and the “goodness of fit” for students identified for special education services. Multi-tiered Systems of Support will be highlighted.

SPED 5490.description: Students will engage in ninety (90) hours of supervised internship experiences in educational assessment, educational planning, consultation, research-based remediation of learning difficulties, and Child Study Team participation.

metadata.full_name: Licensed Professional Counseling, Non-Degree, Post Masterâs

details.description: The Post-Master’s Licensed Professional Counseling program is intended for those who hold previous master’s degrees in counseling, preferably from CACREP accredited programs, and wish to become a Licensed Professional Counselor (LPC) in New Jersey. The New Jersey Licensure Law requires a total of 60 graduate credits, including 45 credits in 8 of 9 specified competency areas. An advisor will evaluate each applicant’s competencies and course distribution in order to determine the number of additional credits required, which may be more than the 12 credits for 60 credit total. Some candidates degrees or course deficiencies may disqualify them from enrolling in this program.

CED 5987.description: This course deepens the understanding of counselors-in-training on evidenced-based, ethically responsible, and competent application of practices within a school, college, post-secondary program, clinical mental health, or closely related setting under the supervision of a cooperating experienced, professional counselor and university supervisor.

CED 5501.description: An in-depth survey of various methods,models,and procedures in assessment and treatment of alcohol and drug abuse. Theory and process of alcohol and drug abuse counseling and prevention are included.

CED 5925.description: This course examines major theories of human development including individual, systematic and contemporary theories.  Counselors learn developmentally appropriate conceptualization and interventions.

CED 5972.description: This course explores professional practice issues in clinical mental health counseling. It addresses history, roles, functions, ethical and legal issues and the trends affecting the field and practice of mental health counseling.  Required: Required of all students desiring to obtain the Clinical Mental Health degree.

CED 5979.description: This course will provide students with the basic process for making a clinical diagnosis and developing a treatment plan. Included is the identification of co-existing disorders, developing counseling strategies, making appropriate referrals, and applying prevention approaches.

CED 5989.description: This course prepares counselors to use a variety of individual and group supervision models that are appropriate for counseling settings. Class time is devoted to instruction, role-play, theoretical and supervision issues, and ethical dilemmas. Students also discuss specific situations and issues that arise in their supervisory relationships.

metadata.full_name: Marriage and Family Therapy, M.A.

details.description: Marriage and Family Therapy is a professional training program designed to prepare students for licensure as a marriage and family therapist in New Jersey. The program includes a two-term practicum and focuses on understanding the varied religious, ethnic, cultural, and national characteristics of marriage and family therapists' clientele.

PSY 5020.description: An intensive program of readings in the literature of the behavioral sciences to build interdisciplinary understanding and identify practical consequences for education. Current journals, books, and ideas, as well as classic materials will be investigated.

PSY 5040.description: Advanced study of the physiological, psychological, and social aspects of human sexual behavior, with a focus on the origin and treatment of human sexual and gender identity disorders. Prerequisite(s): Matriculation in Marriage and Family Therapy program.

PSY 5130.description: A life-span approach to understanding the degree to which personality, cognitive abilities, and socio-emotional competencies show stability/change over time. Examination of the role of cohort, historical context, and culture in human development.

PSY 5210.description: Introductory course addressing specific ethical guidelines for beginning marriage and family therapists, with attention to the broader historical, cultural and professional contexts within which helping behavior occurs. Emphasis is on developing professional identity while maintaining ethics.

PSY 5500.description: Advanced study of marriage and family therapy theories emphasizing topics such as increasing self-awareness, identifying personal issues, self compassion, mindfulness, and implementation of 'the self of the therapist’ as an effective therapeutic tool.

PSY 5530.description: Advanced exploration of the major biopsychosocial theoretical and applied approaches to the diagnosis, treatment, and prevention of psychological disorders. Implications of current relevant research and clinical work.

PSY 5611.description: An introduction to cultural competency with a focus on sociopolitical values, beliefs, and attitudes in therapy, as well as issues related to power and oppression.

PSY 5720.description: A survey of established and emerging approaches to the treatment of individuals, couples, and families using a systems-based orientation in a variety of clinical contexts. It includes the historical development of the relational/systemic perspective and contemporary conceptual directions of the field of marriage and family therapy.

PSY 5740.description: A survey of advanced counseling techniques designed to teach students about practical and theoretical concepts, theories and research, practice and methods and group processes inherent within group counseling formats (therapy, psychoeducation, and support) in outpatient and inpatient settings. Students will explore interpersonal dynamics, personal communication styles, group strategies and facilitation through class and experiential work.

PSY 5760.description: Advanced study in the theories, techniques and approaches in marriage and family therapy, with an emphasis on developing  appropriate skills, competencies, and knowledge working with diverse populations. The focus of the course is on expanding systemic thinking for treating families throughout the lifespan, with a variety of presenting problems.

PSY 5770.description: An advanced study of theoretical and applied approaches to the assessment, treatment planning, and intervention in couple therapy.

PSY 5780.description: Advanced study of various schools of family therapy, along with current trends and issues. Focus includes professional development, and emerging, integrative approaches.

PSY 5790.description: An advanced course with a systemic lens for viewing sex and sexuality within the broader contexts of the intimate relationship, family, society, culture, power, privilege, and the self-of-the-therapist.

PSY 6600.description: Examines ethical and legal issues, cultural diversity, family legislation, professional identification, community resources and professional practice. Graded on a credit granted/ no credit basis.

PSY 6610.description: Examines diversity in the family with an emphasis on power, privilege, cross-cultural dynamics, poverty, and social justice, how they relate to culture, ethnicity, gender, nationality, race, religion, sexual orientation, disability, and spirituality, as well as their relevance to individual, couple, and family clinical practice. Graded on a credit granted/no credit basis.

PSY 6620.description: Supervised practicum experience for advanced students in the marriage and family therapy program focusing on professional development, case presentation skills, group supervision, and professional ethics. One hundred and fifty clinical hours are required, fifty percent of which must be completed with couples or families. Graded on a credit granted/no credit basis.

PSY 6630.description: Supervised practicum experience for advanced students in the marriage and family therapy program focusing on professional development, case presentation skills, group supervision, and professional ethics. Fifty clinical hours are required. Graded on a credit granted/no credit basis.

PSY 6730.description: Supervised practicum experience in various settings such as schools, hospitals, clinics, and/or other agencies engaged in professional therapy services.

PSY 6750.description: An advanced study of trauma with a systemic lens. Emphasis is placed on relational trauma and evidence informed practice.

PSY 5800.description: This course is designed to facilitate students’ development of cognitive and practical counseling skills, including micro-counseling skills, initiating and maintaining a therapeutic relationship, goal setting, case conceptualization, case management, case presentation, and application of counseling theories and techniques. Students will receive individual and group supervision from faculty supervisor. Graded on a credit granted/no credit basis.

PSY 5890.description: This course is designed to advance students’ clinical skills and cultural competencies, including case conceptualization/presentation skills, case management, application of counseling theories and techniques, individual supervision, group supervision, professional development, explore self-of-therapist, and systemic diagnosis. Graded on a credit granted/no credit basis.

PSY 5910.description: Principles of research development and design to aid the student in preparing a thesis project. Types of experiments, sampling, experimental procedures, data analysis and interpretation considered. Each student is expected to make significant progress on a thesis project.

PSY 5920.description: Development and completion, under individual supervision, of a research thesis. Grading on credit granted/no credit basis.

PSY 5800.description: This course is designed to facilitate students’ development of cognitive and practical counseling skills, including micro-counseling skills, initiating and maintaining a therapeutic relationship, goal setting, case conceptualization, case management, case presentation, and application of counseling theories and techniques. Students will receive individual and group supervision from faculty supervisor. Graded on a credit granted/no credit basis.

PSY 5890.description: This course is designed to advance students’ clinical skills and cultural competencies, including case conceptualization/presentation skills, case management, application of counseling theories and techniques, individual supervision, group supervision, professional development, explore self-of-therapist, and systemic diagnosis. Graded on a credit granted/no credit basis.

PSY 5910.description: Principles of research development and design to aid the student in preparing a thesis project. Types of experiments, sampling, experimental procedures, data analysis and interpretation considered. Each student is expected to make significant progress on a thesis project.

PSY 5920.description: Development and completion, under individual supervision, of a research thesis. Grading on credit granted/no credit basis.

metadata.full_name: Marriage and Family Therapy, Professional Diploma (This program is no longer accepting students)

details.description: The Marriage and Family Therapy Program combined with the Masters of Arts in Psychology Program is a 66 credit professional training program which is structured to satisfy the educational requirements for licensure as a marriage and family therapist in New Jersey. The Professional Diploma in Marriage and Family Therapy follows the training and practice standards of the American Association of Marriage and Family Therapy (AAMFT), which is the primary professional organization for marriage and family therapists. The program includes a two-term internship that generally follows the marriage and family course work, but is largely free of other educational and professional demands. Students in the MFT program are required to complete a minimum of 500 client contact hours and 100 hours of face-to-face supervision during the course of their internship and practicum experience. Students may enter the program with either a Bachelor’s degree or Master’s degree. All students who enter without an appropriate Master’s degree will earn the MA in Psychology, Psychological Services Specialization after successful completion of the first 33 credit hours of the master’s program. Students will then advance to the professional sequence of the Marriage and Family Therapy Professional Diploma Program only after the successful completion of a progress review.

PSY 5020.description: An intensive program of readings in the literature of the behavioral sciences to build interdisciplinary understanding and identify practical consequences for education. Current journals, books, and ideas, as well as classic materials will be investigated.

PSY 5320.description: Major contemporary learning theories and their behavioral applications emphasizing use in schools.

PSY 5530.description: Advanced exploration of the major biopsychosocial theoretical and applied approaches to the diagnosis, treatment, and prevention of psychological disorders. Implications of current relevant research and clinical work.

PSY 5500.description: Advanced study of marriage and family therapy theories emphasizing topics such as increasing self-awareness, identifying personal issues, self compassion, mindfulness, and implementation of 'the self of the therapist’ as an effective therapeutic tool.

PSY 5640.description: Forces and factors which determine group interaction. Group influences on the behavior of members. The theory and analysis of group development.

PSY 5810.description: An introduction to the selective use of individual and group tests and other measures used in diagnosis and the development of a case study.

PSY 5515.description: Current personality theory and experimental research emphasizing topics such as traits, anxiety, stress, mechanisms of defense, creativity, intelligence, and biological and cognitive influences. Emphasis on reading and evaluation of empirical work and research and clinical application.

PSY 5720.description: A survey of established and emerging approaches to the treatment of individuals, couples, and families using a systems-based orientation in a variety of clinical contexts. It includes the historical development of the relational/systemic perspective and contemporary conceptual directions of the field of marriage and family therapy.

PSY 5130.description: A life-span approach to understanding the degree to which personality, cognitive abilities, and socio-emotional competencies show stability/change over time. Examination of the role of cohort, historical context, and culture in human development.

PSY 5910.description: Principles of research development and design to aid the student in preparing a thesis project. Types of experiments, sampling, experimental procedures, data analysis and interpretation considered. Each student is expected to make significant progress on a thesis project.

PSY 5040.description: Advanced study of the physiological, psychological, and social aspects of human sexual behavior, with a focus on the origin and treatment of human sexual and gender identity disorders. Prerequisite(s): Matriculation in Marriage and Family Therapy program.

PSY 5730.description: Supervised practicum experience in various settings such as schools, hospitals, clinics, and/or other agencies engaged in professional therapy services.

PSY 5740.description: A survey of advanced counseling techniques designed to teach students about practical and theoretical concepts, theories and research, practice and methods and group processes inherent within group counseling formats (therapy, psychoeducation, and support) in outpatient and inpatient settings. Students will explore interpersonal dynamics, personal communication styles, group strategies and facilitation through class and experiential work.

PSY 5760.description: Advanced study in the theories, techniques and approaches in marriage and family therapy, with an emphasis on developing  appropriate skills, competencies, and knowledge working with diverse populations. The focus of the course is on expanding systemic thinking for treating families throughout the lifespan, with a variety of presenting problems.

PSY 5780.description: Advanced study of various schools of family therapy, along with current trends and issues. Focus includes professional development, and emerging, integrative approaches.

PSY 5770.description: An advanced study of theoretical and applied approaches to the assessment, treatment planning, and intervention in couple therapy.

PSY 6600.description: Examines ethical and legal issues, cultural diversity, family legislation, professional identification, community resources and professional practice. Graded on a credit granted/ no credit basis.

PSY 6610.description: Examines diversity in the family with an emphasis on power, privilege, cross-cultural dynamics, poverty, and social justice, how they relate to culture, ethnicity, gender, nationality, race, religion, sexual orientation, disability, and spirituality, as well as their relevance to individual, couple, and family clinical practice. Graded on a credit granted/no credit basis.

PSY 6620.description: Supervised practicum experience for advanced students in the marriage and family therapy program focusing on professional development, case presentation skills, group supervision, and professional ethics. One hundred and fifty clinical hours are required, fifty percent of which must be completed with couples or families. Graded on a credit granted/no credit basis.

PSY 6700.description: A professional field expereince practicing marriage and family therapy under the supervision of a licensed mental health professional. Site approved by program coordinator. Weekly meetings with program faculty. Graded on a credit granted/no credit basis.

PSY 6710.description: A 3 credit two-term internship experience that generally follows the marriage and family therapy course work. Placement in approved agencies for a total of 225 clinical hours is required. Graded on a credit granted/no credit basis.

metadata.full_name: Master of Science in Nursing (MSN): Nursing Leadership
metadata.coordinator: Courses selected with approval of Program Coordinator

details.description: This program is designed for RN's with a bachelors degree who plan to have careers in Nursing Leadership. It provides in-depth knowledge and skills related to advanced leadership. Graduates of the option have a unique combination of knowledge and skills in nursing leadership, fiscal management and transcultural nursing to lead healthcare organizations. Students entering the program pursue part-time or full-time study (3 to 9 credits per semester).

NURS 5000.description: Examination and application of theories and frameworks from nursing, organizational science, epidemiology, and public health in advanced nursing practices. Transcultural nursing concepts will be emphasized by exploring specific subcultures within the context of health care delivery systems.

NURS 5001.description: This course will assist students in developing the writing skills necessary for graduate nursing practice, with emphasis on application of guidelines of the Publication Manual of the American Psychological Association and preparation of manuscripts for submission to peer-reviewed journals.

NURS 5601.description: This course prepares the student to utilize informatics and healthcare technologies in the management of individuals, groups and organizations for the improvement of patient outcomes. An examination of important informatics tools that are currently being utilized in healthcare to assure safe and quality care will be completed. Electronic Health Records (EHR), Telehealth, evidence-based practice (EBP) will be emphasized. Development of computer skills for healthcare applications in patient care, personnel and unit management systems will be an additional focus.

NURS 5800.description: The learner will be introduced to the economic and financial factors affecting practice in a complex healthcare environment.  Principles of healthcare economics, budgeting, leadership to assure ethical and critical decision-making and advocacy for policy changes that influence healthcare will be explored.  The impact of fiscal responsibility on global quality outcomes will be investigated.

NURS 5915.description: Synthesis of current research with identification of a problem in the area of nursing leadership or practice. There is an emphasis on the critical appraisal of qualitative and quantitative evidence that leads to the development of an Evidence-Based Practice (EBP) question using the PICO format. Knowledge, skills and attitudes required for successful implementation of an evidence-based practice project will be discussed. Concepts include patient safety and quality principles, quality/process improvement, and evidence-based practice processes.

MATH 5500.description: A course designed to give graduate students with some mathematical maturity basic skills in the use of statistics in research. The course includes the use of the computer to do statistical analysis.  Some topics are: review of descriptive statistics; correlation and regression; hypothesis testing for different types of data; distributions of data; estimation. An undergraduate course in statistics, or the equivalent.

NURS 5010.description: Analysis of nursing and organizational behavior with an emphasis on theories of leadership.  Motivation, learning, team building, quality and safety, organizational culture and staff development will be discussed within the context of nursing leadership. The theories will be discussed in relation to individual differences among staff and client populations. The clinical components of this course will emphasize the development of nursing leadership skills to include designing and implementing care in complex organizations for culturally diverse groups of patients.  Required for MSN Students

NURS 5020.description: Analysis and integration of communication theories in nursing leadership. Clinical application will focus on advanced concepts in health care leadership, conflict management; healthcare work environment, ethics, human resources, change strategies, inter-professional collaboration and organization governance. Required for MSN students.

NURS 5920.description: Emphasis of this course is on planning and developing practice changes, creating outcome measures, and identifying additional gaps in nursing knowledge. The course will address issues associated with leading practice changes to include ethics, policies, quality and safety measures, and understanding the principles of patient-centered care and population health.  Interprofessional perspectives and other resources will guide the clinical decision-making process that is integral to improving patient, population and organizational outcomes. Evidence-based practice (EBP) processes are explored, which will culminate into the creation of a ready-to-implement EBP project.  Critical appraisal of the literature developed in NURS 5900 will form the basis for the EBP project. All MSN courses must be completed prior to taking this course.

metadata.full_name: Nursing Educational Leadership, Ph.D.

details.description: This Nursing Educational Leadership is a fifty-nine credit program of study for the preparation of nurses with expertise in the professorial role. The program is comprised of twenty courses leading the graduate to be competent as a scholar, educator and clinician. It is a four-year program plan of study geared to the three perspectives of the professorial role: expert researcher, academic leader and accomplished clinician (NLN, 2006). Core values support the respect for and implementation of a curriculum addressing care, diversity, ethics, excellence, holism, integrity and patient/client-centeredness. Both the didactic and clinical components of the program foster the integration of concepts sustaining nursing's context and environment, quality, safety, relationship centered care, interdisciplinary teamwork and the multidimensional development of the nursing educational leader (NLN, 2010, AACN 2006).

NURS 6000.description: Examination of the origins and evolution of the philosophy of science and nursing knowledge. A concentration on the broad paradigms of nursing's ways of knowing and the spirit of inquiry. Investigation into the inter-relationships of theory, research, education and practice as they impact the standards of nursing care and the implementation of educational leadership. Fostered are the characteristics of a coherent and depth of the knowledge base as expressed in clinical reasoning, universal humanistic, holistic perspectives consistent with the values of a scholarly educator, excellence and professional identity.  Admission to the PhD in Nursing Educational Leadership required.

NURS 6100.description: Advanced analysis of specific concepts, theoretical formulations of nursing and other disciplines, and strategies for theory development such as conceptual mapping pertinent to a selected area of inquiry. Various interdisciplinary concepts are reviewed and compared. The techniques used in concept analysis are implemented. Content addresses concepts related to human responses and health in the general population, as well as, diverse, underserved and vulnerable populations. It also provides critique and analysis of the nursing models and theories used across a range of nursing settings in relation to existing interdisciplinary theoretical knowledge and in the spirit of inquiry. Admission to the PhD in Nursing Educational Leadership Program required.

NURS 6200.description: Traces the history of international and American nursing education with an emphasis on pre-and post-Nightingale historical events in society that influenced nursing practice and education. Emphasis on how major changes in health care systems, economics and social roles influence the demand for increasing workforce expertise and numbers. Philosophical schools of thought are examined with the prospect of predicting tomorrow's curricular needs. The integration of process is learner- and results-focused, as well as, patient/client-centered. Ensured is excellence in education through outcome measures which determine quality of the program, and its impact on safe, quality nursing services to all communities.

NURS 6210.description: Principles of the art and science of nursing curriculum development and the professorial faculty leadership role including a range of learning theories, additional educational taxonomies and critical reasoning paradigms. External and internal factors which influence the assessment of curriculum needs and the core components of curriculum development are implemented across the various levels of nursing curricula. Contemporary educational software and advanced library searches are utilized throughout. Variations in educational and practice levels are addressed as they relate to curriculum design. Curriculum and program evaluation, fiscal, legal, ethical, a broad range of client population requirements are incorporated.

NURS 6220.description: An in-depth presentation of various faculty leadership roles in educational and clinical settings. Development of the nursing professional as teacher, scholar and citizen of the academy and agency. The background and historical development of national educational accreditation organization, current standards of practice statements from major nursing education, boards of nursing and clinical associations are integrated and analyzed in detail. Implications for the use of informatics and technological resources are addressed with appropriate depth and scope. National and nursing academic organization values, structures, cultures and communications within the collegiate and agency systems are explored didactically and experientially.

NURS 6120.description: Actions essential for conducting research legally and ethically, with modules including: Introduction to the Responsible Conduct of Research from the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, the Collaborative Institutional Training Institutes (CITI), the institutional review board (IRB) training sponsored by the National Institutes for Health (NIH) and the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (IACUC) sponsored by the Laboratory Animal Training Association. Certification is granted as required compliance with Kean University policies and procedures for the protection of human and animal subjects in research. Activities with Kean University's Human Rights Institute are incorporated to promote added awareness of human rights issues and violations across the globe.

NURS 6300.description: Interdisciplinary didactic and clinical leadership project for diverse, under-served and vulnerable populations which encompasses the full scope of competencies and values of an expert nursing clinician and consumer of research. Therapeutic actions are aimed at the reduction of health disparities with a particular emphasis on health promotion, illness prevention and morbidity reduction in a safe and quality care environment. Project outcomes are based on a broad range of scientific data and incorporated as rationale for nursing education curriculum development in a professional and inter-disciplinary context.

NURS 6110.description: Examination of the types of quantitative research designs, such as descriptive, correlational, quasi-experimental and experimental, including the steps of the quantitative research process and the appropriate level of technological supports and applications. The identified phenomenon of interest is examined and evaluated using the current state of the science and interpreted using quantitative methodology in order to achieve and generate a comprehensive understanding of the concept.

PSY 6111.description: This course is an introduction to advanced statistics with application to nursing science with complex application of intensive statistics to analyze quantitative problems of interest to nursing in culturally diverse populations. Topics include descriptive statistics, probability, This course is an introduction to advanced statistics with application to nursing science with complex application of intensive statistics to analyze quantitative problems of interest to nursing in culturally diverse populations. Topics include descriptive statistics, probability, binomial normal distribution, parametric procedures, estimation and hypothesis testing, one paired and independent sample test procedures, univariate analysis of variance techniques, correlation, simple linear regression analysis, and discriminant analysis procedures. Statistical software programs will be utilized to analyze data sets.

NURS 6115.description: Examination of types of qualitative research designs such as phenomenological, grounded theory, ethnographic, and historical, including the steps of the qualitative process. It is expected that the identified phenomenon of interest is examined and evaluated using the current state of the science and interpreted using qualitative methodology in order to achieve and generate a comprehensive understanding of the phenomenon. Ethical and legal principles are fostered in the context of safe and highest quality nursing.

NURS 6130.description: Foundation in grantsmanship for research and academic initiatives. Knowledge regarding grant writing competencies, identifying potential funding sources and the preparation of the proposal, the value of interdisciplinary team initiatives and budgets are presented. A review of essential 501 (c) (3) legal and fiscal management issues and related technology including responsible budget monitoring of cost center expenditures. Access to the resources Kean University Foundation and the Kean University Office of Research and Sponsored Programs is available on a continuous basis.

NURS 6310.description: Use of technology as a constructivist tool to enhance the safe delivery and outcomes measurement of quality nursing education, research and practice. A thoughtful interweaving of subject content knowledge, technology and pedagogy is presented as central to the educator's contemporary conceptualization of teaching/learning change and innovations such as genomics. The nursing educational leader acts in terms of being a designer, or active user, of hardware and software in creative, novel and situation-specific ways to achieve quality and evidence-based learning and practice outcomes.

NURS 7210.description: Test development and measurement instruments integral to a research-based, internally consistent, valid and reliable curriculum. Assessment of the didactic and clinical educational process, qualities of effective assessment procedures, including a broad range of test designs, statistical methods, legal and ethical considerations, assembling, administration and analysis of learning outcomes. Valuing the exploration of the rigorous science of education is the central theme in the preparation of the scholarly, nurse educational leader and academician.

NURS 7220.description: Educator role transition from socialization and role-taking to formation, with particular attention to using critical reasoning for the integration of quality, safe and science-based education and care principles. Awareness of ethical, legal and research method perspectives, diverse and vulnerable population requirements, fiscal perspectives, inter-disciplinary activities and contemporary technology to engage the educator with the learners in innovative pedagogy. Settings include all program levels and care delivery agencies.

NURS 7200.description: Development of context-relevant range of curricula for nursing educational programs. Selected approaches to data-gathering for curriculum design, analysis, interpretation and synthesis of contextual data. Integration of historical and contemporary curricula which represent a range of philosophical advances, formulating course content, test creation, and measuring outcomes result in the aggregate development of innovative curricula which are appropriate for all levels and settings of professional nursing education.

NURS 8000.description: Development of initial proposal chapters including introduction and theoretical framework, review of the literature and methodology using contemporary technology under the supervision of the selected dissertation committee. Repeated registration is required until the candidates successfully completes the written proposal as determined by the dissertation committee. Upon the completion of the written proposal, a successful oral presentation is required. Successful completion of the Qualifying Examination required.

NURS 8500.description: Preparation, submission and approval of the Institutional Review Board (IRB) application. Completion of the remaining dissertation related activities including the written components of the dissertation including informed consent, data collection, data analysis, results, discussion and recommendations for future research. Repeated registration is required until the candidate successfully completes the written dissertation as determined by the dissertation committee. Upon the approval of the written dissertation, an oral defense is required; a successful defense is determined by the dissertation committee.

metadata.full_name: Nursing, M.S.N.: Community Health Nursing Option
metadata.coordinator: Courses selected with approval of Program Coordinator

details.description:
PROGRAM DESCRIPTION
This program is designed for RN’s with a bachelor’s degrees who plan to have careers in Community Health Nursing. It provides in-depth knowledge and skills related to Community Health Nursing Advanced Practice. Graduates of the option have a unique combination of knowledge and skills in nursing leadership, transcultural nursing and community health. Students entering the program pursue part-time or full-time study (3 to 9 credits per semester).

NURS 5000.description: Examination and application of theories and frameworks from nursing, organizational science, epidemiology, and public health in advanced nursing practices. Transcultural nursing concepts will be emphasized by exploring specific subcultures within the context of health care delivery systems.

NURS 5001.description: This course will assist students in developing the writing skills necessary for graduate nursing practice, with emphasis on application of guidelines of the Publication Manual of the American Psychological Association and preparation of manuscripts for submission to peer-reviewed journals.

NURS 5601.description: This course prepares the student to utilize informatics and healthcare technologies in the management of individuals, groups and organizations for the improvement of patient outcomes. An examination of important informatics tools that are currently being utilized in healthcare to assure safe and quality care will be completed. Electronic Health Records (EHR), Telehealth, evidence-based practice (EBP) will be emphasized. Development of computer skills for healthcare applications in patient care, personnel and unit management systems will be an additional focus.

NURS 5800.description: The learner will be introduced to the economic and financial factors affecting practice in a complex healthcare environment.  Principles of healthcare economics, budgeting, leadership to assure ethical and critical decision-making and advocacy for policy changes that influence healthcare will be explored.  The impact of fiscal responsibility on global quality outcomes will be investigated.

NURS 5915.description: Synthesis of current research with identification of a problem in the area of nursing leadership or practice. There is an emphasis on the critical appraisal of qualitative and quantitative evidence that leads to the development of an Evidence-Based Practice (EBP) question using the PICO format. Knowledge, skills and attitudes required for successful implementation of an evidence-based practice project will be discussed. Concepts include patient safety and quality principles, quality/process improvement, and evidence-based practice processes.

MATH 5500.description: A course designed to give graduate students with some mathematical maturity basic skills in the use of statistics in research. The course includes the use of the computer to do statistical analysis.  Some topics are: review of descriptive statistics; correlation and regression; hypothesis testing for different types of data; distributions of data; estimation. An undergraduate course in statistics, or the equivalent.

NURS 5010.description: Analysis of nursing and organizational behavior with an emphasis on theories of leadership.  Motivation, learning, team building, quality and safety, organizational culture and staff development will be discussed within the context of nursing leadership. The theories will be discussed in relation to individual differences among staff and client populations. The clinical components of this course will emphasize the development of nursing leadership skills to include designing and implementing care in complex organizations for culturally diverse groups of patients.  Required for MSN Students

NURS 5020.description: Analysis and integration of communication theories in nursing leadership. Clinical application will focus on advanced concepts in health care leadership, conflict management; healthcare work environment, ethics, human resources, change strategies, inter-professional collaboration and organization governance. Required for MSN students.

NURS 5920.description: Emphasis of this course is on planning and developing practice changes, creating outcome measures, and identifying additional gaps in nursing knowledge. The course will address issues associated with leading practice changes to include ethics, policies, quality and safety measures, and understanding the principles of patient-centered care and population health.  Interprofessional perspectives and other resources will guide the clinical decision-making process that is integral to improving patient, population and organizational outcomes. Evidence-based practice (EBP) processes are explored, which will culminate into the creation of a ready-to-implement EBP project.  Critical appraisal of the literature developed in NURS 5900 will form the basis for the EBP project. All MSN courses must be completed prior to taking this course.

NURS 5400.description: This course examines the epidemiological and nursing processes from the perspective of advanced practice nursing. Emphasis is on the synthesis of theories and models of epidemiology and nursing with special attention to nursing practice from an epidemiological view, within the context of cultural and social pluralism.

NURS 5500.description: This course explores the theoretical basis for advanced practice in Community Health Nursing (including home health care, public health and managed care) through analysis of models and theories that contribute to this unique area of the Nursing discipline. Emphasis is placed on the design of community health nursing processes that emphasize community-nursing practice that is population focused, and based on theories and models from nursing and supportive disciplines. The dialectical process includes the examination of the relationship among theory, research and practice.

metadata.full_name: Nursing-School Nursing Graduate Certificate: Instructional Track
metadata.coordinator: Program Coordinator

details.description: The School of Nursing offers a Graduate Certificate in School Nursing which reflects contemporary School Nursing practice from the Whole School, Whole Community, and Whole Child perspective. Through the integration of the National Association of School Nurses (NASN) Framework for 21st Century School Nursing Practice, students are prepared for excellence and leadership in school nursing practice. The program prepares students to apply for certification with the New Jersey Department of Education. Courses offered on Saturday only at this time.

NURS 5520.description: This course facilitates the development of school nurse competencies through exploration of the principles and trends in evidence-based school nursing practice, with a specific focus on the National Association of School Nurses (NASN) Framework for 21st Century School Nursing Practice. Includes 2 field observation experiences in special education.

NURS 5530.description: This course enables students to identify problems and plan solutions related to the organization and administration of a school health program, based on the Whole School, Whole Community, Whole Child approach and informed by the National Association of School Nurses (NASN) 21ST Century School Nursing Framework.  Specific topics include: ethics, legal mandates pertaining to school health, social determinants of health & the reduction of health disparities, evidence-based practice (EBP), quality & safety in school health, and professionalism in school nursing practice.

NURS 5540.description: This University-supervised practicum provides the student with opportunities to apply the Key Principles of the NASN's 21st Century School Nursing Framework in approved primary and secondary school settings, including opportunities to participate in the delivery of state mandated school health services.

NURS 5503.description: This course focuses on the preparation and delivery of culturally and developmentally appropriate health education curricula in the Pre K-12th grade school setting. Specific topics include methods and materials used in health education, learning assessment, classroom management techniques, student engagement, and creative lesson-planning including evaluation methods. Focus areas of the NASN's Framework for 21st Century School Nursing Practice pertaining to health education will be addressed.

NURS 5511.description: This University-supervised practicum provides the student with opportunities to apply the knowledge gained in NURS 5503, including curriculum development and methods of teaching health in the Pre K-12th grade setting. Required for School Nurse Certification Program. Successful completion of all required prerequisites and courses for the School Nurse Certification. Non-Instructional Endorsement track, formal declaration of the School Nurse Certification-Instructional Endorsement track, current CPR/AED certification, current individual malpractice insurance & clinical clearance (including flu vaccination).  Students must apply for clinical placement at least 4 weeks prior to course registration.  Course includes a total of 90 hours of clinical practice.

metadata.full_name: Nursing-School Nursing Graduate Certificate: Non-Instructional Track
metadata.coordinator: Program Coordinator

details.description: The School of Nursing offers a Graduate Certificate in School Nursing which reflects contemporary School Nursing practice from the Whole School, Whole Community, and Whole Child perspective. Through the integration of the National Association of School Nurses (NASN) Framework for 21st Century School Nursing Practice, students are prepared for excellence and leadership in school nursing practice. The program prepares students to apply for certification with the New Jersey Department of Education. Courses offered on Saturdays only at this time.

NURS 5520.description: This course facilitates the development of school nurse competencies through exploration of the principles and trends in evidence-based school nursing practice, with a specific focus on the National Association of School Nurses (NASN) Framework for 21st Century School Nursing Practice. Includes 2 field observation experiences in special education.

NURS 5530.description: This course enables students to identify problems and plan solutions related to the organization and administration of a school health program, based on the Whole School, Whole Community, Whole Child approach and informed by the National Association of School Nurses (NASN) 21ST Century School Nursing Framework.  Specific topics include: ethics, legal mandates pertaining to school health, social determinants of health & the reduction of health disparities, evidence-based practice (EBP), quality & safety in school health, and professionalism in school nursing practice.

NURS 5540.description: This University-supervised practicum provides the student with opportunities to apply the Key Principles of the NASN's 21st Century School Nursing Framework in approved primary and secondary school settings, including opportunities to participate in the delivery of state mandated school health services.

metadata.full_name: Occupational Therapy Doctorate, OTD
metadata.coordinator: Program Coordinator

details.description: The entry level Occupational Therapy Doctorate (OTD) will train students to become licensed occupational therapists. The cohort-based curriculum consists of 90 credits that are earned over nine semesters. The OTD is designed to provide entry level education in occupational therapy for individuals who have a baccalaureate degree in another field. Each student completes 9 semesters of required academic courses including a minimum of six months of supervised full-time fieldwork in approved clinical settings and a 14-week doctoral residency experience with a capstone project. Clinical fieldwork must be completed within 24 months of academic completion.

OT 6405.description: This course provides an in-depth exploration of functional anatomy and biomechanical principles underlying human movement, including the structure and function of the human body with an emphasis on the relationship to occupational performance. Students will gain the knowledge and skills necessary to assess and address movement-related dysfunctions through a biomechanical and rehabilitative frame of reference.

OT 7101.description: This course explores the history, philosophy, scope and professional responsibilities of occupational therapy practice. Official documents and professional expectations are reinforced and applied to orient all students to the profession’s expectations and to facilitate the student's professional identity and development to become competent members of intra and interprofessional teams. Each student will create and implement a plan to address individualized personal and professional responsibilities that are consistent with current accepted standards and long-term professional goals.

OT 6403.description: An overview of the theoretical foundations of the profession will be explored. Students will demonstrate understanding, application and critical analysis of frames of reference and practice models used to facilitate occupational participation. Students will also understand, apply and critically analyze theoretical frameworks used to promote health and wellbeing, and prevent disease and occupational dysfunction.

OT 6612.description: Study of the concepts and principles of group development and activity groups and their use in occupational therapy. Understand role of groups in promoting occupational performance across the lifespan. Principles of the therapeutic use of self are emphasized as students demonstrate competency in designing and implementing groups.

OT 6200.description: This course introduces the student to the doctoral process, roles and responsibilities. Students will solidify their deeper understanding of the profession's history and philosophical base in order to respond to a current societal need in a leadership role.

OT 6213.description: The study of major clinical conditions encountered by occupational therapists that may impact a person’s occupational performance and wellbeing across the lifespan. Students will examine specific conditions and the impact on health, wellness, and disability with an emphasis on safety, etiology and occupational performance.

OT 6303.description: Study of human development in connection with occupational performance from birth to senescence. Analysis and synthesis of underlying performance skills and patterns, contexts and environments, activity demands and client factors as they contribute to occupational performance and selection of intervention within occupational therapy service delivery process.

OT 6406.description: This course provides an overview of the central and peripheral neurologic systems and how they support occupational performance across the lifespan. This course provides a comprehensive overview of OT Task oriented theories of learning as well as a review of early neurologic frames of references and more contemporary models. An overview of motor control and motor learning processes with an emphasis on neuromotor recovery, frames of references and their application in professional practice will occur.

OT 6611.description: Students will participate in community-based service-learning experiences. They will apply theoretical constructs and frames of reference in the design and implementation of direct occupational therapy service interventions with community partners.

OT 6401.description: A continuation from Theoretical Guidelines to Practice I, students will continue to build their knowledge and understanding of conceptual practice models used to facilitate occupational performance. Additional screening and assessment tools used in occupational therapy practice will be introduced.

OT 6552.description: Students will learn to manage occupational therapy service provision to promote quality care in accordance with laws, regulations, state occupational therapy practice acts, and accreditation guidelines to protect consumers and meet applicable reimbursement requirements in relation to service delivery settings. This includes advocacy and risk management techniques at individual and service levels and managing the professional development activities and competency assessment task professional activities tasks using evidence-based strategies.

OT 6613.description: This travel learn course covers global health topics related to individual, group, and population-based wellness, quality of life and healthcare. The course may include lectures, field observations, service-learning, and/or cultural experiences, depending on the topic and destination. Students will participate in a collaborative interprofessional healthcare team through interprofessional education activities. Topics and destinations will vary and may include domestic or international travel for a separate fee.

OT 7208.description: This course will develop/advance/enhance the leadership skills for the doctoral level student in preparation as an emerging leader in the healthcare profession, public health arena and academia.

OT 6523.description: The concepts and methodologies of scientific research related to health and occupational therapy are introduced. Topics include research design types, methodology, applied statistics, analysis and interpretation of data, and evidence-based practice. Emphasis will be placed on becoming an educated consumer of research.

OT 7522.description: Course work will emphasize the performance of data collection and analysis using various methodologies to understand the process and importance of research for best practice. Students will design a scholarly project associated with their doctoral capstone topic that will contribute to occupational therapy’s body of knowledge. Students will learn how to create and deploy a needs assessment to gather data to support the development of their proposed doctoral residency, pending IRB approval.

OT 7923.description: Students will apply foundational knowledge gained in the first year of the occupational therapy program. Students will use clinical reasoning to select practice models and/or frames of reference to guide screening, assessment and intervention planning using the best available evidence. As part of this course, students will successfully complete a mandatory level I fieldwork community placement.

OT 7924.description: Students will apply foundational knowledge gained in the first year of the occupational therapy program. Students will use clinical reasoning to select practice models and/or frames of reference to guide screening, assessment and intervention planning using the best available evidence. As part of this course, students will successfully complete a mandatory level I fieldwork community placement.

OT 7925.description: Students will concurrently enroll in this course and a specific practice area seminar(s). Students will engage in reflection and skills-based training and processing of safety and ethical considerations as related to experiences at assigned level I fieldwork sites. Students will understand supervisory regulations among occupational therapy practitioners and non-occupational therapy practitioners.

OT 7926.description: Students will concurrently enroll in this course and a specific practice area seminar(s). Students will engage in reflection and skills-based training and processing of safety and ethical considerations as related to experiences at assigned level I fieldwork sites. Students will understand supervisory regulations among occupational therapy practitioners and non-occupational therapy practitioners.

OT 7921.description: Students will apply foundational knowledge gained in the first year of the occupational therapy program. Students will use clinical reasoning to select practice models and/or frames of reference to guide screening, assessment and intervention planning using the best available evidence. As part of this course, students will successfully complete a mandatory level I fieldwork community placement.

OT 7927.description: Students will concurrently enroll in this course and a specific practice area seminar(s). Students will engage in reflection and skills-based training and processing of safety and ethical considerations as related to experiences at assigned level I fieldwork sites. Students will understand supervisory regulations among occupational therapy practitioners and non-occupational therapy practitioners.

OT 7610.description: Provide understanding, knowledge and application of the principles and theoretical considerations governing the use of prosthetic devices, fabrication and use of orthotics devices, and the application of basic and advanced physical modalities. Major focus is to develop the clinical reasoning skills necessary to determine the type of intervention that will help support the occupational performance.

OT 7212.description: Students will synthesize data from needs assessment that was created and deployed to begin doctoral residency proposal. Students will finalize a written manuscript focused on their identified doctoral topic and disseminate scholarly results to relevant stakeholders.  Students will draft a memorandum of understanding (MOU) with identified stakeholders and supervision plan.

OT 7100.description: Students will explore the use of evidence-based technology in order to support occupational performance across the lifespan.

OT 7103.description: This course delves into holistic, complementary, and integrative health principles within occupational therapy practice for persons, groups and populations with an emphasis on community health across the lifespan. Health practices are highlighted, detailing their benefits, applications, and evidence-based effectiveness. Students will explore approaches or interventions for preventing and managing chronic conditions, as well as enhancing emotional wellness, mental health and overall well-being. Additionally, students will develop, recommend and provide interventions for persons, groups and/or populations promoting community safety, health and wellness, management of chronic condition and improving occupational performance.

OT 7962.description: This seminar is a bridge for students to apply their knowledge, skills and attitudes developed during academic preparation into settings where occupational therapy services are provided.  As students assume the role as an entry level practitioner, they will be supervised by a qualified occupational therapist over a minimum of 12 weeks. This course (the first of two) must be successfully completed within 24 months following completion of academic preparation.

OT 7963.description: This seminar is a bridge for students to apply their knowledge, skills and attitudes developed during academic preparation into settings where occupational therapy services are provided. As students assume the role as an entry level practitioner, they will be supervised by a qualified occupational therapist over a minimum of 12 weeks. This course (the second of two) must be successfully completed within 24 months following completion of academic preparation.

OT 7205.description: This course builds upon the knowledge and skills acquired in Introduction to Residency I and Residency Proposal. Students will propose and defend the framework for the implementation of the 14-week doctoral residency experience and project. Framework for the doctoral residency experience and project must have final approval by doctoral capstone manager, faculty expertise mentor, community expertise mentor(s), and community experiential site.

OT 7206.description: Under the mentorship of the community expertise mentor, faculty expertise mentor, doctoral capstone manager and community site supervisor, students will complete their doctoral residency experience demonstrating one or more advanced skills in the potential areas of clinical skills, research skills, administration, leadership, program development and evaluation, policy development, advocacy and education.

OT 7207.description: Taken concurrently with OT 7206, Doctoral Residency Experience, this course ensures completion and dissemination of an individual doctoral capstone project that relates to the doctoral capstone experience and demonstrates synthesis of in-depth knowledge in the focused area of study.

metadata.full_name: Occupational Therapy, M.S.
metadata.coordinator: Program Coordinator

details.description: The Master of Science program in Occupational Therapy is a 72 credit 2-year program designed to provide entry level education in occupational therapy for individuals who have a baccalaureate degree in another field. Thirty students are admitted into the program each September. Each student accepted into the program completes eight semesters, including six semesters of required academic courses and a minimum of six months of supervised fieldwork in an approved clinical setting. Clinical fieldwork must be completed within 24 months of academic courses. Students successfully completing the program will be eligible to take the national certification examination administered by the National Board for Certification of Occupational Therapy, Inc. (NBCOT). After successful completion of this exam, the individual will be able to identify themselves as an Occupational Therapist, Registered (OTR). All 50 states require an OT license to practice and you must pass the NBCOT exam to get a license.*

OT 5002.description: Foundations of the profession of occupational therapy: the occupational therapist's role as a professional, the domain and process of occupational therapy, occupation across generations, cultures, and the impact of occupation on health and wellness.

OT 5303.description: Study of human development in connection with occupational performance from birth to senescence. Analysis and synthesis of underlying performance skills and patterns, contexts and environments, activity demands and client factors as they contribute to occupational performance and selection of intervention within occupational therapy service delivery process.

OT 5612.description: Study of the concepts and principles of group development and activity groups and their use in occupational therapy. Understand role of groups in promoting occupational performance across the lifespan. Principles of therapeutic use of self are emphasized as students demonstrate competency in designing and implementing groups.

OT 5400.description: An overview of the theoretical foundations of the profession will be explored. Students will demonstrate knowledge and understanding of frames of reference/practice models used to facilitate occupational performance. Common screening and assessment tools used in occupational therapy will be introduced.

OT 5611.description: Students will participate in community based service learning experiences. They will apply theoretical constructs and frames of reference in the design and implementation of direct occupational therapy service interventions with community partners.

BIO 5405.description: An in-depth, regional approach to the study of gross and functional anatomy utilizing skeletons and models that depict the skeletal, muscular, vascular and nervous systems. A general overview and regional study of the viscera. Open to students seeking the Masters in Occupational Therapy. Students cannot earn credit for both BIO 5405 and BIO 3405, Basic Gross Anatomy.

OT 5520.description: The concepts and methodologies of scientific research as related to occupational therapy are introduced. Topics include research design types, methodology, applied statistics, analysis and interpretation of data, and evidence-based practice. Emphasis will be placed on becoming an educated consumer of research. Students will complete an initial literature review for proposed investigation.

BIO 5406.description: An in-depth study of anatomy and physiology of the nervous system, specifying function and related clinical conditions. Open to graduate students seeking the Masters in Occupational Therapy.

OT 6521.description: Course work will emphasize the performance of data collection and analysis using various methodology to understand the process and importance of research for best practice. Students will design a scholarly project that will contribute to occupational therapy's body of knowledge.

OT 5212.description: The study of major clinical conditions encountered by occupational therapists that may impact a person’s occupational performance and well being across the lifespan. Students will examine specific conditions and the impact on health, wellness, and disability with an emphasis on safety, etiology and occupational performance.

OT 6920.description: Students will apply foundational knowledge gained in the first year of the occupational therapy program. Students will use clinical reasoning to select practice models and/or frames of reference to guide screening, assessment and intervention planning the best available evidence.

OT 5401.description: A continuation from Theoretical Guidelines to Practice I, students will continue to build their knowledge and understanding of conceptual practice models used to facilitate occupational performance. Additional screening and assessment tools used in occupational therapy practice will be introduced.

OT 5500.description: Application of gross anatomy to the study of body movements with emphasis on understanding the functional relationship of the musculoskeletal system to occupational performance.

OT 5551.description: Students will learn about instructional design and the scholarship of teaching and learning. Students will learn the context of service delivery and factors that affect it, including social cultural, political, and economical influences. They will be exposed to management, supervisory, and administrative responsibilities that can be expected of an occupational therapist. Students will have the opportunity to practice these principles and skills with actual implementation of program development and advocacy activities.

OT 6525.description: Students will integrate knowledge from previous research courses to become proficient evaluators of research and other resources. Students will implement a scholarly project and disseminate the results in a professional manner.

OT 6923.description: Models of practice for pediatric occupational therapy as applied in the intervention process with children.  Assessment and intervention will focus on children with pediatric and/or developmental conditions affecting occupational performance areas.  Development of skills in screening, evaluation, and occupationally based intervention planning with children.  Fieldwork I is required.

OT 6921.description: Students will apply foundational knowledge gained in the first year of the occupational therapy program. Students will use clinical reasoning to select practice models and/or frames of reference to guide screening, assessment and intervention planning using the best available evidence. As part of this course, students will successfully complete a mandatory level I fieldwork community placement.

OT 6610.description: Provide understanding, knowledge and application of the principles and theoretical considerations governing the use and fabrication of orthotics, devices, and the use of therapeutic modalities. Major focus is to develop the clinical reasoning skills necessary to determine the type of intervention that will help support the occupational performance.

OT 6962.description: This seminar is a bridge for students to apply their knowledge, skills and attitudes developed during academic preparation into settings where occupational therapy services are provided.  As students assume the role as an entry level practitioner, they will be supervised by a qualified occupational therapist over a minimum of 12 weeks. This course (the first of two) must be successfully completed within 24 months following completion of academic preparation.

OT 6963.description: This seminar is a bridge for students to apply their knowledge, skills and attitudes developed during academic preparation into settings where occupational therapy services are provided.  As students assume the role as an entry level practitioner, they will be supervised by a qualified occupational therapist over a minimum of 12 weeks. This course (the second of two) must be successfully completed within 24 months following completion of academic preparation.

metadata.full_name: P-3 Modified Alternate Route, Non-Degree Certification Program
metadata.coordinator: Dr. Steven Locascio, Director

details.description: The Alternate Route program is designed for non-education Bachelors and Masters degree holders who wish to become P-3 school teachers.

EC 5000.description: Examination and evaluation of philosophical, historical, psychological, and social foundations of early childhood and family studies and their implications for policy and practice.  Formerly: EC 5000 Modern Trends in EC

EC 5100.description: In-depth study of language development and reading as a cognitive and effective process. Current research and strategies for developing language arts/reading programs for young children.

EC 5111.description: Students explore the policies and procedures for identifying children with special needs and understanding the laws relevant to teaching this group of children (Disabilities Act Amendments). Students learn how to appropriately assess student learning and implement differentiated instruction to address all learning needs in the classroom.

EC 5222.description: Students learn about various developmentally, linguistically, and culturally appropriate forms of assessment to promote learning and development in young children. Further examination focuses on what these forms of assessment are, why they are important, and how to apply them effectively.

EC 5230.description: Students will acquire an understanding of major theories related to child development in the contexts of the child and the family, and their applications to program planning and activities for young children and their families.

EC 5275.description: Students will develop skills and strategies for planning and teaching integrated curriculum for young children.

EC 5320.description: Using an ecological system perspective, this course prepares professionals in early childhood settings, birth through age 8, to design and implement partnership program with diverse family groups.

EC 5500.description: Explore developmentally appropriate use of technology by young children in early childhood settings as well as examine the application of digital tools for use in research and action research. Emphasize computer, Internet and other digital technologies. Elective all other graduate students. Formerly: Innovative Computer Applications for Primary Grade Teachers

metadata.full_name: Physical Therapy, D.P.T.

details.description: The professional preparation Doctor of Physical Therapy (DPT) program at Kean University follows a hybrid curricular model of planned and advanced learning experiences including problem-based learning, team-based learning, systems based approach, clinical grand rounds, and online course delivery. The program includes a total of 42 weeks full-time and 240 hours of integrated clinical experiences that align with clinical and didactic coursework.

DPT 6003.description: This course utilizes a Team-Based Learning (TBL), regional approach to the in-depth examination of gross human anatomic structure and function, emphasizing the musculoskeletal and neurological systems. The TBL portion of this course will accompany laboratory content, which involves regional cadaveric dissection under the instruction of course instructors, as well as surface palpation activities. An emphasis on clinical application of basic anatomical foundations will be emphasized throughout the course.

DPT 6010.description: The overall goal of this course is to provide the foundational basis for understanding the body's physiological systems and systematic responses to physical activity. This course provides an in-depth study of the major physiological systems controlling human body function; normal and pathological function. Emphasis is placed upon the interdependency and relationships existing between systems, and their impact on human function. Clinical application of physiological systemic responses to various factors (including exercise and rehabilitation) will be threaded throughout.

DPT 6100.description: This course is designed to prepare students with the foundational knowledge and skills necessary to enter a clinical practice environment. Student will be introduced to the history and development of physical therapy profession, the American Physical Therapy Association (APTA) and vision for the future of PT. Also includes an overview of health care system and delivery models, and HIPPA. Clinical skills include examination components, patient history, establishing patient rapport, ergonomics, guarding techniques, goniometry, clinical safety, and medical terminology.

DPT 6110.description: An integrated approach to the study of all relevant physiologic, anatomic, pathologic, medical and therapeutic concepts related to musculoskeletal physical therapy practice. The course focuses upon the structures within the upper extremity and cervical spine regions. It includes the physical therapy diagnostic process including examination, differential diagnosis, physical therapeutic interventions, patient care program development, and outcomes assessment. Experiences related to psychological, social, cultural, economic, and vocational aspects of illness and disability are included. The course offers laboratory learning experiences supplemented with problem-based learning, organized around the study of the musculoskeletal system, with an orientation toward critical thinking and clinical decision making. Students will also be exposed to clinical experiences in DPT 6600, which are correlated with this course.

DPT 6020.description: This course serves as an introduction to the structure and function of the human neurological system, with special emphasis on topics most relevant to rehabilitation. The course does contain laboratory sessions, and covered topics include: neuroanatomy and neurophysiology of the central and peripheral nervous systems, vascular brain anatomy, neural control of human systems, neuromuscular control, spinal reflexes, learning and memory, basic principles of motor learning and control, special senses, and basic principles of neuropsychology.

DPT 6120.description: This course is designed to provide an introduction to neuromusculoskeletal factors related to human movement with a primary focus on the role of the musculoskeletal system. Students will revisit basic principles of physics and apply them to the anatomical system in the study of biomechanics. This course will include a detailed study of osteokinematics, arthrokinematics, and biomechanics of human tissue and joints, as well as basic information on the neuroregulation of movement. Emphasis will be placed on gaining a clinical perspective of the relationship between structure and function at individual joints. Didactic lectures will describe motion by anatomic regions and be followed by laboratory experiences of the same region that relate mechanical concepts to a direct experience of the body in motion. Problem based exercises will be used to facilitate application of the foundational content in clinical contexts. Upon completion of the course, students will be expected to be able to describe the mechanics of human motion at each individual joint and be able to describe all neuromusculoskeletal factors that contribute to the creation of human movement.

DPT 6200.description: The historical, present-day and future directions of the physical therapy profession are explored, as well as the role of the American Physical Therapy Association (APTA), state and federal regulations, and other regulatory agencies in influencing professional practice.
