Thus, students will build on their advanced generalist practice knowledge to advocate for social and economic justice issues from a systemic and global perspective. Finally, students will have the opportunity to develop a self-care plan in the development of their professional use of self.

SW 5403.description: This survey course covers the theoreticalfoundations of Human Behavior and the Social Environment. Human development is explored within the context of the life cycle with emphasis on environmental determinants of behavior, diversity, oppression and discrimination. The course addresses individuals, families, groups, organizations, communities, and social institutions as well as the impact of social and economic forces on individual and social systems. These issues will be explored within the context of race, culture, ethnicity, gender, age, socioeconomic status, sexual orientation and levels of ability. Value and ethical issues in society and in social work will be addressed throughout.

SW 5103.description: An introduction for students in the Advanced Standing Program to the Generalist/Advanced Generalist Approach to social work practice. Focus on the four social work roles of enabler, broker, advocate and coordinator and the skills of working with systems of different types and sizes. development and application of knowledge of ethnicity, race, gender, age, socioeconomic status and sexual orientation in planning interventions.

SW 6703.description: Analysis of contemporary family issues, such as child abuse and neglect, teen pregnancy, school dropouts, domestic violence, substance abuse and HIV/AIDS. Study of varied family structures, and systematic interrelationships. Influence of race, ethnicity, class, gender, sexual orientation and disability on family functioning. Application of strengths perspective. Focus on Local and global issues. Advanced generalist practice course, to be taken only by second year MSW students.

SW 6704.description: Explication of similarities and differences in needs and issues facing the elderly nationally and internationally. An analysis of legislative responses and their impact on service delivery. Emphasis on the importance of social work advocacy.

SW 6706.description: A study of issues related to palliative and end-of-life care. Discussion of legal, ethical, medical and social issues for professionals to enhance provision of competent care for people with severe chronic and life threatening illnesses. The expectation is that students will enter the course with several practice behaviors in place: demonstrating professional demeanor and attending to professional roles and boundaries; tolerating ambiguity in and applying strategies of ethical reasoning; demonstrating effective oral and written communication; being culturally proficient; responding to oppression and discrimination, advocating for human rights and engaging in practices that advance and advocate for social and economic justice; applying research skills to practice; and critiquing and applying knowledge to understand person and environment.

SW 6801.description: This is an advanced MSW elective clinical practice course that will develop students' professional practice behaviors in trauma, grief, loss, and survival with individuals, families, groups, organizations, and communities. This course concentrates on the etiology and treatment of traumatic symptomatology and will explore conditions that contribute to the intergenerational, sociocultural, and societal impact of trauma. The course will orient students to the assessment of traumatic symptoms, as well as to some generally applicable treatment approaches, and to explore research related to the psychobiology of trauma.  Students will be expected to demonstrate evidence-based knowledge and skills for identifying and assessing survivors of domestic violence, physical and sexual abuse, school violence, gangs, victims of natural disasters, violent crimes, human rights violations, combat veterans, and terrorism. Students will gain an appreciation and understanding of the values and ethical dilemmas inherent in working with trauma survivors in such areas of immigration, refugees, and human trafficking. Finally, students will have the opportunity to develop a self-care plan to develop a self-care plan in the development of their professional use of self.

SW 6802.description: This is a specialized elective designed to help students develop an understanding of substance use disorders from an ecosystems perspective. Students will examine the theoretical and practical implications regarding the causes of and treatment for addictions.

metadata.full_name: Social Work, M.S.W. - Extended Option

details.description: *An extended three-year full-time program is offered to students who are eligible for admission into the full-time two-year program. This option is specifically designed for those students who either must work or who have family responsibilities that make it impossible for them to complete 15 credits of coursework per semester. However, in the second year of study, students will have to make adjustments to their work schedule to accommodate the 16 hours of field experience (2 full-days per week) in the 2 year and the 20 hours of field experience( 2-3 days per week) in their third and last year of study. The MSW program is willing to adjust student credit hours in order to accommodate circumstances, however it is the responsibility of students to assure they have completed all degree requirements.

Incorporating a systems/ecological framework to problem solving the focus of this course is on understanding the inter-connectivity between systems and the selective application of common skills and knowledge for intervention at the mezzo and macro-levels.

SW 5202.description: The second semester field practicum experience at foundation level of curriculum. Field practicum is designed to provide an opportunity to apply knowledge, skills, values, and cognitive and affective processes taught in the classroom to a practice setting under the direction of a field instructor.

SW 5502.description: This course is a continuation of Integrative Seminar I and allows students the opportunity to integrate foundation social work practice concepts with fieldwork practicum experiences. The course also emphasizes skill building and application of the social work competencies across dimensions of knowledge, values, skills, cognitive and affective processes.

SW 6101.description: This specialization clinical course builds on competencies from the first-year practice courses. The course will provide an understanding the role of clinician and focus on the therapeutic relationships as the framework for helping and developing interviewing and assessment skills appropriate with challenging clients and supported by empirical research. The course will help to develop competencies in assessment and treatment principles from the interpersonal, psychodynamic, and cognitive-behavioral approaches including motivational interviewing skills to engage involuntary and hard-to-reach clients. Staying cognizant of complex ethical and value issues, impact of diversity, different system sizes, settings and populations is relevant to advanced clinical practice, and complements the mission, goals, and objectives of the MSW program.

SW 6203.description: First advanced field practice course aimed at the application and integration of a clinical direct/practice approach through a placement in a social service setting. Under the supervision of an experienced MSW, students will concentrate on the social work roles of psychosocial therapist and supervisor and the strategies related to those functions.

SW 6701.description: This practice course focuses on a biopsychosocial understanding of mental wellbeing and mental illness from a social work and social environment perspective. It gives special attention to the social work view in relation to the person in in environment and other classification systems available to the person in environment and other classification systems available to the practitioner. The course emphasizes an appreciation of the critical influence of culture, class, race and ethnicity, religion, and social values of the individual, family, group and social institutions in the assessment of client strengths and vulnerabilities. This course critically reviews current classification systems and major theories regarding the nature of mental disorders, their diagnoses and etiologies, and the treatment approaches available to help people in their discovery.

SW 6102.description: This course will concentrate on evidence based clinical social work practice for working with groups, families, and individuals. It will build on the knowledge, skills and values of engagement and intervention from the first semester. Focus is on advanced social work, clinical and client advocacy skills, techniques at each stage of the helping process, and with difficult practice situations. The course will provide students with the skills to integrate knowledge from other courses including" psychopathology; research; policy; human behavior; and the MSW program's commitment to culturally proficient social work practice.

SW 6204.description: Final advanced field practice course. Application and integration of the advanced clinical approach. Under the supervision of an experienced MSW, placement in a clinical social service setting concentrating on the social work roles of counselor and advocate and strategies related to those functions. Continued use of the enabler, broker, advocate, coordinator, psycho-social therapist, and supervisor roles.

SW 6703.description: Analysis of contemporary family issues, such as child abuse and neglect, teen pregnancy, school dropouts, domestic violence, substance abuse and HIV/AIDS. Study of varied family structures, and systematic interrelationships. Influence of race, ethnicity, class, gender, sexual orientation and disability on family functioning. Application of strengths perspective. Focus on Local and global issues. Advanced generalist practice course, to be taken only by second year MSW students.

SW 6704.description: Explication of similarities and differences in needs and issues facing the elderly nationally and internationally. An analysis of legislative responses and their impact on service delivery. Emphasis on the importance of social work advocacy.

SW 6802.description: This is a specialized elective designed to help students develop an understanding of substance use disorders from an ecosystems perspective. Students will examine the theoretical and practical implications regarding the causes of and treatment for addictions.

metadata.full_name: Special Education, M.A.: Applied Behavior Analysis and Autism Spectrum Disorders Option
metadata.coordinator:
Dr. Randi A. Sarokoff, BCBA-D
Office: Hennings Hall, Room 310
Phone: 908-737-3849
Email: rsarokof@kean.edu

details.description: This Master’s in Applied Behavior Analysis and Autism Spectrum Disorders is rooted in the science of applied behavior analysis (ABA). The master’s program is designed for those who are not certified teachers (although teachers are welcome) and would like to pursue becoming a Board-Certified Behavior Analyst (BCBA®). At Kean University, you will develop an evidenced-based approach to teaching students with autism spectrum disorder (ASD) and other developmental disabilities. Master’s students will be required to conduct a master’s thesis.

SPED 7900.description: Course includes, but is not limited to, the study of single-subject research design in applied behavior analysis. Evaluation of behavior interventions using systematic manipulation of independent variables to analyze treatment outcomes. Interpreting graph data, and examining internal, social and external validities.  *Course content for those taking the BACB 5th Edition exam , 6th edition exam, and those taking the BCBA exam after 2027. *As indicated in the course content section, this course is consistent with BACB Fifth Edition and Sixth Edition emphasizes Content Area C-Measurement, Data Display, and Interpretation and D- Experimental Design.

SPED 7906.description: Candidates will complete a literature review of a thesis topic using the current edition of APA and the accompanying Title Page, Introduction, References section and partial Appendix of a Professional APA Paper. Research will be compliant with Human Subject Research Guidelines. Only students with a goal of publishing survey research or implementing single-subject intervention research will be required to submit a proposal to IRB.

SPED 7907.description: In this second course of a three-semester experience, students will design and plan the implementation of a single-subject research design or survey project, resulting in a current APA style manuscript of publication-quality Research proposals, developed under the supervision of the professor, must be submitted to Kean University IRB and granted approval, before any data can be collected.

SPED 7908.description: In this last course of a three-semester experience, students will complete the implementation of a single-subject research design or behavior-analytic survey project, analyze results, discuss implications and identify ideas for subsequent projects.

SPED 5100.description: Course content includes, but is not limited to, the basic concepts and principles of operant and respondent conditioning. *As indicated in the course content section, this course is consistent with BACB Sixth Edition Task List and emphasizes Content Areas, B - Concepts and Principles

SPED 5101.description: Course content includes, but is not limited to, the selection and implementation of behavior assessment techniques and approaches from applied behavior analysis (ABA) and is in alignment with the BACB sixth edition.

SPED 5102.description: Course content includes, but is not limited to, behavior assessment, selection and implementation of behavior intervention techniques, and generalization and maintenance of behavior change, from the applied behavior analysis research in alignment with the BACB sixth edition course contents.

SPED 5103.description: Course content includes, but is not limited to, performance analysis and management, supervision, staff training, behavioral systems analysis, organizational culture, and leadership aligned with the BACB 6th ed. Task List  *As indicated in the course content section, this course is consistent with BACB Sixth Edition Task List and emphasizes Content Areas, G-Behavior-Change Procedures and I-Personnel Supervision and Management.

SPED 5200.description: Course content includes, but is not limited to, the underlying theoretical and philosophical foundations of behavior analysis. *This course is for students sitting for the BACB certification exam after January 2027.

SPED 7903.description: Students will gain an in-depth understanding of ethical and professional standards of conduct for behavior analysts based on the BACB Professional and Ethical Compliance Code for Behavior Analysts.*  *This course meets the BACB Professional and Ethical Compliance Code for Behavior Analysts (for those taking the BACB 5th Edition exam, 6th edition exam, and those taking the BCBA exam after 2027.) *As indicated in the course content section, this course is consistent with BACB Fifth Edition and emphasizes Content Area E-Ethics; and with BACB Sixth Edition and Emphasizes Content Area E-Ethical and Professional Issues.

SPED 5005.description: This course examines the etiologies and characteristics of learning and behavioral disabilities as defined by IDEIA (2004). Implications for classroom practice, including the referral process for special education, and effective inclusion, are discussed in relation to historical and legal foundations. Current issues and trends are discussed.

SPED 5020.description: Theories and techniques that pertain to special education are applied to specific instructional, social, and emotional domains. Evidence-based practices for students with Autism Spectrum Disorder are covered. Current legislation, special education processes, and trends are reviewed.

SPED 5023.description: Through hands-on learning, students will learn about various software applications and website accessibility options, and become familiar with adaptive technologies. Students will learn to integrate technology into their curriculum while meeting the needs of individuals with disabilities.

metadata.full_name: Special Education, M.A.: Learning and Behavioral Disabilities Option
metadata.coordinator: Dr. Joseph A. Hogan, Ed.D., Program Coordinator

details.description: The Master of Arts in Special Education: Learning and Behavioral Disabilities provides students with an in-depth background to work with students who have learning, emotional, behavioral and/or developmental disabilities. Students learn about the physiological causes of learning disabilities, enabling them to gain a more holistic perspective of their students. Students will be exposed to various instructional approaches, assessments, and practices to better serve students with disabilities. Coursework actively involves students in critical thinking activities, in which they examine and analyze theories and practices for teaching students in this domain, with a strong emphasis on best practices for inclusive education. Students may apply to receive New Jersey Certification as a Teacher of Students with Disabilities after the completion of the required 21 credits. The program is offered in a fully online modality.

SPED 5000.description: This course provides a basic knowledge of the neurological basis of educational disabilities. Students explore basic brain functioning, neurological development, and abnormal conditions that might impact learning and typical development. Students will explore current research related to brain functioning and neurological development with classroom implications.

SPED 5005.description: This course examines the etiologies and characteristics of learning and behavioral disabilities as defined by IDEIA (2004). Implications for classroom practice, including the referral process for special education, and effective inclusion, are discussed in relation to historical and legal foundations. Current issues and trends are discussed.

SPED 5020.description: Theories and techniques that pertain to special education are applied to specific instructional, social, and emotional domains. Evidence-based practices for students with Autism Spectrum Disorder are covered. Current legislation, special education processes, and trends are reviewed.

SPED 5023.description: Through hands-on learning, students will learn about various software applications and website accessibility options, and become familiar with adaptive technologies. Students will learn to integrate technology into their curriculum while meeting the needs of individuals with disabilities.

SPED 5024.description: This course examines the essential elements of inclusive education. Strategies to differentiate instruction in inclusive settings are explored, along with methods to foster acceptance and to address Harassment, Intimidation, and Bullying (HIB).

SPED 5050.description: Formal and informal assessment tools will be examined in cognition, achievement, interests, and social/emotional functioning. Statistical bases of educational assessment will be analyzed, including limitations and misuses of educational assessment for students who are racially, culturally, and linguistically diverse.

SPED 5068.description: Legal and historical influences that impact the transition process are covered. Agency and program factors are considered. Skills needed to successfully plan for transition and lifelong supports for students with disabilities are explored.

SPED 5071.description: This course will explore special issues and approaches to teach literacy to students with an emphasis placed upon students with special needs. Theory, research, and practice grounded in the science of reading are explored in the field of literacy.

SPED 5080.description: This course examines practices for inclusive education in curriculum development and school-wide programs, including RtI, MTSS, and UDL. Emphasis is also placed on best practices for co-teaching and developing effective and meaningful IEPs.  *Practicum hours required (10 hours) in observing co-teaching and participating in IEP development and IEP meetings.

SPED 5410.description: Theories and practices in psychoeducational diagnosis and remediation of learning and behavioral disabilities are covered. The areas of perception, cognition, literacy and motivation are explored in relation to academics, behavior and classroom performance.

SPED 5810.description: In this capstone course, students will be introduced to understanding and conducting research in education. Students will conduct a literature review and action research project in a classroom setting.

SPED 5005.description: This course examines the etiologies and characteristics of learning and behavioral disabilities as defined by IDEIA (2004). Implications for classroom practice, including the referral process for special education, and effective inclusion, are discussed in relation to historical and legal foundations. Current issues and trends are discussed.

SPED 5810.description: In this capstone course, students will be introduced to understanding and conducting research in education. Students will conduct a literature review and action research project in a classroom setting.

metadata.full_name: Speech-Language Pathology Doctorate, SLPD

details.description: The clinical doctoral program is designed for post-masters, ASHA certified speech-language pathologists. The objective of the program is to prepare advanced practitioners and future university clinical faculty in Communication Sciences and Disorders through provision of an affordable, rigorous, high quality clinical doctoral program. The program emphasizes the social and cultural aspects of communication sciences and disorders as it applies to both allied health care and educational services to under-served populations. The clinical doctorate will deepen the knowledge, leadership and problem-solving skills of current practitioners with a focus on coursework and applications that incorporate current research, ethical decision-making and models of best practice. The curriculum will provide primary and secondary concentrations of study and an applied research experience that enhances the knowledge and skills required in advanced clinical practice and administration/leadership in educational, medical, rehabilitation and other clinical settings (e.g., coursework, directed teaching, clinical research, etc.).

CDD 6298.description: This first seminar in the CDD doctoral program will provide a solid background in research design and methodology in Communication Sciences and Disorders. Students will identify the most effective designs, both quantitative and qualitative, in research literature in communication disorders and related fields and learn to critically evaluate methodologies employed in current behavioral research.

CDD 7110.description: Statistical methods commonly used in clinical research, including sampling, levels of measurement, probability, sensitivity/specificity, and Type I and Type II errors and inferential statistical tests including chi-square, independent and dependent samples t-tests, linear regression, and analysis of variance, non-parametric and qualitative researched are explored.

CDD 7000.description: Seminar addressing current evidence-based practices in a chosen topical area. Students will identify a major topical area and work with an advisory committee to generate a series of readings that will deepen each student's knowledge of current evidence-based research and best practices in the topical area.

CDD 7001.description: Evidence-based practice research and application course. Students will identify three clients who have communication disorders related to the designated major topic area. Based on the needs of the clients, an evidence-based intervention plan will be implemented and evaluated.

CDD 7002.description: Seminar on developing professional training programs in CSD. The course will focus on adult learning theory and teaching strategies as they relate to the dissemination of evidenced-based practices in the major topical area.

CDD 7004.description: Seminar addressing current evidence-based practices in a chosen topical area. Students will identify a minor topical area and work with an advisory committee to generate a series of readings that will deepen each student's knowledge of current evidence-based research and best practices in the topical area.

CDD 7005.description: Evidence-based practice research and application course. Students will identify one client with a communication disorder related to the designated minor topic area. Based on the needs of the client, an evidence-based intervention plan will be implemented and evaluated.

CDD 8000.description: A demonstration of evidence-based interventions. Student will identify an intervention area and implement multiple, evidence-based assessment/interventions with an appropriate client population.

CDD 8001.description: This seminar addresses the complex contexts and current evidence-based approaches to effective supervision in speech-language pathology. Following a review of the literature, the course will examine the competencies required for effective supervision and provides practical hands-on strategies for implementing the supervisory process.

CDD 8002.description: A case-based interprofessional seminar examining quality care as the product of individual professional skills, teamwork skills, and effective systems/processes of of care.

metadata.full_name: Speech-Language Pathology, M.A.

details.description: This advanced study program is designed to prepare students for professional positions as Speech-Language Pathologists in hospitals, institutional, and school settings. They will be able to identify, assess and enhance the communicative skills of individuals with a variety of communication disorders in a variety of settings. These disorders include articulation, language, voice, fluency, cognition and swallowing. The program is accredited by the Council on Accreditation in Audiology and Speech-Language Pathology(CAA) of the American Speech-Language-Hearing Association (ASHA) and meets the academic and practicum requirements of the New Jersey Licensing Board in Speech-Language Pathology/Audiology as well as Speech-Language Specialist certification issued by the New Jersey Department of Education. Students holding degrees in disciplines other than Speech Pathology may apply to the program and, if admitted, will be required to successfully complete the following prescribed undergraduate coursework prior to enrolling in graduate classes: Introduction to Communication Disorders; Phonetics; Anatomy and Physiology of the Ear and Speech Mechanism; Language Development; Disorders of Speech Production and Voice; Audiology; Speech Science.

CDD 5202.description: Introduction to therapeutic methodologies in preparation for participation in on-site clinical practicum.

CDD 5227.description: Provides the first supervised clinical experience in diagnosing and treating clients with communication disorders at the Kean University Center for Communication Disorders in order to provide the training necessary to meet state and ASHA certification /license requirements. Students must earn a grade of B or better to continue through the clinical sequence. This course may be repeated a second time in order to meet clinical competencies.

CDD 5228.description: This course provides the second supervised clinical experience in diagnosing and treating clients with communication disorders at the Kean University Center for Communication Disorders in order to provide the training necessary to meet state and ASHA certification /license requirements. Students must earn a grade of B or better to continue through the clinical sequence. This course may be repeated a second time in order to meet clinical competencies.

CDD 5229.description: Major theories of normal and disordered speech sound development; individualized assessment and intervention techniques for children and adults.

CDD 5231.description: The nature, etiology and symptoms of aphasia and other neurologically based disorders that affect speech and language; discussion of assessment and intervention approaches and techniques.

CDD 5235.description: A review of the processes involved in normal language development. Focus on the nature of language disorders and of children with language disorders. Assessment techniques, both formal and informal, and remediation strategies are discussed.

CDD 5236.description: Roles and responsibilities of the Speech-Language Specialist re: legal procedures guiding identification and delivery of services to the eligible student; service delivery models, assessment and intervention techniques; current education reform initiatives.

CDD 5237.description: Investigates current external issues impacting on the profession; Internal, non-clinical issues encountered when entering the workforce are also addressed.

CDD 5240.description: Major theories and current research on stuttering and other fluency disorders and its impact on individuals and families. Assessment and remediation approaches for children and adults.

CDD 5262.description: Anatomy and physiology of laryngeal mechanism; effects on voice production; clinical management for voice disorders.

CDD 5264.description: Principles and practices of augmentative and alternative communication technologies; assessment and implementation strategies of both unaided and aided systems for individuals with complex communication needs across the lifespan.

CDD 5268.description: Standardized and non-standardized assessment techniques/procedures for evaluating children and adults with a variety of communication disorders; test selection criteria; test administration and interpretation of results; report writing.

CDD 5269.description: The role of the speech-language pathologist in the identification, assessment and remediation of individuals with a variety of feeding and swallowing disorders.

CDD 5270.description: Clinical practicum for students to apply the strategies and procedures acquired in CDD 5268: Assessment Procedures in Speech-Language Pathology.

CDD 5298.description: The Advanced Seminar is designed for graduate students to explore significant issues in Speech-Language Pathology. The courses are taken in the Fall and Spring. During the Fall seminar, students explore a topic and construct a research design in their area of specialization. Thesis required. .

CDD 5299.description: The Advanced Seminar is designed for graduate students to explore significant issues in Speech-Language Pathology. The courses are taken in the Fall and Spring. During the Spring seminar, students prepare a written thesis on completed research.  Thesis required.

CDD 5204.description: This is an on-line course that delineates the roles, responsibilities and techniques used by speech-language-pathologists to increase conversational intelligibility for non-native speakers of English.

CDD 5205.description: Online introductory course with a major focus on the role of the speech-language pathologist in a health care setting. Provides professional training for students to meet University and ASHA certification requirements.

CDD 5226.description: Development of communication-related structures and skills during prenatal, perinatal, neonatal, infant, and toddler periods; development risk factors; pre-linguistic, early linguistic, and feeding/swallowing assessment; treating models, goals, and strategies.

CDD 5233.description: Assessment and Management of cognitive-linguistic difficulties subsequent to traumatic brain injury.

CDD 5238.description: The nature, diagnosis, and treatment of neuromotor disorders of speech in children and adults.

CDD 5242.description: Comprehensive overview for speech-language clinicians of assessment and intervention issues for students who are deaf or hard of hearing.  Special emphasis on the needs of students with hearing loss in public school programs with regard to speech, language, and audition.

CDD 5243.description: Introduction to basic theories of counseling and the role of speech-language pathologists in counseling individuals having communication disorders and their families.

CDD 5250.description: Designed to address an emergent issue or topic that arises in more depth than is possible within an existing course.

CDD 5252.description: Focus on the interrelationship between language and literacy and how reading and writing deficits are often rooted in basic areas of language. The SLPs role in literacy for individuals with speech-language impairments will be discussed.

CDD 5253.description: An advanced course providing students opportunities to administer and interpret clinical bedside evaluations to assess swallow function, and to develop and write interdisciplinary evaluation, treatment and management plans.

CDD 5254.description: Designed to learn and to practice best known strategies in conducting fair language assessments of children from diverse linguistic backgrounds, enabling student clinicians to plan appropriate assessment plans.

CDD 5255.description: Expand the student's knowledge of the differences in social communication, pragmatics, and social skills for individuals with autism spectrum disorder, social (pragmatic) communication disorder and related disorders.

CDD 5261.description: Overview of laryngeal cancer; effects on communication and lifestyle; clinical management for voice and speech restoration; alternative communication options.

CDD 5263.description: This course presents a critical review of the behavioral and neuro-physiological aspects of normal and dysfunctional auditory processing. Auditory-linguistic and cognitive factors contributing to auditory processing disorders (APD) are discussed. Assessment and intervention strategies for APD are identified and illustrated.

CDD 5265.description: Evaluation and management of infants and children with oral-motor dysfunction, feeding difficulties, and swallowing problems.

CDD 5266.description: Following the Neurodiversity paradigm, the assessment and treatment of language and communication impairments in autistic children and adolescents in the pre-linguistic, emerging, developing language stages, or language for learning stages. Students will describe and implement Neurodiversity-friendly assessment and learn the major evidence based and established treatment approaches.  Students will apply these approaches to Autism in the clinic.

CDD 5273.description: Physiological, psychological and sociological effects of aging on communication; assessment, treatment, rehabilitation and advocacy roles of professionals working in geriatric settings are explored.

metadata.full_name: Speech-Language Pathology, Pre-Professional Program Option
metadata.coordinator:
EC-104F
Tel: 908-737-5809
E-mail: rainas@kean.edu

details.description: The Speech-Language Pathology Pre-Professional Program is housed in the School of Communication Disorders and Deafness. The purpose of the program is to provide foundational preparation for students who have an academic and/or professional background in a field other than speech-language-hearing sciences and disorders who intend to apply for entrance into a graduate program in speech-language pathology or audiology. This is a non-degree program that does not meet the requirements for SLP-As.

CDD 2254.description: Study of the sound patterns of spoken language; phonetic transcription; acoustic aspects; theories of phonological development, phonological processes; application to disorders of articulation and dialectical differences.

CDD 2256.description: Developmental processes and behavioral sequences related to speech and language acquisition in children from birth to 5 are studied. Biological, cognitive, motor, psychosocial, environmental, and cultural factors in interactions are examined as determinants of language and use.

CDD 2260.description: An introductory study of the basic structures and functions of the speech and auditory mechanisms.

CDD 3252.description: Study of the nature, etiology, diagnosis, and remediation techniques associated with the major disorders of speech, language, and communication disorders across the lifespan. Case studies and simulated observations with a variety of clinical populations.

CDD 3259.description: An introduction to the field of audiology, anatomy and physiology of the auditory system, hearing, hearing disorders, theory and practice of basic audiometric assessment, and diagnosis and management of hearing loss and other auditory disorders.  Emphasis will be made on understanding the importance of the auditory system in speech-language pathology.

CDD 3271.description: An introduction to the biological production and reception of human speech.

CDD 3269.description: Emphasizes the human nervous system, including neuroanatomy/-physiology, neural processing, and neural feedback loops of the central and peripheral nervous systems. Covers neuroscience, especially as related to speech, language, and hearing.

metadata.full_name: Student Assistance Coordinator, Certification Program
metadata.coordinator: The Student Assistance Coordinator certification program has been approved by the NJ Department of Education as a 21-27 credit program leading to New Jersey State Certification as a Student Assistance Coordinator in a public school. Holders of school psychologist, school social worker, school counselor, director of school counseling services or school nurse are exempt from the practicum requirement.  Applicants from other universities should meet with an advisor to match their coursework to determine the number of credits required and whether their qualifications, such other certifications, are eligible for this certification.

CED 5910.description: Introduction to Philosophy, history and development of counseling and human services, theories and methods of counseling, and current research. Focus on self-exploration and self-awareness, and a foundation for all future counseling courses.  Previous title: Introduction to Counseling and Human Service.

CED 5962.description: Major emphasis on achieving better understanding of the self, the small group, its characteristics and dynamics, and the relationship of group process to effective counseling.

CED 5950.description: Understanding of use of self by the counselor in a helping relationship. Development of basic counseling skills. Synthesis of theories and skills in the counseling process. Practice in counseling through audio and video taping, critique and feedback.

CED 5944.description: The course introduces students to the basic roles, job functions, and activities performed by school counselors, with emphasis on evidence-based outcomes and best practice models.

CED 5501.description: An in-depth survey of various methods,models,and procedures in assessment and treatment of alcohol and drug abuse. Theory and process of alcohol and drug abuse counseling and prevention are included.

CED 5502.description: An overview of the responsibilities of the Substance Awareness Coordinator, including programming prevention, training, counseling, intervision, and referral in schools and communities.

CED 5503.description: The effects of a alcohol, tobacco, and other drugs on child and adult lifespan development. Includes the examination of risk, protective and resiliency factors and counseling interventions, with children at-risk of failure.

CED 5504.description: A skills approach to clinical assessment, treatment plaanning, counseling strategies implementation and techniques for alcohol /drug affected children and families.

CED 5983.description: Experience conducting professional counseling in a school as a student assistance coordinator intern, under the supervision of a cooperating, experienced, professional certified student assistance counselor [FA/SP/SU I].

metadata.full_name: Teacher of Reading, Certification (Currently not accepting new students)

details.description: This program fulfills requirements for New Jersey certification as teacher of reading and focuses on the skills of prevention, remediation, and reading development. The Praxis Examination is required for certification.

CS 5480.description: Models and theories of reading acquisition and the reading process.  Implications of the models and theories are presented through the study of exemplary classroom practices.

CS 5410.description: Focuses on the implications of the contemporary theoretical concepts of the reading process for classroom instruction.  Emphasis will be placed on instructional methods and materials for the teaching of reading and the related language arts in the elementary grades.

CS 5411.description: The reading curriculum in grades 7 through 12. A teaching model, fusing content and process, will be developed as the basis for skill development, reading improvement and construction of a teaching guide.  Current issues affecting reading instruction in the secondary school will be examined.

CS 5412.description: A consideration of the neurological, physical, mental A consideration of the neurological, physical, mental and emotional aspects of the child which may impede learning.  Attention is focused on understanding and interpreting standardized tests and testing instruments, their validity and reliability, and on classroom diagnosis and remediation of specific reading disabilities.

CS 5470.description: An investigation of the nature and purposes of various methods and materials used in the teaching of reading in order to ascertain their appropriateness and effectiveness in teaching young children to read.  Considerations will be given to current issues related to children with diverse backgrounds.

CS 5481.description: A study of a diagnostic-prescriptive teaching in basic skills for children and adults.  Emphasis will be on the development and implementation of presciptive strategies applicable to the individual student's needs.

CS 5493.description: A minimum of 75 hours of experience in develop- mental, corrective and remedial instruction in a school setting under the supervision of cooperating specialist and a college supervisor.  Hours are arranged cooperatively with the student.

CS 5600.description: This course is designed for graduate students who wish to increase their effectiveness in the teaching of basic skills to children requiring remedial assistance.  Emphasis will be placed on causal diagnosis, followed by remediation for the various skills.

CS 5471.description: Develops understanding of literacy acquisition and strategies to facilitate literacy growth. Emphasis on current issues in literacy education.

EC 5100.description: In-depth study of language development and reading as a cognitive and effective process. Current research and strategies for developing language arts/reading programs for young children.

EMSE 5310.description: A survey of literature for children and adolescents and its impact on learning. a cross-section of genre, thematic unit development and literature circles across cultures and curricula.  Formerly:  IEL 5310.

metadata.full_name: Teacher of Students with Disabilities, Certification
metadata.coordinator: Dr. Joseph A. Hogan, Ed.D., Program Coordinator

details.description: Track A: Candidates seeking special education certification who are general education certification candidates under the Alternate Route Program.

SPED 5005.description: This course examines the etiologies and characteristics of learning and behavioral disabilities as defined by IDEIA (2004). Implications for classroom practice, including the referral process for special education, and effective inclusion, are discussed in relation to historical and legal foundations. Current issues and trends are discussed.

SPED 5020.description: Theories and techniques that pertain to special education are applied to specific instructional, social, and emotional domains. Evidence-based practices for students with Autism Spectrum Disorder are covered. Current legislation, special education processes, and trends are reviewed.

SPED 5050.description: Formal and informal assessment tools will be examined in cognition, achievement, interests, and social/emotional functioning. Statistical bases of educational assessment will be analyzed, including limitations and misuses of educational assessment for students who are racially, culturally, and linguistically diverse.

SPED 5023.description: Through hands-on learning, students will learn about various software applications and website accessibility options, and become familiar with adaptive technologies. Students will learn to integrate technology into their curriculum while meeting the needs of individuals with disabilities.

SPED 5024.description: This course examines the essential elements of inclusive education. Strategies to differentiate instruction in inclusive settings are explored, along with methods to foster acceptance and to address Harassment, Intimidation, and Bullying (HIB).

SPED 5068.description: Legal and historical influences that impact the transition process are covered. Agency and program factors are considered. Skills needed to successfully plan for transition and lifelong supports for students with disabilities are explored.

SPED 5071.description: This course will explore special issues and approaches to teach literacy to students with an emphasis placed upon students with special needs. Theory, research, and practice grounded in the science of reading are explored in the field of literacy.

metadata.full_name: Teaching English as a Second Language, Non-Degree: Alternate Route Option

details.description: The Kean University Alternate Route program for Teaching English as a Second or Other Language enables candidates to be employed ESL teachers while they are completing their training for initial certification in TESOL. In oder to enter the Alternate Route in TESOL, candidates must first obtain a Certificate of Eligibility (CE) from the State. They must then secure a teaching position in a school district. The district is then responsible for providing candidates with the appropriate mentoring and pedagogical training, and for recommending them to the State for certification when they have completed all requirements, including an approved university program.

EDUC 5300.description: This course includes theories of learning, child/adolescent development, curriculum, assessment procedures & instruments, classroom management, diverse cultures and learners, state standards and the creation of positive classroom cultures; edTPA lesson planning and commentaries. Required for Graduate Students.

ENG 5105.description: An introduction to the systematic study of language and its components, phonology, morphology and syntax.

ENG 5130.description: Seminar in the structure of American English.Emphasis on the relationship between the phonological and systems as well as on the differences between spoken and written English.

TESL 5404.description: Examination of historical and cultural backgrounds, cognition, & communicative processes that impact second language learning. Emphasis is placed on emergent bilingual students in various contexts and differentiated instruction aligned with TESOL & WIDA standards.

TESL 5435.description: This course reviews current theories & research in language acquisition within the context of ESL/EFL classrooms, learning language through content, TESOL & WIDA standards and L2 acquisition and assessment.

TESL 5420.description: This course examines the methodologies required to effectively teach all students, with a focus on ELLs in ESL and EFL settings. TESOL & WIDA standards-based instruction, curriculum development, and assessment will be emphasized.

TESL 5810.description: This course requires candidates to complete systematic observation, instruction and assessment of learners in ESL/EFL settings under the guidance of a highly qualified professional. Candidates will complete learning segments that incorporate TESOL & WIDA standards for the edTPA assessment portfolio. For students in Graduate TESL programs.

TESL 5500.description: An overview of L2 learners' literacy development and their transition from basic English proficiency to success in the use of Academic English across the major content areas, e.g., LA, Math, Science, Social Studies, & Instructional Technology.

metadata.full_name: Teaching the Holocaust and Prejudice Reduction, Post Baccalaureate Certificate

details.description: This 4 course, 12 credit certificate is designed for teachers and other educators in K-12 schools. It directly addresses the mandate of the New Jersey State Legislature in 1994 to teach about Holocaust, genocide, and prejudice in New Jersey public schools. The prerequisites are taught both at Kean and at local school districts and are also electives in the Kean University Master of Arts in Holocaust and Genocide Studies program.

EMSE 5342.description: This course is designed to prepare K-12 teachers in New Jersey to teach the Holocaust in accordance with the mandate of the State Legislature in 1994.  Prerequisite(s): New Jersey teacher certification.

EMSE 5343.description: Students will develop strategies for teaching prejudice reduction by examining topics such as race, ethnicity, discrimination, bullying, and preconception.  University instructors will emphasize approaches that will reduce the role of prejudice in their student's lives.

EMSE 5348.description: This seminar is designed to bring together graduate students who have previously taken EMSE 5342 and wish to pursue an in-depth examination of critical issues in Holocaust education. In addition to this examination, the course is primarily a research seminar, and participants will be engaged in independent study for the purpose of designing and implementing courses of study for their students and their districts. Students will also have the opportunity to examine scholarly works on related Holocaust issues. The seminar meetings will provide an opportunity for educators from diverse districts to network and share strategies and curricular materials that have been successfully implemented in their districts.

EMSE 5349.description: This seminar is designed to bring together graduate students who have background in teaching prejudice reduction for the purpose of designing and implementing courses of study for their students and school districts.
